title: $Q$ Factors Exceeding $10^{4}$ in Wavelength-to-Subwavelength-Scale Free-Space Resonators
abstract: Free-space-addressable optical resonators that combine long photon lifetimes (high $Q$ factors) with strong spatial localization of optical fields (small mode volumes, $V_m$) enhance light-matter interactions with facile far-field excitation. The Purcell factor governing spontaneous emission enhancement scales as $Q\,V_m^{-1}$. Periodically asymmetric resonators, in which perturbations convert bound modes into radiating modes, offer a route to free-space resonances, with the radiative $Q$ factor tuned by the geometric and optical strength of the asymmetry-inducing perturbations. However, free-space resonators that simultaneously achieve high $Q$ and small $V_m$ have remained rare. This limitation arises in part because existing designs do not tailor geometric and optical asymmetries concurrently, thus limiting access to high-$Q$ regimes. Here, we show that jointly tuning geometric and optical asymmetries unlocks a biaxial radiative landscape with iso-$Q$ contours that connect disparate perturbations with equivalent $Q$ factors. We demonstrate this framework with very-large-scale-integrated single-crystalline Si nanoantenna pixels (VINPix) with out-of-plane perturbations of 35-150 nm amorphous Si, SiN$_x$, and SiO$_2$. We experimentally establish biaxial $Q$ factor control in air and achieve $Q$ factors up to $76,000$ at wavelength-scale mode volumes ($V_m \sim 1.7\,λ_0^3\,n_{\mathrm{eff}}^{-3}$) in simultaneously imaged arrays of $>80$ resonators in water. Furthermore, we computationally demonstrate 50-nm-wide slotted VINPix that reach $Q$ factors of $10^6$ at subwavelength mode volumes ($V_m \sim 0.2\,λ_0^3\,n_{\mathrm{eff}}^{-3}$) with 20 nm SiO$_2$ perturbations, yielding Purcell factors as high as $5 \times 10^5$ in an all-dielectric free-space resonator.
one_liner: Free-space silicon nanoresonators reach Q up to 76,000 experimentally and 10^6 in slotted designs while keeping wavelength-to-subwavelength mode volumes.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical route to high-Purcell, free-space-coupled dielectric resonators that could strengthen emitters, nonlinear optics, and optical interfaces without complex coupling hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a biaxial design framework for free-space resonators by jointly tuning geometric and optical asymmetry in periodically asymmetric single-crystal Si nanoantenna pixels. Experiments on VLSI-fabricated arrays with amorphous Si, SiNx, and SiO2 perturbations show controlled radiative Q tuning and Q values up to 76,000 in water with mode volumes around 1.7 lambda^3 scaled by effective index. Simulations of 50 nm slotted structures predict Q near 10^6 at Vm about 0.2 lambda^3 with 20 nm SiO2 perturbations, corresponding to Purcell factors up to 5 x 10^5.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate free-space-addressable dielectric resonators with Q up to 76,000 and computationally identify slotted variants reaching Q of 10^6 at subwavelength mode volume.
application_angle: These resonators could support efficient free-space quantum emitter interfaces, compact photonic sensing, and enhanced light-matter coupling in scalable chip-compatible optical systems.

title: Quantum Clock Synchronization Networks: A Survey
abstract: Quantum clock synchronization (QCS) aims to establish a shared temporal reference between distant nodes by exploiting uniquely quantum phenomena such as entanglement, single-photon interference, and quantum correlations. In contrast to classical synchronization and time-transfer techniques, which are limited by signal propagation delays, atmospheric disturbances, and oscillator drift, QCS protocols offer the potential to surpass classical precision bounds and enhance resilience against adversarial manipulations. As precise and secure time synchronization underpins distributed quantum networks, navigation systems, and emerging quantum Internet infrastructures, understanding QCS principles, capabilities, and implementation challenges has become increasingly important. This survey provides a unified and critical overview of the rapidly growing QCS research landscape, highlighting fundamentals, protocol types, enabling resources, performance constraints, security considerations, and practical implementations of QCS. We first introduce the theoretical underpinnings of QCS, including entanglement-assisted time transfer, Hong-Ou-Mandel interference-based synchronization, and quantum slow-clock transport. We then categorize the main QCS protocols, ranging from ticking-qubit and entanglement-based schemes to time-of-arrival correlation methods, conveyor-belt synchronization, and quantum-enhanced two-way time transfer. This organization clarifies the relationships between protocol families and their achievable precision advantages over classical methods. Key quantum resources such as spontaneous parametric down-conversion-based entangled photon pairs, Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger and W multipartite states, squeezed and frequency-entangled light, quantum frequency combs, and quantum memories are reviewed in the context of scalability and robustness.
one_liner: A survey maps the protocol, resource, performance, and security landscape of quantum clock synchronization for distributed quantum networks.
why_it_matters: Clock synchronization is a core systems problem for quantum networks, and this survey clarifies which photonic resources and protocol families are most viable for scalable secure timing links.
detailed_summary: This survey organizes quantum clock synchronization into protocol classes including ticking-qubit, entanglement-assisted, time-of-arrival correlation, slow-clock transport, conveyor-belt, and quantum-enhanced two-way time transfer schemes. It reviews the operating principles behind entanglement-assisted timing, single-photon and HOM-interference methods, and analyzes constraints from loss, drift, atmospheric effects, security, and scalability. The paper also compares enabling resources such as SPDC photon pairs, GHZ and W states, squeezed and frequency-entangled light, frequency combs, and quantum memories in the context of network deployment.
main_result: The work provides a unified technical taxonomy of QCS protocols, resources, performance limits, and security considerations for distributed quantum timing networks.
application_angle: It is directly useful for designing robust timing layers in quantum internet, navigation, and secure networked sensing architectures.

title: Digital-Analog Quantum Simulation and Computing: A Perspective on Past and Future Developments
abstract: Quantum simulation and computing traditionally has been based on two main paradigms, namely, digital and analog. In the digital paradigm, usually single and two-qubit gates (where qubit is an acronym for quantum bit) are employed as building blocks for scalable, universal quantum computing, although errors add up fast and error correction will be ultimately needed for scaling up. In the analog paradigm, large analog blocks are normally employed for a unitary dynamics that carries out the computation, enabling quantum operations on many qubits with reduced errors, but with the drawback of a limited choice of evolutions and lack of universality. In the past decade, a new paradigm has emerged, showing interesting possibilities for quantum simulation and computing in the near and mid term. This is the paradigm of digital-analog quantum technologies, which proposes to combine the best of both paradigms: large analog blocks, provided by native interactions of the employed quantum platform, enabling scalability, combined with digital gates, allowing for more versatility and, ultimately, universality. In this Perspective, I give an overview of the evolution of the field along the past decade, and an outlook for its future possibilities.
one_liner: This perspective argues digital-analog methods can combine native many-body interactions with gate control to improve near-term quantum simulation and computing.
why_it_matters: Hybrid digital-analog control is one of the clearest routes to better resource efficiency on imperfect hardware, especially where native interactions can replace long gate sequences.
detailed_summary: The article reviews the evolution of digital-analog quantum technologies as a middle ground between gate-based universality and low-error analog evolution. Its core technical point is that native many-qubit interaction blocks can implement substantial fractions of a target unitary, while digital layers add programmability and can in principle recover universality. The perspective frames this as a practical strategy for reducing circuit depth and accumulated error on near- and mid-term platforms, with implications for both simulation and computation.
main_result: The paper synthesizes past progress and future opportunities for combining analog interaction blocks with digital gates to improve scalability and versatility.
application_angle: Its relevance is in guiding hardware-software co-design for useful quantum processors where fully digital fault tolerance remains out of reach.

title: Efficient direct quantum state tomography using fan-out couplings
abstract: Characterizing quantum states is essential for validating quantum devices, yet conventional quantum state tomography becomes prohibitively expensive as system size grows. Direct tomography offers a distinct route by enabling selective access to individual complex density-matrix elements, with a particular advantage for sparse target states and some verification tasks. Here we introduce a direct quantum state tomography scheme combining strong-measurement estimation with a fan-out coupling architecture. It enables mutually commuting interactions between system qubits and a single meter qubit, thereby achieving constant circuit depth, independent of system size. Notably, the involutory fan-out coupling reduces to the identity under repetition, enabling straightforward noise scaling for quantum error mitigation. We experimentally validate the scheme on a superconducting quantum processor via the IBM Quantum Platform, demonstrating four-qubit state reconstruction and single-circuit GHZ-state fidelity estimation up to 20 qubits with error mitigation. Consistent results with standard tomography and improved efficiency establish our scheme as a promising approach to reconstructing full quantum states and scalable verification tasks.
one_liner: Fan-out couplings enable constant-depth direct tomography and GHZ fidelity estimation up to 20 qubits on superconducting hardware.
why_it_matters: Efficient state verification is a major bottleneck in scaling quantum processors, and this method cuts circuit depth while supporting mitigation-friendly noise scaling.
detailed_summary: The scheme uses mutually commuting fan-out interactions between many system qubits and a single meter qubit, allowing direct access to selected density-matrix elements with circuit depth independent of system size. Because the involutory coupling becomes identity under repetition, the protocol supports straightforward noise scaling for quantum error mitigation. On IBM superconducting hardware, the authors reconstruct four-qubit states and estimate GHZ-state fidelity in a single circuit for systems up to 20 qubits, matching standard tomography while using fewer resources for targeted tasks.
platform_or_system: superconducting qubits with a single meter qubit
main_result: The authors experimentally validate constant-depth direct state tomography and scalable GHZ fidelity estimation on a superconducting quantum processor.
application_angle: The approach is practical for benchmarking, verification, and sparse-state characterization in larger quantum processors where full tomography is too expensive.

title: A Demon that remembers: An agential approach towards quantum thermodynamics of temporal correlations
abstract: This thesis develops a decision-theoretic framework for extracting thermodynamic work from temporal correlations in quantum systems. We model a classical agent -- lacking quantum memory -- performing adaptive work extraction through continuous inference and decision-making under uncertainty. By introducing $ρ^*$-ideal protocols, we demonstrate that exploiting memory effects allows adaptive strategies to surpass non-adaptive bounds. We formalize this via the Time-Ordered Free Energy (TOFE), a novel upper bound for causal, adaptive operations that reveals a thermodynamic gap linked to adaptive ordered discord. Additionally, we tackle work extraction from unknown sources using reinforcement learning. By adapting multi-armed bandit algorithms, we show an agent can simultaneously learn an unknown i.i.d. quantum state and extract work, achieving polylogarithmic cumulative dissipation that significantly outperforms standard tomography. Overall, this work lays the foundation for predictive and learning-based quantum thermodynamics.
one_liner: A decision-theoretic framework shows adaptive agents can extract more work from quantum temporal correlations and learn unknown sources efficiently.
why_it_matters: It links temporal quantum correlations to operational thermodynamic advantage, which matters for autonomous quantum devices that must infer, adapt, and act under uncertainty.
detailed_summary: The thesis studies a classical agent without quantum memory that performs adaptive work extraction by continuously inferring temporal structure in a quantum process. It introduces rho-star ideal protocols and the Time-Ordered Free Energy, an upper bound for causal adaptive operations that exposes a gap between adaptive and non-adaptive strategies tied to ordered discord. It also casts work extraction from unknown i.i.d. quantum sources as a reinforcement-learning problem and shows bandit-style algorithms can learn the source while keeping cumulative dissipation polylogarithmic.
main_result: The work establishes formal adaptive advantages for extracting work from temporal quantum correlations and proposes learning-based protocols for unknown quantum sources.
application_angle: These ideas could inform autonomous quantum control and energy-aware operation in networked or continuously monitored quantum devices.

title: Optimal, Qubit-Efficient Quantum Vehicle Routing via Colored-Permutations
abstract: We formulate a global-position colored-permutation encoding for the capacitated vehicle routing problem. Each of the $K$ vehicles selects a disjoint partial permutation, and the sum of these $K$ color layers forms a full $n\times n$ permutation matrix that assigns every customer to exactly one visit position. This representation uses $n^2K$ binary decision variables arranged as $K$ color layers over a common permutation structure, while vehicle capacities are enforced by weighted sums over the entries of each color class, requiring no explicit load register and hence no extra logical qubits beyond the routing variables. In contrast, many prior quantum encodings introduce an explicit capacity or load representation with additional qubits. Our construction is designed to exploit the Constraint-Enhanced QAOA framework together with its encoded-manifold analyses. Building on a requirements-based view of quantum utility in CVRP, we develop a routing optimization formulation that directly targets one of the main near-term bottlenecks, namely the additional logical-qubit cost of vehicle labels and explicit capacity constraints. Our proposal shows strong algorithmic performance in addition to qubit efficiency. On a standard benchmark suite, our end-to-end pipeline recovers the independently verified optima. The feasibility oracle may also be of independent interest as a reusable polynomial-time decoding and certification primitive for quantum and quantum-inspired routing pipelines.
one_liner: A colored-permutation encoding solves capacitated vehicle routing with fewer logical qubits by avoiding explicit load registers.
why_it_matters: Logical-qubit pressure is a hard limit for near-term optimization on quantum hardware, and this encoding removes a major overhead while retaining strong solution quality.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a global-position colored-permutation encoding in which K vehicles occupy K color layers over a common n x n permutation structure, using n^2K binary variables. Capacity constraints are enforced through weighted sums over each color class instead of separate load registers, reducing logical-qubit requirements relative to many prior formulations. Designed for Constraint-Enhanced QAOA, the approach is paired with a polynomial-time feasibility oracle and is reported to recover independently verified optima on standard CVRP benchmarks.
platform_or_system: qubit-efficient QAOA for capacitated vehicle routing
main_result: The authors present a qubit-efficient CVRP encoding and optimization pipeline that reaches benchmark optima without explicit capacity-register overhead.
application_angle: This improves the practicality of running industrial routing problems on limited quantum hardware by trading extra constraint structure for lower qubit cost.

title: Phase-Stable Hologram Updates for Large-Scale Neutral-Atom Array Reconfiguration
abstract: Assembling large-scale, defect-free Rydberg atom arrays is a key technology for neutral-atom quantum computation. Dynamic holographic optical tweezers enable the assembly and reconfiguration of such arrays, but phase mismatches between successive holograms can induce destructive interference and transient trap loss during spatial-light-modulator refresh. In this work, we introduce the weighted-projective Gerchberg--Saxton (WPGS) algorithm, a phase-stable approach to dynamic hologram updates for large-scale Rydberg atom-array reconfiguration. By enforcing inter-frame trap-phase continuity while retaining weighted intensity equalization, WPGS suppresses refresh-induced transient degradation. The phase-difference distribution between consecutive holograms further provides a simple diagnostic of transient robustness. Moreover, enforcing the phase constraint reduces the number of iterations required at each update step, thereby accelerating hologram generation. Numerical simulations of 2D and 3D reconfiguration with more than $10^3$ traps, including multilayer assembly and interlayer transport, show robust transient intensities and significantly faster updates than conventional methods. These results establish inter-frame phase continuity as a practical design principle for dynamic holographic control and scalable neutral-atom array reconfiguration.
one_liner: A phase-constrained hologram algorithm enables faster, more robust reconfiguration of neutral-atom tweezer arrays beyond 1,000 traps.
why_it_matters: Transient trap loss during SLM refresh is a real bottleneck for scalable neutral-atom processors, and this directly targets that hardware control failure mode.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces the weighted-projective Gerchberg-Saxton algorithm, which enforces inter-frame phase continuity between successive holograms while preserving weighted trap-intensity equalization. In simulations of 2D and 3D neutral-atom array updates with more than 10^3 traps, the method suppresses destructive interference and transient intensity degradation during SLM refresh. The authors also show that the phase-difference distribution between frames serves as a practical robustness diagnostic. Imposing the phase constraint reduces iteration count per update, improving hologram generation speed for multilayer assembly and interlayer transport.
main_result: The method achieves numerically robust, phase-stable reconfiguration of large 2D and 3D atom arrays with faster hologram updates than conventional approaches.
application_angle: It improves the real-time assembly and rearrangement layer needed for scalable neutral-atom quantum computing and high-fidelity atom transport.

title: Interaction-free measurement of multiple objects using a universal integrated photonic processor
abstract: The phenomenon of interaction-free measurement (IFM) enables the probabilistic detection of an absorbing object with reduced photon absorption. We report the experimental implementation of a simultaneous IFM of multiple objects using a single quantum probe on the cloud-based Ascella photonic processor of company Quandela. We demonstrate sequential IFM of up to 5 objects using a single photon, significantly extending the original IFM scheme for a single object. The experimental error-mitigated results confirm the theoretical predictions for this sequential IFM setup, and demonstrate a practical approach to scaling IFM to more complex quantum interrogation tasks.
one_liner: A cloud photonic processor performs sequential interaction-free measurement of up to five objects using a single photon.
why_it_matters: It shows that a nontrivial quantum interrogation protocol can be scaled on programmable integrated photonic hardware rather than only in bespoke tabletop setups.
detailed_summary: Using Quandela's Ascella universal integrated photonic processor, the authors experimentally implement sequential interaction-free measurement with a single quantum probe. The setup detects up to five absorbing objects in sequence while limiting photon absorption, extending the original single-object Elitzur-Vaidman style protocol. Error-mitigated experimental data agree with theoretical predictions for the multi-object scheme. The work demonstrates that complex quantum interrogation tasks can be mapped onto reconfigurable photonic circuits available through the cloud.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates sequential interaction-free measurement of as many as five objects with a single photon on a programmable photonic chip.
application_angle: Programmable low-absorption probing could be useful for fragile-sample sensing, quantum measurement primitives, and deployable photonic quantum protocols.

title: Physical currents for stochastic Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen quantum trajectories
abstract: Theories of the measured homodyne current generated by a stochastic Schrödinger equation (SSE) can be tested in a simulation of the Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) correlations for a two-mode squeezed state. We carry out such a simulation, and determine the correct stochastic term for the measured current in the broad-band limit. Stratonovich rather than Ito stochastic noise agrees with experiment. We show that this is relevant to measurement noise and errors in quantum technologies. By analyzing the SSE trajectories as measurement settings are changed, we propose a modern version of Schrodinger's gedanken experiment, where one measures position and momenta simultaneously, ``one by direct, the other by indirect measurement''.
one_liner: EPR homodyne-trajectory simulations identify Stratonovich noise as the physically correct stochastic term for measured current.
why_it_matters: Getting the measurement-current model right matters for quantum control, estimation, and error analysis in continuously monitored photonic systems.
detailed_summary: The authors test theories of measured homodyne current by simulating Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen correlations for a two-mode squeezed state under a stochastic Schrodinger equation. They find that, in the broadband limit, the physically correct stochastic contribution to the measured current is described by Stratonovich rather than Ito noise. The paper analyzes how inferred trajectories change as measurement settings vary and connects this to simultaneous direct and indirect measurements of conjugate observables. The result is relevant to interpreting measurement noise and error sources in continuously monitored quantum-optical technologies.
main_result: The study shows that Stratonovich-form stochastic noise reproduces the physical measured homodyne current in EPR trajectory simulations.
application_angle: This refines the modeling used in quantum sensing, feedback, and metrology systems that rely on continuous optical readout.

title: What quantum computer to buy?
abstract: The phrase ``buy a quantum computer'' hides several different procurement problems. An institution may be seeking cloud access for teaching, reserved capacity for research, a local instrument for hardware training, an optimization appliance, or a strategic installation that reshapes facilities, staffing, and budgets. Because these choices differ in purpose, operating burden, and useful lifetime, the decision should be framed as acquisition of \emph{quantum capability} rather than selection of a presumed hardware winner. This manuscript develops a practical procurement framework that distinguishes five capability layers, separates peer-reviewed results from commercial offerings, pricing anchors, and public roadmaps, and compares the main commercial platform families -- superconducting circuits, trapped ions, neutral atoms, quantum annealing, and photonics -- through the lens of institutional fit, access model, and refresh pressure. The main conclusion is that most institutions should begin with the smallest layer of capability that produces repeatable near-term value, builds internal expertise, and preserves strategic flexibility. Large on-premises systems are justified only when mission requirements, site readiness, staffing, governance, and upgrade paths are already clear.
one_liner: A procurement framework compares quantum platform families and argues most institutions should buy capability in stages, not chase a hardware winner.
why_it_matters: Real quantum adoption depends on matching platform, access model, staffing, and refresh cycle to use case, and this paper formalizes that decision process.
detailed_summary: Rather than benchmarking a single best machine, the paper defines five layers of quantum capability spanning teaching access, research capacity, local training hardware, optimization appliances, and major strategic installations. It compares superconducting, trapped-ion, neutral-atom, annealing, and photonic offerings using peer-reviewed results, commercial products, pricing anchors, and public roadmaps. The analysis emphasizes operating burden, refresh pressure, and institutional fit over headline qubit counts. Its main recommendation is to start with the smallest capability tier that yields repeatable near-term value while preserving upgrade flexibility.
main_result: The paper delivers a structured framework for selecting quantum computing capability based on use case, lifecycle, and operational constraints across major hardware modalities.
application_angle: It is directly useful for labs, universities, and enterprises making near-term purchasing or access decisions around quantum hardware.

title: Coexistence of CHSH Nonlocality and KCBS Contextuality in a Single Quantum State
abstract: Contextuality and nonlocality are distinct manifestations at the foundation of quantum mechanics, yet their coexistence within a single quantum state remains subtle. In a hybrid CHSH--KCBS scenario involving the entanglment of a qubit and a qutrit, the qutrit supports the KCBS contextuality test, and the CHSH nonlocality arises from correlations between the qubit and qutrit. Here, we derive the analytical closed-form expressions for both inequalities and also simulate this physics on a quantum circuit. We show that contextuality is governed solely by a population parameter $p_2$, associated with the occupation of the qutrit subsystem in the $|2\rangle$ level, which plays a distinguished role in the KCBS structure. In contrast, nonlocality depends irreducibly on coherence, involving both amplitudes and phases encoded in parameters $(X_i, Y_i)$. This separation of physical resources reveals parameter regimes that optimize KCBS violation while suppress CHSH violation, and vice versa. As a result, the optimal regions do not overlap, and coexistence is restricted to a narrow intermediate regime in parameter space.
one_liner: A qubit-qutrit model maps where CHSH nonlocality and KCBS contextuality can coexist, showing only a narrow overlap regime.
why_it_matters: It clarifies which physical resources drive different nonclassical signatures, informing how hybrid photonic or circuit experiments should be designed and interpreted.
detailed_summary: The work derives closed-form analytical expressions for CHSH and KCBS violations in a hybrid scenario where a qubit is entangled with a qutrit, with the qutrit hosting the KCBS test. The authors show that KCBS contextuality depends only on the qutrit population parameter p2, while CHSH nonlocality depends irreducibly on coherences through amplitude and phase parameters. Quantum-circuit simulations confirm that the parameter regions maximizing each effect do not coincide. Coexistence is therefore confined to a narrow intermediate region of state space.
main_result: The paper identifies distinct resource dependencies for contextuality and nonlocality and shows their simultaneous violation is limited to a small shared parameter regime.
application_angle: This can guide experimental state engineering and certification strategies in hybrid photonic and other multilevel quantum information platforms.

title: Toward Quantum Simulation of SU(2) Gauge Theory using Non-Compact Variables
abstract: Simulating lattice gauge theories on quantum computers presents unique challenges that drive the development of novel theoretical frameworks. The orbifold lattice approach offers a scalable method for simulating SU($N$) gauge theories in arbitrary dimensions. In this work, we present three improvements: (i) two new simplified Hamiltonians, (ii) an encoding of the SU(2) theory with smaller number of qubits, and (iii) a reduction in the requirement for large scalar masses to reach the Kogut-Susskind limit, achieved via the inclusion of an additional term in the Hamiltonian. These advancements significantly reduce circuit depth and qubit requirements for quantum simulations. We benchmarked these improvements using Monte Carlo simulations of SU(2) in (2+1) dimensions. Preliminary results demonstrate the effectiveness of these developments and further validate the use of noncompact variables as a promising framework for scalable quantum simulations of gauge theories.
one_liner: Improved noncompact-variable encodings cut qubit count and circuit depth for SU(2) lattice gauge theory simulations.
why_it_matters: Resource reduction is essential if gauge-theory quantum simulation is to become practical on near-term or early fault-tolerant hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper advances the orbifold lattice approach for quantum simulation of SU(2) gauge theory by introducing two simplified Hamiltonians, a more compact qubit encoding, and an extra Hamiltonian term that eases the large-scalar-mass requirement for reaching the Kogut-Susskind limit. These changes are designed to reduce both qubit overhead and circuit depth relative to prior formulations. The authors benchmark the approach with Monte Carlo simulations in 2+1 dimensions. The results support noncompact variables as a scalable route for digital quantum simulation of gauge theories.
main_result: The proposed formulation lowers resource requirements for SU(2) gauge-theory simulation while preserving access to the target lattice-physics limit.
application_angle: Lower-depth, lower-qubit encodings make scientifically relevant gauge simulations more feasible on emerging quantum processors.

title: Modeling the non-Markovian Brownian motion of an optomechanical resonator
abstract: We propose a globally-admissible phenomenological spectral density of the bath for the non-Markovian Brownian motion of an optomechanical resonator, motivated by the near-resonance experimental observation of a non-Ohmic spectrum in [Nat. Commun. 6, 7606 (2015)]. To avoid divergences arising from a naive global extrapolation, we construct this phenomenological bath spectral density that reproduces the observed local-power-law behavior near the mechanical resonance while remaining well defined globally, ensuring the finiteness of the bath-induced renormalizations and quadrature fluctuations of the resonator. The corresponding model of the structured environment produces a nonlocal mechanical susceptibility whose analytic pole structure encodes the observed linewidth. The resulting dissipation kernel exhibits a power-law-modulated exponential decay with transient negativity, signaling strong memory effects. In the weak-coupling regime, the optical readout based on homodyne detection enables near-resonance spectroscopy and, with a calibrated drive on the resonator, permits, in principle, the reconstruction of the full mechanical susceptibility, thereby providing access to both the dissipative and dispersive bath contributions. Our results provide a consistent route from locally-inferred spectral properties to globally-admissible open-system descriptions and establish a framework for probing structured environments in cavity optomechanics.
one_liner: A structured-bath model links near-resonance non-Ohmic noise measurements to a globally consistent non-Markovian optomechanical dynamics.
why_it_matters: It gives optomechanics experiments a physically consistent way to infer and reconstruct environmental memory effects from optical readout data.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs a globally admissible bath spectral density for an optomechanical resonator that matches the locally observed non-Ohmic power law near the mechanical resonance while avoiding ultraviolet divergences. This yields finite bath-induced frequency renormalization and quadrature fluctuations, together with a nonlocal mechanical susceptibility whose pole structure reproduces the measured linewidth. The resulting dissipation kernel shows power-law-modulated exponential decay and transient negativity, clear signatures of strong environmental memory. The authors further argue that homodyne optical readout plus calibrated mechanical driving can reconstruct the full complex susceptibility, including dissipative and dispersive bath contributions.
main_result: A divergence-free non-Markovian bath model is derived that reproduces near-resonance experimental spectra and enables susceptibility reconstruction from optical measurements.
application_angle: The framework supports practical noise spectroscopy and environment characterization in precision optomechanical devices and photonic quantum sensors.

title: Connection between the contextuality breaking and incompatibility breaking qubit channels
abstract: Contextuality and measurement incompatibility are two fundamental aspects of nonclassicality, and their manifestations in observed quantum correlations are often deeply interconnected. Recently, measurement incompatibility has been studied in connection with nonlocality, particularly in terms of their robustness under various quantum channels. This line of investigation helps establish a connection between the channels that break nonlocality and those that break incompatibility. In this study, we focus on an asymmetric bipartite Bell scenario involving three and four inputs on Alice and Bob sides, respectively, with each of these inputs having dichotomous outcomes. Under the assumption of locality, the observed statistics in this asymmetric scenario obeys the Elegant Bell inequality (EBI). Here, we use a different version of the EBI that relies on the assumption of the preparation noncontextuality. By taking the violation of this noncontextual version of EBI as a witness of preparation contextuality we establish a connection between the channels that break contextuality and the channels that break triple-wise measurement incompatibility. Our results suggest that any channel which breaks EBI contextuality will also break Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt (CHSH) nonlocality; however, the reverse does not hold. We also show that a depolarising channel that breaks N-wise incompatibility can also break a certain form of contextuality, witnessed by a generalised inequality involving N measurements on one wing of a bipartite Bell scenario.
one_liner: This work maps when qubit channels destroy contextuality to when they destroy measurement incompatibility in asymmetric Bell scenarios.
why_it_matters: It sharpens which noise processes erase useful nonclassical resources, informing robust photonic and qubit communication or certification protocols.
detailed_summary: The authors study asymmetric bipartite Bell scenarios with three and four dichotomic inputs and use a preparation-noncontextual version of the Elegant Bell inequality as a witness of contextuality. They prove a connection between channels that break this contextuality and channels that break triple-wise measurement incompatibility, and show that any channel breaking EBI contextuality also breaks CHSH nonlocality. The converse fails, so contextuality breaking is a stricter notion than CHSH nonlocality breaking in this setting. They also generalize the analysis to depolarizing channels that break N-wise incompatibility and corresponding contextuality witnesses with N measurements.
platform_or_system: qubit noise channels in Bell-test scenarios
main_result: The paper establishes that EBI-contextuality-breaking channels also break triple-wise incompatibility and CHSH nonlocality, but not vice versa.
application_angle: These bounds help identify tolerable noise levels for device-independent communication, certification, and contextuality-based photonic protocols.

title: Information Propagation in Rydberg Arrays via Analog OTOC Calculations
abstract: Out-of-time-order correlators (OTOCs) are the main tool for probing quantum chaos and scrambling, and have become crucial probes in many areas of quantum computing. However, the measurement of OTOCs is difficult to implement on analog quantum computers due to the requirement of backward time evolution. In this paper, we develop and implement a randomized measurement protocol to compute OTOCs on Aquila by QuEra Computing. Unlike traditional methods that require backward time evolution, our approach utilizes a sequence of global randomized quenches that approximates the unitary 2-design properties necessary for extracting infinite-temperature OTOCs from statistical correlations. We demonstrate the protocol's success by explicitly observing the lightcone of information propagation in 1D Rydberg chains, and compare hardware results to both state-vector simulations and matrix product state (MPS) tensor network calculations. This work establishes the first demonstration of fully analog randomized OTOC measurements in neutral-atom simulators, providing a scalable pathway to probe quantum chaos in complex many-body systems.
one_liner: A randomized analog protocol measures OTOCs on QuEra's Aquila and directly resolves information lightcones in 1D Rydberg chains.
why_it_matters: It removes the need for backward time evolution, making scrambling diagnostics practical on scalable neutral-atom hardware.
detailed_summary: The work implements an analog OTOC measurement protocol on Aquila using sequences of global randomized quenches that approximate unitary 2-design statistics. Unlike conventional OTOC methods, it avoids backward time evolution and instead extracts infinite-temperature OTOCs from measurement correlations. On 1D Rydberg chains, the hardware data explicitly shows a lightcone for operator spreading and is benchmarked against exact state-vector and MPS simulations. This is presented as the first fully analog randomized OTOC measurement in a neutral-atom simulator.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates analog randomized OTOC estimation on Aquila and observes a measurable lightcone of information propagation in Rydberg chains.
application_angle: The method provides a scalable hardware-native diagnostic for chaos, scrambling, and simulator validation in analog quantum processors.

title: Instability-Enhanced Quantum Sensing with Tunable Multibody Interactions
abstract: Dynamical instabilities can amplify small perturbations into measurable signals, offering a route to quantum-enhanced sensing. This mechanism was experimentally demonstrated in a collective-spin system with quadratic interactions, described by a twisting-and-turning Hamiltonian, where quantum evolution near an unstable point leads to exponential growth of spin fluctuations, enabling metrological gain beyond the standard quantum limit. Here, we show that a quartic extension of this Hamiltonian substantially increases the amplification. The additional nonlinear term reshapes the phase-space structure, generating new unstable points and accelerating signal amplification. As a result, enhanced sensitivity is achieved within experimentally accessible coherence times. Remarkably, even at fixed instability rate (equal Lyapunov exponent), multibody interactions outperform the quadratic case due to enhanced short-time dynamics. We analyze the classical and quantum behavior of the multibody model and discuss its experimental implementations. Our results identify phase-space curvature as a resource for optimizing the speed and performance of quantum sensors.
one_liner: Adding quartic multibody terms to an unstable collective-spin sensor boosts short-time amplification and improves quantum metrological gain.
why_it_matters: It points to faster, more sensitive quantum sensors that can outperform quadratic schemes within realistic coherence limits.
detailed_summary: The paper extends the twisting-and-turning collective-spin Hamiltonian with a quartic interaction term and analyzes the resulting unstable phase-space structure. The extra nonlinearity creates new unstable points and increases the speed at which weak perturbations are amplified into measurable spin fluctuations. Even when the Lyapunov exponent is fixed to match the quadratic model, the multibody system yields stronger short-time gain because of altered phase-space curvature. The authors discuss both classical and quantum dynamics and outline experimentally feasible implementations.
main_result: A quartic multibody extension is shown to deliver larger and faster metrological amplification than the standard quadratic instability-based sensing scheme.
application_angle: This design can improve practical sensor bandwidth and sensitivity in platforms that engineer nonlinear collective spin interactions.

title: Unlocking a fast adiabatic CZ gate and exact residual $ZZ$ cancellation between fixed-frequency transmons using a floating tunable coupler
abstract: Tunable couplers in superconducting qubit architectures enable strong qubit-qubit interactions for two-qubit gates while suppressing unwanted coupling during single-qubit operations. However, achieving low error rates for fast two-qubit gates remains challenging, as suppressing leakage and non-adiabatic errors typically requires specialized qubit, coupler, or pulse designs, often at the expense of an idling $ZZ=0$ condition. In this work, we demonstrate that a symmetric floating tunable coupler provides a natural platform for fast, high-fidelity adiabatic controlled-Z (CZ) gates. Its favorable energy-level structure eliminates the conventional trade-off between rapid conditional-phase accumulation and adiabatic evolution while preserving exact cancellation of residual $ZZ$ interaction at idling. This architecture exhibits intrinsic robustness to non-adiabatic transitions, even under simple flux modulation waveforms. To push performance at short gate durations, where maintaining adiabaticity becomes more challenging despite the favorable level structure, we introduce pulse-shaping techniques based on the instantaneous adiabatic factor that further suppress non-adiabatic errors. We experimentally realize a 24 ns adiabatic CZ gate with fidelity exceeding 99.9% and stable operation over several hours.
one_liner: A floating tunable coupler enables a 24 ns adiabatic CZ gate above 99.9% fidelity while exactly cancelling residual idle ZZ.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core superconducting hardware tradeoff by combining fast entangling gates with clean idling in a realistic architecture.
detailed_summary: The authors show that a symmetric floating tunable coupler between fixed-frequency transmons creates an energy-level structure favorable for fast adiabatic CZ gates without sacrificing exact idle-state ZZ cancellation. This removes the usual tension between rapid conditional-phase accumulation and leakage suppression. They further introduce pulse shaping based on the instantaneous adiabatic factor to reduce nonadiabatic errors at very short durations. Experimentally, they realize a 24 ns CZ gate with fidelity exceeding 99.9% and stable operation over hours.
platform_or_system: fixed-frequency transmons with floating tunable coupler
main_result: The work experimentally demonstrates a 24 ns adiabatic CZ gate above 99.9% fidelity together with exact residual ZZ cancellation at idle.
application_angle: The architecture is directly relevant to scalable superconducting processors that need both high-throughput entangling gates and low crosstalk during idling.

title: A superconducting quantum circuit single artificial atom maser
abstract: We demonstrate a circuit QED analog of an atomic micromaser that utilizes an artificial, multi level atom, pumped into a population-inverted state by a microwave tone, as the gain medium. Our demonstration is enabled by the flexibility of the circuit QED platform, which allowed us to precisely engineer the level-structure, coupling, and dissipation of the micromaser components. Our device shows rich physics and perhaps points to ways to use the recent developments in the domain of microwave quantum circuits to probe the domain of maser physics.
one_liner: A circuit-QED artificial atom maser realizes a microwave micromaser analog using a pumped multilevel superconducting circuit as the gain medium.
why_it_matters: It brings maser physics into a controllable superconducting platform that can double as a sensitive microwave quantum device and testbed.
detailed_summary: The device uses a multilevel superconducting artificial atom that is driven into population inversion by a microwave pump and coupled to a resonator to provide gain. Because the circuit-QED platform allows precise engineering of level structure, coupling, and dissipation, it functions as a controllable analog of an atomic micromaser. The experiment demonstrates masing behavior in a single-artificial-atom setting and highlights the richer parameter control available relative to conventional atomic implementations. The setup also opens a route to studying maser dynamics with modern microwave quantum-circuit tools.
main_result: The paper demonstrates a single artificial atom microwave maser implemented in a superconducting circuit with engineered pumping, coupling, and dissipation.
application_angle: Such controllable on-chip masers could become useful microwave sources, probes of quantum-limited amplification, and platforms for sensitive circuit-based measurements.

title: Quantum noise in ranging with optical pulses
abstract: Optical frequency combs combine ultrashort pulse duration and phase stability, making them powerful resources for high-precision ranging even when affected by atmospheric dispersion. It has been established that by classical modal engineering and mdoe-sensitive detection sensitivity to distance at the standard limit can be achieved, however attaining improved uncertainties by the use of squeezing has not been explored. Here, we apply an effective Hamiltonian framework to the problem of ranging with quantum frequency combs in order to derive the associated precision bounds for distance estimation. We analyse the role of intensity anti-squeezing and temporal beam shaping, and find that quantum solutions may be appealing mostly for short-distance applications.
one_liner: This work derives quantum precision limits for distance ranging with optical frequency comb pulses, including squeezing and mode-shaped detection.
why_it_matters: It quantifies when quantum resources actually improve comb-based ranging and indicates the practical regime is short-range metrology rather than generic long-distance sensing.
detailed_summary: The paper develops an effective-Hamiltonian treatment of optical-pulse ranging with quantum frequency combs and derives distance-estimation bounds in the presence of atmospheric dispersion. It compares classical modal engineering and mode-sensitive detection against quantum-enhanced strategies that use squeezing. The analysis explicitly tracks how intensity anti-squeezing and temporal beam shaping affect achievable uncertainty. The main conclusion is that quantum enhancement is possible but is most attractive in short-distance operating regimes.
main_result: It establishes precision bounds for comb-based distance estimation and shows that squeezing-based gains are limited and mainly favorable for short-range ranging.
application_angle: The results help designers of precision lidar and timing systems decide whether the complexity of squeezed-comb sources is justified for practical ranging tasks.

title: In-Situ Simultaneous Magic State Injection on Arbitrary CSS qLDPC Codes
abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes can encode many logical qubits within a single code block at low physical qubit overhead, yet magic state injection into such codes remains largely underexplored. Existing state injection proposals for qLDPC codes predominantly follow an external prepare-and-transfer paradigm, in which raw magic states are prepared outside the target code block and subsequently injected via inter-code operations. We propose the first \emph{in-situ} magic state injection: a scheme in which logical magic states are directly prepared within a qLDPC memory block, only using resources required for syndrome extraction. We show that our scheme is generalizable to any CSS qLDPC code, with examples of circuit-level simulations on the $[[144,12,12]]$ Bivariate Bicycle (BB) code and the $[[225,9,4]]$ Hypergraph Product code. We focus on a regime where correlated injection errors are negligible. In the BB code, this corresponds to a configuration that simultaneously injects four logical $|Y\rangle$ states. Under a uniform depolarizing noise model with physical error rate $10^{-3}$, this achieves an injection error rate of $1.62 \times 10^{-3}$ per logical qubit, while the correlated-error contribution is only $2 \times 10^{-5}$ per logical qubit (about $1\%$ of the injection error rate). Under a hardware-motivated asymmetric noise model where single-qubit gate errors are $10\%$ of two-qubit gate errors, the injection error rate per logical qubit falls to $ 6.7 \times 10^{-4} $, below the error rate ($ 10^{-3} $) of the two-qubit gates used to encode the magic states. Its simplicity allows our scheme to be applied to arbitrary CSS qLDPC codes using only the ancilla qubits native to syndrome extraction, and yield a reduction in space overhead relative to both prepare-and-transfer approaches and surface-code-based magic state injection schemes.
one_liner: A general in-situ protocol injects multiple logical magic states directly inside arbitrary CSS qLDPC code blocks using only native syndrome-extraction resources.
why_it_matters: It cuts space overhead for fault-tolerant state preparation in qLDPC architectures and shows competitive logical injection error rates without separate prepare-and-transfer machinery.
detailed_summary: The authors propose the first in-situ magic-state injection method for arbitrary CSS qLDPC codes, avoiding external preparation and inter-code transfer steps. Circuit-level simulations are presented for the [[144,12,12]] bivariate bicycle code and the [[225,9,4]] hypergraph-product code. For the BB code, four logical |Y> states are injected simultaneously with per-logical-qubit error 1.62e-3 at physical error rate 1e-3, while correlated-error contributions remain around 2e-5. Under an asymmetric hardware-motivated noise model, the logical injection error drops to 6.7e-4, below the two-qubit gate error used during encoding.
main_result: The scheme directly prepares logical magic states inside CSS qLDPC blocks and achieves low logical injection error with negligible correlated-error contribution in simulated examples.
application_angle: This offers a more hardware-efficient route to non-Clifford resources for scalable fault-tolerant quantum processors based on qLDPC codes.

title: Enhanced enantiomer discrimination with chiral surface plasmons
abstract: Strong light-matter coupling in chiral cavities has been proposed as an effective way to selectively interact with an enantiomer that shares the same handedness as the cavity's chiral mode. We show that surface plasmons supported by a two-dimensional interface with both electric and chiral conductivities discriminate enantiomers more efficiently than chiral optical cavities. A quantum-electrodynamic treatment is developed to incorporate the molecule's electric and magnetic dipole moments. We show that the discrimination factor for a chiral plasmon can exceed that of the best chiral-mirror cavity by almost an order of magnitude due to stronger field confinement. In addition, surface plasmons couple to a dipole's projection onto an entire plane, whereas cavity (or free-space) modes couple only to a single polarization axis. This geometric difference produces a $\sqrt{2}$ orientation-averaged boost in chiral discrimination for chiral surface platforms. A handedness-preserving reflector further amplifies the enhancement, opening a practical route towards chiral sensing using twisted-layer platforms.
one_liner: Chiral surface plasmons on conductive 2D interfaces can discriminate molecular enantiomers more strongly than the best chiral optical cavities.
why_it_matters: It points to a realistic path for highly sensitive chiral sensing by combining strong confinement, planar coupling geometry, and handedness-preserving photonic interfaces.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a quantum-electrodynamic model for a chiral molecule interacting with surface plasmons supported by a two-dimensional interface carrying both electric and chiral conductivities. Electric and magnetic dipole contributions are included explicitly in the light-matter coupling. The authors find the chiral-plasmon discrimination factor can exceed that of the best chiral-mirror cavity by nearly an order of magnitude, with an additional orientation-averaged sqrt(2) boost from planar coupling geometry. A handedness-preserving reflector further enhances the effect, especially for twisted-layer platforms.
main_result: It predicts substantially stronger enantiomer discrimination for chiral plasmonic platforms than for state-of-the-art chiral optical cavities.
application_angle: The work is directly relevant to compact chip-scale chiral sensors for chemistry, pharmaceuticals, and molecular analysis.

title: Sparsely repeated 21.7 Tb/s Net-Rate Transoceanic Transmission with 266 km Ultra-Long Spans Enabled by Low IMI and Low loss Hollow Core Fiber
abstract: We demonstrate 21.7-Tb/s net-rate transmission across 6660-km with 266-km ultra-long spans of HCF. By exploiting a newly designed GTA-ST-HCF, high-power booster, and adaptive channel rates, we realize WDM transoceanic transmission with fewer than 30 repeaters.
one_liner: A hollow-core-fiber system delivers 21.7 Tb/s net transmission over 6660 km using ultra-long 266 km spans and fewer than 30 repeaters.
why_it_matters: It shows hollow-core fiber can materially reduce repeater count while sustaining transoceanic-class throughput, a key systems advance for long-haul optical networks.
detailed_summary: This experiment demonstrates 21.7 Tb/s net-rate wavelength-division-multiplexed transmission across 6660 km using a newly designed GTA-ST hollow-core fiber. The link operates with 266 km ultra-long spans, enabled by low loss, low intermodal interference, a high-power booster, and adaptive channel-rate allocation. The architecture achieves transoceanic-scale performance with fewer than 30 repeaters, which is unusually sparse for this reach. The result highlights the system-level advantages of hollow-core fiber for next-generation submarine communications.
main_result: The authors demonstrate 21.7 Tb/s net transmission over 6660 km with 266 km spans in hollow-core fiber, reducing the repeater requirement to under 30.
application_angle: This is immediately relevant to lower-latency, lower-infrastructure transoceanic communications and to scaling future high-capacity optical backbone networks.

title: Square-root Time Atom Reconfiguration Plan for Lattice-shaped Mobile Tweezers
abstract: This paper proposes a scalable planning algorithm for creating defect-free atom arrays in neutral-atom systems. The algorithm generates a $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt N)$ time plan for $N$ atoms by parallelizing atom transport using a two-dimensional lattice pattern generated by acousto-optic deflectors. Our approach is based on a divide-and-conquer strategy that decomposes an arbitrary reconfiguration problem into at most three one-dimensional shuttling tasks, enabling each atom to be transported with a total transportation cost of $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt N)$. Using the Gale--Ryser theorem, the proposed algorithm provides a highly reliable solution for arbitrary target geometries. We further introduce a peephole optimization technique that improves reconfiguration efficiency for grid target geometries. Numerical simulations on a 632$\times$632 atom array demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves a grid configuration plan that reduces the total transportation cost to 1/7 of state-of-the-art algorithms, while resulting in 32%--35% more atom captures. We believe that our scalability improvement contributes to realizing large-scale quantum computers based on neutral atoms. Our experimental code is available from https://github.com/kotamanegi/sqrt-time-atom-reconfigure.
one_liner: A divide-and-conquer planner achieves O(sqrt(N)) neutral-atom array reconfiguration time for lattice-shaped mobile-tweezer systems.
why_it_matters: Fast, scalable atom rearrangement is a central bottleneck in large neutral-atom processors, and this algorithm materially improves both transport cost and capture yield.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a scalable reconfiguration algorithm for defect-free neutral-atom assembly using two-dimensional lattice patterns generated by acousto-optic deflectors. The method decomposes arbitrary rearrangement problems into at most three one-dimensional shuttling stages, yielding O(sqrt(N)) transport cost per atom and overall plan time scaling. A peephole optimization further improves efficiency for grid targets. Simulations on a 632x632 array show about a 7x reduction in transportation cost relative to prior methods and 32%-35% more atom captures.
main_result: It provides an O(sqrt(N)) reconfiguration strategy that significantly outperforms prior algorithms in large-scale simulated neutral-atom array assembly.
application_angle: The planner directly supports larger and faster neutral-atom quantum computers by reducing preparation overhead for high-fidelity defect-free registers.

title: Addressing a device in a quantum network: A quantum approach including routing
abstract: In this work we propose an addressing scheme for quantum networks which relies on quantum states held by devices. Quantum network devices use their address state together with a request state that encodes the tasks to be executed. Our approach not only removes the necessity to classically communicate addresses, but also the need to communicate the operations a device must apply. It turns out that utilizing entanglement to encode addresses of devices in a quantum network leads to interesting applications such as overlaying different network states. We present a distributed quantum routing protocol using entanglement that coherently selects a route in a network of Bell-states for controlled-teleportation and lastly we prove that addressing using quantum states is equivalent to performing tasks in superposition in a quantum network.
one_liner: This paper encodes device addresses and routing choices into quantum states, enabling coherent tasking and route selection in quantum networks.
why_it_matters: It reframes network control as a quantum-native operation, potentially reducing classical coordination overhead and enabling superposed network behaviors unavailable classically.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a quantum addressing framework in which each device is identified by an address state, while requested actions are encoded in a separate quantum request state. They show that entanglement-based addressing can overlay different network states and remove the need to classically communicate both destination and operation details. A distributed routing protocol is introduced that coherently selects a path through a Bell-state network for controlled teleportation. The paper also proves an equivalence between quantum-state addressing and performing tasks in superposition across a network.
main_result: It introduces a quantum-native addressing and routing formalism that uses entangled states to coherently select devices and communication paths.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant to future programmable quantum internets where routing, control, and service invocation may need to operate coherently across distributed nodes.

title: Non-Markovian exceptional points in waveguide quantum electrodynamics
abstract: Spontaneous emission of a quantum emitter, such as an excited atom, is a fundamental process in quantum electrodynamics (QED), typically associated with exponential decay to the ground state accompanied by irreversible photon emission. This simple Markovian picture, however, is profoundly modified in the presence of time-delayed feedback, structured continua, or cooperative emission, as occurs when an emitter radiates in front of a mirror, when several emitters radiate collectively, or in the case of a giant atom. In such regimes, strong non-Markovian dynamics arise from photon reabsorption and interference effects, leading to pronounced deviations from exponential decay. Here we demonstrate the emergence of exceptional points (EPs) in these highly non-Markovian waveguide-QED environments, i.e., non-Markovian EPs. These EPs appear directly in the relaxation dynamics as sharp transitions to oscillatory behavior, manifested by the appearance of real zeros in the excited-state amplitude. We analyze in detail the spontaneous emission of giant atoms with two or more coupling points, highlighting the mechanisms leading to non-Markovian EPs, and show that similar phenomena arise in other waveguide-QED settings, such as the collective spontaneous emission of spatially separated point-like emitters. Our results reveal waveguide-QED systems as experimentally accessible platforms for realizing and exploring non-Markovian EP physics.
one_liner: Waveguide-QED with delayed feedback hosts non-Markovian exceptional points visible directly in spontaneous-emission dynamics.
why_it_matters: It identifies an experimentally reachable route to exceptional-point physics in open quantum photonic systems beyond the usual Markovian approximation.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that exceptional points can emerge in spontaneous-emission dynamics when emitters interact with structured, time-delayed waveguide environments. For giant atoms with multiple coupling points, interference and photon reabsorption create sharp transitions from monotonic relaxation to oscillatory decay, signaled by real zeros in the excited-state amplitude. The analysis is extended to spatially separated point emitters undergoing collective emission, showing the phenomenon is generic across several waveguide-QED geometries. The work frames these systems as accessible platforms for studying non-Hermitian and non-Markovian dynamics together.
main_result: The authors demonstrate non-Markovian exceptional points in waveguide-QED relaxation dynamics and identify their signatures in emitter decay behavior.
application_angle: The result suggests programmable photonic waveguides and giant-atom hardware can be used to engineer exotic open-system responses for quantum photonic devices and simulators.

title: PQC-Enhanced QKD Networks: A Layered Approach
abstract: We present a layered and modular network architecture that combines Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) to provide scalable end-to-end security across long distance multi-hop, trusted-node quantum networks. To ensure interoperability and efficient practical deployment, hop-wise tunnels between physically secured nodes are protected by WireGuard with periodically rotated pre-shared keys sourced via the ETSI GS QKD 014 interface. On top, Rosenpass performs a PQC key exchange to establish an end-to-end data channel without modifying deployed QKD devices or network protocols. This dual-layer composition yields post-quantum forward secrecy and authenticity under practical assumptions. We implement the design using open-source components and validate and evaluate it in simulated and lab test-beds. Experiments show uninterrupted operation over multi-hop paths, low resource footprint and fail-safe mechanisms. We further discuss the design's compositional security, wherein the security of each individual component is preserved under their combination and outline migration paths for operators integrating QKD-aware overlays in existing infrastructures.
one_liner: A layered network stack combines QKD links with PQC overlays to deliver scalable end-to-end secure multi-hop quantum networking.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical migration path for deployed QKD networks to gain end-to-end post-quantum security without changing the underlying QKD hardware.
detailed_summary: The architecture uses hop-wise WireGuard tunnels protected by periodically refreshed pre-shared keys sourced through the ETSI GS QKD 014 interface, while Rosenpass adds an end-to-end PQC key exchange layer. This separates physical-link QKD integration from application-layer end-to-end security, preserving interoperability with existing trusted-node QKD infrastructure. The implementation is built from open-source components and evaluated in simulation and lab test-beds, where it sustains uninterrupted multi-hop operation with low resource overhead and fail-safe behavior. The authors also discuss compositional security and operator migration paths.
platform_or_system: Trusted-node QKD network with PQC overlay
main_result: The work implements and validates a modular QKD-plus-PQC network stack that provides multi-hop operation and end-to-end post-quantum secure channels.
application_angle: This directly targets deployable quantum communication networks by upgrading current QKD infrastructures with stronger end-to-end security and operational compatibility.

title: A solid-state quantum memory based on a continuous optoacoustic system
abstract: Quantum memories for optical states are essential resources for quantum communication and information processing. We propose a quantum memory protocol based on coherent photon-phonon transduction in a Brillouin-active optical waveguide supporting traveling acoustic modes. A pulsed pump drives an effective beam-splitter interaction between optical and acoustic fields, enabling the mapping of a propagating optical quantum state onto a traveling phononic excitation and its subsequent retrieval on demand. Using a continuum optoacoustic model, we show that the protocol enables broadband quantum state storage in a distributed medium without relying on discrete cavity modes. Analytical and numerical results demonstrate high-fidelity storage and retrieval of squeezed and entangled states under experimentally realistic parameters. The memory bandwidth is set by the Brillouin interaction and can reach hundreds of MHz. Our results identify continuum Brillouin optomechanical systems as a scalable platform for broadband quantum memories and multimode quantum signal processing.
one_liner: A Brillouin-active waveguide is proposed as a broadband quantum memory by mapping traveling optical states into traveling phonons.
why_it_matters: It points to a scalable solid-state memory architecture for photonic quantum networks without relying on narrowband cavity modes.
detailed_summary: The protocol uses a pulsed pump to induce an effective beam-splitter interaction between optical and acoustic fields in a Brillouin-active waveguide. In the continuum optoacoustic model, a propagating optical quantum state is written into a traveling phononic excitation and later retrieved on demand, avoiding discrete cavity-mode constraints. Analytical and numerical simulations indicate high-fidelity storage and retrieval for squeezed and entangled states under realistic parameters, with bandwidths reaching hundreds of MHz. The distributed architecture also supports multimode quantum signal processing.
main_result: The authors show that a traveling-wave optoacoustic system can serve as a high-fidelity, broadband solid-state quantum memory for nonclassical optical states.
application_angle: Broadband on-chip quantum memories are a key missing component for quantum repeaters, photonic processors, and multiplexed quantum communication links.

title: A Global Model Structure for $\mathbb{K}$-Linear $\infty$-Local Systems
abstract: Parameterized stable homotopy theory organizes local systems of spectra over homotopy types, governed by a "yoga" of six functors. To provide semantics for the recently developed Linear Homotopy Type Theory (LHoTT), good model categories of these spectra are required, preferably monoidal with respect to the external smash product. We focus on the case of parameterized $H\mathbb{K}$-module spectra ($\infty$-local systems), motivated by recent applications of parameterized homotopy to topological quantum computing. While traditionally treated via dg-categories, we leverage combinatorial model structures on simplicial chain complexes to construct the first dedicated global model structure for $\mathbb{K}$-linear $\infty$-local systems, which offers better control than existing models for general parameterized spectra. In particular, when restricted to base 1-types, our model structure is monoidal with respect to the external tensor product, making it a candidate target semantics for the multiplicative fragment of LHoTT.
one_liner: This work builds a global model structure for K-linear infinity-local systems aimed at semantics for linear homotopy type theory.
why_it_matters: It strengthens the mathematical foundations for parameterized quantum-topological models, though its impact is more enabling than near-term experimental.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs a dedicated global model structure for parameterized HK-module spectra using combinatorial model structures on simplicial chain complexes. Compared with general parameterized-spectra models, the framework provides tighter control over K-linear infinity-local systems and is tailored to applications involving linear homotopy type theory. On base 1-types, the model is monoidal with respect to the external tensor product, making it a candidate semantic target for the multiplicative fragment of LHoTT. The motivation includes recent uses of parameterized homotopy in topological quantum computing.
main_result: The main result is the first dedicated global model structure for K-linear infinity-local systems, including monoidal behavior on base 1-types.
application_angle: Its practical relevance is indirect: it supplies formal machinery that could support rigorous models for topological quantum information and related architectures.

title: A plug-and-play superconducting quantum controller at millikelvin temperatures enables exceeding 99.9% average gate fidelity
abstract: The development of large-scale superconducting quantum computing requires efficient in-situ control methods that allow high-fidelity operations at millikelvin temperatures. Superconducting circuits based on Josephson junctions offer a promising solution due to their high speed, low power dissipation, and cryogenic nature. Here, we report a superconducting quantum controller that enables direct chip-to-chip interconnection with qubits at 10 mK and high-fidelity, all-digital manipulation. Randomized benchmarking reveals a uniformly high average Clifford fidelity of 99.9% with leakage to high energy levels on the order of $10^{-4}$, and an estimated average gate operation energy of 0.121 fJ, demonstrating the potential to resolve the control bottleneck in superconducting quantum computing.
one_liner: A millikelvin superconducting controller achieves all-digital qubit control with 99.9% average Clifford fidelity and ultralow energy per gate.
why_it_matters: It addresses the cryogenic control bottleneck in superconducting quantum computing by moving efficient high-fidelity control closer to the qubits.
detailed_summary: The paper reports a plug-and-play superconducting quantum controller designed for direct chip-to-chip interconnection with qubits at 10 mK. Using an all-digital control scheme implemented with Josephson-junction circuitry, the system performs high-fidelity gate operations while keeping dissipation extremely low. Randomized benchmarking gives a uniform average Clifford fidelity of 99.9%, leakage to higher levels is on the order of 10^-4, and the estimated average gate energy is 0.121 fJ. These metrics indicate compatibility with scaling dense cryogenic control hardware.
platform_or_system: Millikelvin superconducting qubits with on-chip controller
main_result: The demonstrated controller delivers direct cryogenic qubit manipulation with 99.9% average gate fidelity, very low leakage, and sub-femtojoule energy per gate.
application_angle: Low-power cryogenic controllers are critical for scaling superconducting processors because they reduce wiring, heat load, and room-temperature control complexity.

title: Spectrum-Generating Algebra in Higher Dimensional Gauge Theories
abstract: Non-equilibrium properties of strongly interacting gauge theories are often intractable with classical simulation methods. Due to recent developments of quantum simulations, studies of their properties in two spatial dimensions are becoming accessible. By demonstrating the existence of an approximate spectrum-generating algebra for a pure gauge plaquette ladder, we predict and verify the existence of Quantum Many-Body Scars in spin-1 Quantum Link Models. The analysis of the model is facilitated by a dualization process that maps the original gauge theory to a constrained spin chain. Was it not for the constraint, the system would have an exact spectrum-generating algebra. We propose a set of observables for diagnosing an approximate spectrum-generating algebra, which is expected to guide quantum simulators toward interesting physical regimes.
one_liner: An approximate spectrum-generating algebra predicts quantum many-body scars in a 2D gauge-theory-inspired quantum link ladder.
why_it_matters: It provides experimentally useful diagnostics for finding nonergodic dynamics in quantum simulators of lattice gauge theories.
detailed_summary: The authors study a pure-gauge plaquette ladder and identify an approximate spectrum-generating algebra that organizes special nonthermal eigenstate structure. By dualizing the gauge model to a constrained spin chain, they clarify how the constraint spoils an exact algebra yet still preserves scarred dynamics in a spin-1 quantum link model. They then predict and verify quantum many-body scars and introduce observables that diagnose the approximate algebra in practice. The framework is intended to guide experiments toward regimes with long-lived coherent dynamics.
platform_or_system: Spin-1 quantum link model on a plaquette ladder
main_result: The work predicts and verifies quantum many-body scars in a higher-dimensional gauge-theory setting via an approximate spectrum-generating algebra.
application_angle: These diagnostics can help tune quantum simulation hardware to robust nonergodic regimes useful for probing lattice gauge dynamics and coherence preservation.

title: Quantum-Boosted Nonlinear Tunneling Driven by a Bright Squeezed Vacuum
abstract: Nonlinear processes, mediated by multiphoton interactions rather than single-photon response, drive numerous fundamental phenomena and momentous applications in modern physics. Among these processes, tunneling ionization plays a pivotal role as it drives high-harmonic generation, forming the basis of attosecond science and enabling the visualization and control of electron motion at its natural time scale. Quantum light, with its unique capacity for quantum noise redistribution, offers a transformative solution to boost nonlinear responses. Here, we report the first experiment of nonlinear tunneling ionization of the most fundamental system of atoms boosted by a quantum light -- bright squeezed vacuum (BSV). Remarkably, the tunneling ionization of a single sodium atom induced by a 300 nJ BSV beam matches that achieved with a 7.1 {\textmu}J coherent light source, demonstrating a dramatic boost in nonlinear efficiency from phase-squeezed quantum light. Moreover, the effective intensity of the BSV light and thus the boosted tunneling ionization can be precisely controlled by tuning the degree of phase squeezing while maintaining the average pulse energy. These findings provide fundamental insights into quantum-boosted nonlinear effect and pave the way for efficient frequency conversion and quantum-controlled molecular reactions using tailored quantum light sources.
one_liner: Bright squeezed vacuum drives sodium tunneling ionization with far lower pulse energy than coherent light.
why_it_matters: It shows quantum-noise engineering can strongly enhance a flagship strong-field nonlinear process, pointing to lower-power attosecond and frequency-conversion platforms.
detailed_summary: The work reports tunneling ionization of sodium atoms driven by bright squeezed vacuum rather than a classical coherent pulse. A 300 nJ phase-squeezed BSV beam achieves ionization comparable to a 7.1 uJ coherent source, indicating a large nonlinear efficiency boost at fixed average energy. The authors further show that the effective intensity governing ionization can be tuned by adjusting the squeezing level, linking the response to engineered field fluctuations rather than mean power alone.
platform_or_system: Sodium atoms driven by bright squeezed vacuum
main_result: Phase-squeezed bright squeezed vacuum produces sodium tunneling ionization matching a much higher-energy coherent beam while allowing control through the squeezing degree.
application_angle: Quantum-tailored light could reduce power requirements for high-harmonic generation, frequency conversion, and strong-field control of atoms or molecules.

title: Scalar axion field of toroidal electromagnetic pulses
abstract: Axion electrodynamics extends Maxwell's theory by postulating a hypothetical pseudoscalar axion field sourced by a scalar product of electric and magnetic fields. In this work, we demonstrate that a superposition of toroidal electromagnetic pulses propagating in free space naturally exhibits localized regions, where $\bm{E}\cdot\bm{B}\ne0$. As a consequence of axion electrodynamics, these structured light pulses generate a space-time localized pseudoscalar field co-propagating with the pulses.
