This result should not be interpreted as a mechanism for generating axion particles by light, but rather as a consequence of adopting the axion electrodynamics extension to Maxwell's equations.
one_liner: Structured toroidal pulses in axion electrodynamics create localized co-propagating pseudoscalar field regions where E·B is nonzero.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete structured-light configuration that realizes axion-electrodynamics signatures in free space, useful for tabletop field engineering and fundamental photonics studies.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes superpositions of toroidal electromagnetic pulses and shows that they naturally generate localized spacetime regions with nonzero E·B. Within axion electrodynamics, those regions source a pseudoscalar field that co-propagates with the optical waveform. The result is explicitly framed as an electromagnetic-field consequence of the extended theory, not as optical production of axion particles.
main_result: A superposition of toroidal pulses is shown to generate a localized pseudoscalar field in axion electrodynamics through engineered nonzero E·B overlap.
application_angle: The geometry offers a structured-light route for probing exotic electrodynamic effects and may inform photonic protocols that exploit tailored field topology.

title: Quantum optomechanics of lossy bodies: general approach and structured squeezed vacuum effects
abstract: We investigate the overall optomechanical force experienced by a macroscopic lossy object in free space under external quantum illumination. To this end, utilizing the Modified Langevin Noise Formalism (MLNF), we derive the time-averaged expectation value of the Maxwell stress tensor for a non-equilibrium scenario in which the incoming scattering field is prepared in an arbitrary mixed quantum state, while the medium-assisted field is maintained in local thermal equilibrium. In the limit of full radiation-matter thermal equilibrium, our expression exactly recovers the well-known fluctuation-dissipation relation governing the Casimir effect, and, under coherent illumination, it yields the standard classical radiation pressure. We demonstrate that by driving the scattering field with an anisotropic, multimode squeezed vacuum state, the spatial profile of the electromagnetic quantum fluctuations can be engineered to exhibit broken rotational symmetry, thereby inducing a purely quantum mechanical force acting on the object. Such mechanical interaction is generated in the strict absence of a mean field, $\langle\hat{\mathbf{E}}\rangle=0$, and its non-classical nature is evidenced by its reliance on second-order field correlations $\langle\hat{\mathbf{E}}^2\rangle$, unlike classical optical radiation pressure governed by the squared mean field $\langle\hat{\mathbf{E}}\rangle^2$. Applying this exact formulation to a homogeneous lossy sphere, we demonstrate the experimental feasibility of the effect using realistic material parameters and optical estimations. Ultimately, we establish a general formalism for macroscopic quantum optomechanics that operates beyond the constraints of thermal equilibrium, enabling the prediction of regimes where the purely quantum force circumvents classical mean fields and shot noise while preserving the object's macroscopic quantum coherence.
one_liner: A general theory predicts purely quantum optomechanical forces on lossy bodies illuminated by structured squeezed vacuum with zero mean field.
why_it_matters: It opens a path to mechanically actuate macroscopic objects via engineered quantum correlations instead of classical intensity, relevant for precision control and low-noise force transduction.
detailed_summary: Using the Modified Langevin Noise Formalism, the authors derive the time-averaged Maxwell-stress force on a lossy macroscopic object under arbitrary mixed quantum illumination outside thermal equilibrium. The framework recovers Casimir physics at full equilibrium and standard radiation pressure under coherent drive, then extends to anisotropic multimode squeezed vacuum. For a homogeneous lossy sphere, they predict a broken-symmetry force arising from second-order correlations with vanishing mean electric field, and argue that realistic material and optical parameters make the effect experimentally accessible.
platform_or_system: Lossy sphere under structured squeezed-vacuum illumination
main_result: Structured multimode squeezed vacuum can exert a measurable optomechanical force on a lossy object even when the mean field is exactly zero.
application_angle: This could enable low-classical-background force control, quantum-limited optomechanics, and new photonic interfaces where fluctuation engineering replaces bright drive fields.

title: Fault-Tolerant One-Shot Entanglement Generation with Constant-Sized Quantum Devices in the Plane
abstract: Consider a rectangular grid of qubits in 2D with single-qubit and nearest-neighbor two-qubit operations subject to local stochastic Pauli noise. At different length scales, this setup describes both a single quantum computing device with geometrically limited connectivity between qubits arranged on a disc, and planar networks composed of quantum repeater stations of constant size. We give a protocol which robustly generates entanglement between distant qubits in this setup. For noise below a constant threshold error strength, it generates a constant-fidelity Bell pair between qubits separated by an arbitrarily large distance $R$. To generate distance-$R$ entanglement, a rectangular grid of qubits of dimensions $Θ(R)\times Θ(\mathsf{poly}(\log R))$ suffices. Our protocol applies quantum operations in one shot, establishing a Bell state in a constant time up to a known Pauli correction. In contrast, existing entanglement generation protocols either require local quantum devices controlling a number of qubits growing with the targeted distance, or are not single-shot, i.e., have a distance-dependent execution time. The protocol leverages many-body entanglement in networks and provides the first example of a short-range entangled state in 2D with long-range localizable entanglement robust to local stochastic Pauli noise. As an immediate corollary, we construct a 2D-local stabilizer Hamiltonian whose Gibbs states possess long-range localizable entanglement at constant positive temperature.
one_liner: A one-shot protocol generates constant-fidelity long-distance Bell pairs on noisy 2D nearest-neighbor quantum devices of constant local size.
why_it_matters: It addresses scalable planar entanglement distribution without long runtimes or growing local hardware, which is central for repeater networks and modular fault-tolerant architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper gives a protocol for preparing long-range entanglement on a rectangular 2D grid with local stochastic Pauli noise and only nearest-neighbor operations. Below a constant error threshold, it produces a Bell pair of constant fidelity across distance R using a grid of size Theta(R) by Theta(poly(log R)) and a constant-depth one-shot operation sequence up to Pauli frame correction. The construction also implies a 2D-local stabilizer Hamiltonian whose finite-temperature Gibbs states retain long-range localizable entanglement.
platform_or_system: Planar nearest-neighbor qubit grids and repeater networks
main_result: The authors achieve fault-tolerant, constant-time, distance-independent Bell-pair generation across arbitrarily large planar separations with constant-sized local devices.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to practical repeater layouts and modular quantum hardware that need fast entanglement distribution under realistic geometric constraints.

title: Dynamical decoupling and quantum error correction with SU(d) symmetries
abstract: Dynamical decoupling is a long-established and effective way to suppress unwanted interactions in qubit systems, enabling advances in fields ranging from quantum metrology to quantum computing. For general qudit systems, however, comparable protocols remain rare, mainly because Hamiltonian engineering in higher dimensions lacks the geometric intuition available for qubits. Here we present a general framework for dynamical decoupling in qudit systems, based on Lie group representation theory. By extending the group theory approach to dynamical decoupling, we show how decoupling groups can be systematically identified among the finite subgroups of SU(d) by analyzing their access to the irreducible components of the operator space. As an application, we construct new pulse sequences for interacting qutrit systems based on finite subgroups of SU(3), and show how subgroup factorizations and group orientations can be exploited to obtain shorter and more experimentally practical protocols for spin-1 systems with large zero-field splitting. We further show that the same symmetry-based framework yields quantum error-correcting codes: whenever a finite subgroup of SU(d) acts as a decoupling group for the relevant error algebra, the associated one-dimensional symmetry sectors define codespaces satisfying the Knill-Laflamme conditions, thereby unifying dynamical decoupling and quantum error correction in multi-level quantum systems.
one_liner: A symmetry-based SU(d) framework unifies qudit dynamical decoupling and error-correcting code construction, with new practical qutrit pulse sequences.
why_it_matters: It provides a systematic route to protect multi-level quantum hardware, especially spin-1 and qutrit platforms that are increasingly important for sensing and computing.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate qudit dynamical decoupling using Lie-group representation theory, identifying finite SU(d) subgroups that access the required irreducible operator-space components. They instantiate the method for SU(3), deriving qutrit pulse sequences and optimized subgroup factorizations/orientations tailored to spin-1 systems with large zero-field splitting. The same symmetry sectors define codespaces satisfying Knill-Laflamme conditions, yielding a common construction for both decoupling and error correction.
main_result: Finite-subgroup symmetries of SU(d) are used to systematically build shorter qudit decoupling sequences and corresponding error-correcting codes.
application_angle: The framework can reduce control overhead and improve coherence in multi-level sensors and processors, especially where qudits offer hardware-native advantages over qubits.

title: Adaptive Deformation of Color Code in Square Lattices with Defects
abstract: Quantum error correction is a crucial technology for fault tolerant quantum computing. On superconducting platforms, hardware defects in large scale quantum processors can disrupt the regular lattice structure of topological codes and impair their error correction capabilities. Although defect adaptive methods for surface codes have been extensively studied, other topological codes such as color codes still lack a systematic framework for handling defects. To address this issue, we propose a universal superstabilizer scheme applicable to data qubit defects in arbitrary stabilizer codes. Based on this scheme, we develop concrete repair methods for isolated defects of both internal data qubits and ancilla qubits in color codes defined on square lattices. Furthermore, for ancilla qubit defects, we present two optimization schemes. One scheme reuses neighboring ancilla qubits, and the other employs iSWAP gates. Unlike conventional approaches that directly disable neighboring data qubits and thus cause resource waste, both of our schemes avoid such waste and consequently achieve a lower logical error rate.Integrating the above techniques, we construct a comprehensive defect adaptive architecture for color codes to handle various defect clusters. We also show that our scheme supports a full transversal Clifford gate set and lattice surgery operations. These results provide a systematic theoretical pathway for deploying robust and low overhead color codes on defective quantum hardware.
one_liner: A defect-adaptive color-code architecture repairs data and ancilla defects on square-lattice hardware while preserving transversal Clifford operations.
why_it_matters: It targets a practical bottleneck for superconducting fault-tolerant processors, where fabrication defects can otherwise waste qubits and degrade logical performance.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a universal superstabilizer scheme for handling data-qubit defects in arbitrary stabilizer codes and applies it to square-lattice color codes. It gives explicit repair strategies for isolated internal data-qubit and ancilla defects, plus two ancilla-optimization methods based on neighboring-ancilla reuse and iSWAP gates. These methods avoid disabling nearby data qubits, reduce resource waste, support clustered-defect handling, and retain full transversal Clifford gates together with lattice surgery.
platform_or_system: Square-lattice color codes on superconducting hardware
main_result: The authors present a comprehensive defect-tolerant repair framework for square-lattice color codes that lowers logical error rates without sacrificing key fault-tolerant operations.
application_angle: This improves the deployability of color codes on imperfect chips by turning realistic fabrication defects into a manageable architectural constraint rather than a showstopper.

title: Frequency combs and coherent dissipative structures in nonlinear optical microresonators
abstract: Laser-driven high-Q Kerr-nonlinear optical microresonators enable parametric oscillation with low-power continuous-wave lasers and host a variety of coherent dissipative structures, including dissipative Kerr solitons and switching waves. These time-periodic structures constitute coherent optical frequency combs, and photonic-chip integration has miniaturized them to the chip scale. Such photonic-integrated, microresonator-based frequency combs - often termed 'microcombs' or 'Kerr combs' - have been demonstrated in various system-level and scientific applications. They complement femtosecond-laser-based frequency combs when high repetition rates, broad bandwidths, or high power per comb line are needed. This review introduces the field of microcombs and outlines the fundamental physical principles governing the generation of coherent frequency combs in microresonators.
one_liner: A review of Kerr-microresonator frequency comb physics, coherent dissipative structures, and chip-scale microcomb applications.
why_it_matters: Microcombs are a mature photonic platform for compact precision timing, spectroscopy, telecom, and microwave-photonic systems with high repetition rates and strong line power.
detailed_summary: This review surveys laser-driven high-Q Kerr microresonators that support parametric oscillation, switching waves, and dissipative Kerr solitons. It explains how coherent dissipative structures generate stable optical frequency combs and how dispersion, nonlinearity, pumping, and cavity loss govern comb formation. The paper also situates chip-scale microcombs against femtosecond-laser combs, emphasizing regimes where large mode spacing, broad bandwidth, and high per-line power are advantageous. It provides a systems-level perspective on why integrated microcombs have become important for deployed photonic applications.
main_result: The paper synthesizes the core physics and application landscape of coherent microresonator frequency comb generation on integrated photonic platforms.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to deployable chip-scale comb sources for telecom, metrology, spectroscopy, and microwave photonics.

title: Distributed Quantum Property Testing with Communication Constraints
abstract: We introduce a framework for distributed quantum inference under communication constraints. In our model, $m$ distributed nodes each receive one copy of an unknown $d$-dimensional quantum state $ρ$, before communicating via a constrained one-way communication channel with a central node, which aims to infer some property of $ρ$. This framework generalizes the classical distributed inference framework introduced by Acharya, Canonne, and Tyagi [COLT2019], by allowing quantum resources such as quantum communication and shared entanglement. Within this setting, we focus on the fundamental problem of quantum state certification: Given a complete description of some state $σ$, decide whether $ρ=σ$ or $\|ρ-σ\|_1\geq ε$. Additionally, we focus on the case of limited quantum communication between distributed nodes and the central node. We show that when each communication channel is limited to only $n_q\leq \log d$ qubits, then the sample complexity of distributed state certification is $\mathcal{O}(\frac{d^2}{2^{n_q}ε^2})$ when public randomness is available to all nodes. Moreover, under the assumption that the channels used by the distributed nodes are mixedness-preserving, we prove a matching lower bound. We further demonstrate that shared randomness is necessary to achieve the above complexity, by proving an $Ω(\frac{d^3}{4^{n_q} ε^2})$ lower bound in the private-coin setting under the same assumption as above. Our lower bounds leverage a recently introduced quantum analogue of the celebrated Ingster-Suslina method and generalize arguments from the classical setting. Together, our work provides the first characterization of distributed quantum state certification in the regime of limited quantum communication and establishes a general framework for distributed quantum inference with communication constraints.
one_liner: This work characterizes the communication-sample tradeoff for distributed quantum state certification with limited one-way quantum links.
why_it_matters: It sets quantitative limits for certifying remote quantum states in networked quantum systems where bandwidth and shared randomness are constrained.
detailed_summary: The authors formalize distributed quantum inference where multiple nodes each receive one copy of an unknown d-dimensional state and communicate one-way to a central referee. For state certification with channels limited to n_q <= log d qubits, they prove an O(d^2/(2^{n_q} epsilon^2)) sample complexity with public randomness. Under mixedness-preserving channel assumptions, they establish matching lower bounds and show a stronger Omega(d^3/(4^{n_q} epsilon^2)) barrier in the private-coin setting. The analysis extends quantum Ingster-Suslina methods to distributed certification and clarifies the operational value of shared randomness.
main_result: The paper gives tight or near-tight communication-constrained sample complexity bounds for distributed quantum state certification and proves shared randomness is essential for the best scaling.
application_angle: These bounds inform the design of bandwidth-limited quantum network protocols for verification, certification, and remote sensing of quantum states.

title: Gauss law codes and vacuum codes from lattice gauge theories
abstract: We develop a comprehensive framework for constructing quantum error correcting codes (QECCs) from Abelian lattice gauge theories (LGTs) using quantum reference frames (QRFs) as a unifying formalism. We consider LGTs with arbitrary compact Abelian gauge groups supported on lattices in arbitrary numbers of spatial dimensions, and we work with both pure gauge theories and theories with couplings to bosonic and fermionic matter. The codes that we construct fall into two classes: First, Gauss law codes identify the code subspace with the full gauge-invariant sector of the theory. In models with matter coupled to gauge fields, these codes inherit a natural subsystem structure in which gauge-invariant Wilson loops and dressed matter excitations factorize the code space. Second, vacuum codes restrict the code subspace to the matter vacuum sector within the gauge-invariant subspace, yielding codes where errors correspond to gauge-invariant charge excitations rather than to violations of the Gauss law. Despite their distinct setup, we show that when the gauge group is finite, vacuum codes are unitarily equivalent to pure gauge theory Gauss law codes, and that when the group is continuous, this is only true upon a charge coarse-graining of the vacuum code. In all cases, QRFs provide a systematic apparatus for fully characterizing the codes' algebraic structures and correctable error sets. For clarity, we illustrate our general results in $\mathbb{Z}_2$-gauge theory, as well as in scalar and fermionic QED. These findings offer fundamental insights into the parallelism between quantum error correction and gauge theory and point toward practical advantages for simulating LGTs on noisy quantum devices.
one_liner: A general framework builds quantum error-correcting codes from Abelian lattice gauge theories using quantum reference frames.
why_it_matters: It connects gauge-theory structure to practical code design for noisy quantum simulators and clarifies what error sectors can be corrected in matter-coupled models.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs two code families from Abelian lattice gauge theories on arbitrary lattices and dimensions: Gauss law codes, defined by the full gauge-invariant sector, and vacuum codes, restricted to the matter-vacuum subspace. Using quantum reference frames, it derives the algebraic structure of logical observables and correctable errors for pure-gauge and matter-coupled systems, including bosons and fermions. For finite gauge groups, vacuum codes are shown to be unitarily equivalent to pure-gauge Gauss law codes, while continuous groups require charge coarse-graining for equivalence. Worked examples in Z2 gauge theory and scalar and fermionic QED make the construction concrete.
main_result: The work provides a unified gauge-theoretic recipe for QECC construction and identifies when vacuum and Gauss law code families are equivalent.
application_angle: It offers code structures tailored to lattice-gauge simulations on noisy quantum hardware, potentially improving fault tolerance and resource efficiency.

title: Probing of Core Excitons in Solid NaF with Polarization-Selective Attosecond Time-Resolved Four-Wave Mixing Spectroscopy
abstract: Nonlinear Four-wave mixing processes are a powerful technique to unravel ultrafast dynamics in solid-state systems. Here, we employ attosecond four-wave mixing spectroscopy with one extreme ultraviolet (XUV) pump and two independently delayed, noncollinear near-infrared (NIR) probes to resolve the ultrafast decoherence of both dipole-allowed and dipole-forbidden core excitons at the Na+ L2,3 edge in sodium fluoride (NaF). The decoherence times of the core excitons are observed to be much faster than the 8 fs limit of the instrument response time, which is attributed to strong exciton-phonon coupling. Furthermore, polarization control of the NIR probes (Perpendicular and parallel polarizations) reveals that the bright core excitons exhibit s-like orbital angular momentum, while dark core excitons, reached by two-photon excitation, exhibit p-like angular momentum.
one_liner: Attosecond polarization-selective four-wave mixing resolves ultrafast bright and dark core excitons in solid NaF.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates element-edge-resolved nonlinear spectroscopy of exciton symmetry and decoherence on sub-10-fs timescales in a solid-state material.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses one XUV pump and two independently delayed noncollinear NIR probes to perform attosecond four-wave mixing at the Na+ L2,3 edge in sodium fluoride. It resolves both dipole-allowed bright core excitons and dipole-forbidden dark core excitons accessed through two-photon pathways. The measured decoherence is faster than the 8 fs instrument response, indicating very strong exciton-phonon coupling. Polarization-selective probing further distinguishes s-like angular momentum for bright excitons and p-like character for dark excitons.
main_result: The study demonstrates polarization-resolved attosecond four-wave mixing that identifies exciton symmetry and reveals sub-8-fs decoherence of core excitons in NaF.
application_angle: The method is relevant for ultrafast materials diagnostics and for characterizing solid-state excitations important to photonic and quantum-material device engineering.

title: Necessary and sufficient conditions for the N-representability of functionals of the one-electron reduced density matrix
abstract: We establish necessary and sufficient conditions for the N-representability of the universal one-electron reduced density matrix functional. Functionals satisfying these conditions are guaranteed to yield variational upper bounds on the true energy in one-electron reduced density matrix functional theory, regardless of the strength of the interparticle repulsion. Conversely, any functional violating these conditions will necessarily underestimate the true energy for certain systems. These exact constraints impose a stringent restriction on density matrix functional approximations, as many existing functionals-including the Hartree-Fock functional-appear to violate them. This mathematical formalism, therefore, can guide the development of new approximate functionals and numerical algorithms.
one_liner: This paper derives exact necessary and sufficient N-representability conditions for universal 1-RDM functionals.
why_it_matters: The constraints expose failures of common approximate functionals and provide a rigorous route to more reliable electronic-structure methods for quantum materials and molecules.
detailed_summary: The authors establish exact conditions under which a universal one-electron reduced density matrix functional is N-representable. Any functional obeying these conditions yields a variational upper bound to the true energy for arbitrary interaction strength, while any violating them necessarily underestimates the energy for some systems. The result sharply constrains approximate 1-RDMFT design and suggests that several widely used functionals, including Hartree-Fock, fail the exact test. The framework is therefore both a mathematical characterization and a practical filter for improved algorithms.
platform_or_system: One-electron reduced density matrix functional theory
main_result: The paper proves exact N-representability criteria that are both necessary and sufficient for valid universal 1-RDM functionals.
application_angle: Better-controlled density-matrix functionals can improve predictive modeling for molecules and materials relevant to precision sensing and device design.

title: QAFE$^2$: Quantum Accelerated Multiscale Finite Element Analysis
abstract: The computational cost of concurrent multiscale finite element methods is dominated by the repeated solution of microscopic representative volume element (RVE) problems at macroscopic quadrature points. In this work, we introduce a quantum-classical framework for multiscale finite element analysis (QAFE$^2$) that leverages quantum parallelism to fundamentally alter the scaling of RVE-based homogenisation. At the single-RVE level, the proposed quantum solver attains polylogarithmic complexity with respect to the microscopic discretisation size, yielding an exponential asymptotic speedup over the best available classical solvers. More importantly, QAFE$^2$ exploits quantum superposition and entanglement to evaluate, in a single quantum execution, the entire ensemble of RVE problems associated with all macroscopic quadrature points. This capability is a form of intrinsic quantum concurrency with no classical analogue. Numerical experiments on one- and two-dimensional model problems with known analytical solutions confirm the accuracy of the proposed formulation and verify the theoretical computational scaling and parallel performance.
one_liner: QAFE^2 uses quantum parallelism to accelerate repeated multiscale finite-element microproblem solves across quadrature points.
why_it_matters: It targets a core computational bottleneck in multiscale simulation and suggests quantum speedups for engineering design workflows that rely on homogenization.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a quantum-classical multiscale finite element framework in which microscopic representative volume element problems are solved with a quantum routine. At the single-RVE level, the solver is argued to have polylogarithmic complexity in the microscopic discretization size, outperforming classical approaches asymptotically. More unusually, the method places the full ensemble of RVE instances associated with macroscopic quadrature points into quantum superposition, enabling intrinsic concurrent evaluation. Numerical tests on 1D and 2D benchmark problems confirm solution accuracy and the predicted scaling trends.
main_result: The work demonstrates a quantum-enabled multiscale FEM strategy with exponential asymptotic speedup for single RVE solves and quantum concurrency across many RVEs.
application_angle: If realized on useful quantum hardware, it could reduce compute costs for photonics, materials, and structural simulations that depend on repeated homogenization.

title: Error Correction in Lattice Quantum Electrodynamics with Quantum Reference Frames
abstract: Is gauge symmetry merely a redundancy in our description, or does it carry a deeper information-theoretic significance? Quantum error-correcting codes (QECCs) show that redundancy can serve as a resource for protecting information against noise. In this work, we ask whether gauge theories can be understood in similar terms, and make this idea concrete in lattice quantum electrodynamics (QED), building on and extending earlier works that established a bridge between gauge systems, stabilizer codes, and quantum reference frames (QRFs). For Abelian gauge groups, we show that explicit recovery operations can be constructed using group-theoretical methods for error sets determined by both ideal and non-ideal QRFs. Applied to lattice QED, this yields two QECC structures: one in the pure-gauge sector and one including fermions. We construct a gauge-field QRF based on spanning trees of the lattice and a fermionic field QRF from the matter field, thereby making explicit how physical information is encoded. While the syndromes of gauge-violating errors associated with constraint measurements are generically degenerate, QRFs resolve this degeneracy and single out families of correctable errors. This establishes lattice QED as a QECC beyond the stabilizer setting and shows concretely how gauge symmetry provides an encoding structure that supports error correction.
one_liner: Shows lattice QED can act as a non-stabilizer quantum error-correcting code using quantum reference frames to resolve gauge-error syndromes.
why_it_matters: It connects gauge-theory structure to explicit recovery procedures, suggesting new fault-tolerant encodings for physics-native quantum simulation hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates Abelian lattice gauge theories, specifically lattice QED, as quantum error-correcting codes by using quantum reference frames to expose how physical information is redundantly encoded. It derives explicit recovery operations for error sets arising from both ideal and imperfect reference frames, covering a pure-gauge code and a matter-coupled code with fermions. A spanning-tree gauge-field reference frame and a matter-based fermionic reference frame are constructed to lift degeneracies in gauge-violation syndromes and identify correctable error families. The result extends the gauge-code correspondence beyond standard stabilizer constructions.
platform_or_system: lattice QED with gauge and fermionic fields
main_result: It demonstrates explicit error-correction structures and recovery maps for lattice QED sectors by using quantum reference frames to disambiguate gauge-violating syndromes.
application_angle: The framework could inform physics-native encodings and decoder design for fault-tolerant quantum simulators and hardware targeting lattice gauge models.

title: Heterogeneous architectures enable a 138x reduction in physical qubit requirements for fault-tolerant quantum computing under detailed accounting
abstract: Quantum computer hardware is predicted to scale over hundreds of thousands of qubits coming online in the next decade. Despite significant theoretical and experimental QEC progress, quantum computer architecture has suffered a significant gap, with bottom-up physical-device-driven challenges largely disconnected from top-down QEC-code-driven considerations. In this work, we unify these two views, presenting a complete heterogeneous quantum computing architecture incorporating task-specific hardware selection and QEC encoding, and agnostic to code selection or physical qubit parameters. Our approach further enables special-purpose processing modules, and includes a full microarchitecture for fault-tolerant implementation of interfaces between quantum processing units and quantum memories. Using this architecture and a new fully featured compiler functioning across subsystems at the scale of $1,000$ logical qubits, we schedule and orchestrate a variety of algorithms down to hardware-specific instructions; a detailed accounting of all operations reveals up to 551x reduction in algorithmic logical error and up to 138x reduction in physical-qubit overhead compared to a monolithic baseline architecture. We then consider the factorization of 2048-bit RSA-integers; using an experimentally demonstrated grid-coupling topology, factoring RSA-2048 requires 381k physical qubits and 9.2 days, which can be reduced to 4.9 days via addition of an algorithm-specific accelerator for the Adder subroutine (requiring 439k qubits). Finally, assuming hypothetical long-range coupling, implementing quantum memory using qLDPC codes reduces the resources required for factoring to just 190k qubits and under 10 days. These results and the tooling we have built indicate that heterogeneous quantum-computer architectures can deliver significant, verifiable benefits on realistic hardware.
one_liner: A heterogeneous fault-tolerant architecture and compiler cut physical-qubit needs by up to 138x versus a monolithic design.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete systems-level path to smaller, faster fault-tolerant machines by co-optimizing hardware modules, memories, interfaces, and compilation.
detailed_summary: The work presents a full-stack heterogeneous quantum computing architecture that assigns different hardware and code choices to processing, memory, and interface roles rather than enforcing a single monolithic stack. A compiler schedules algorithms across these subsystems at the 1,000-logical-qubit scale and performs detailed accounting of operations, interfaces, and fault-tolerant microarchitecture. Under this model, the authors report up to 551x lower algorithmic logical error and up to 138x lower physical-qubit overhead than a monolithic baseline. For RSA-2048 factoring, they estimate 381k qubits and 9.2 days with experimentally demonstrated grid coupling, improved to 190k qubits with long-range-coupled qLDPC memory assumptions.
main_result: It shows that heterogeneous processing, memory, and accelerator modules can dramatically reduce qubit overhead and runtime for large fault-tolerant workloads.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to practical quantum computer design because it translates architectural choices into quantified hardware savings for benchmark applications.

title: Probing the Planck scale with quantum computation
abstract: General relativity and quantum mechanics are incompatible at the Planck scale. This contention can be examined if a quantum computer is set to operate at a rate that exceeds the classical limit of one operation per Planck volume-time, or equivalently $2^{491}$ m$^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$. Here we quantify the relation between the logical qubit count and the extent to which classicality is challenged. We argue that 500 logical qubits are sufficient to reject theories confined to a laboratory. We account for the operational cost of computation and communication at all scales up to and including the observable universe, ultimately constrained by a 1600-logical-qubit computer. Remarkably, current plans for commercial quantum computers are projected to surpass this limit, thereby putting the quantum-gravity standoff to the test.
one_liner: Estimates how many logical qubits are needed for a quantum computer to probe whether computation can exceed a classical Planck-scale limit.
why_it_matters: It reframes quantum hardware scale as a testbed for quantum-gravity limits, tying logical-qubit counts to physically testable regimes.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a conjectured classical bound of one operation per Planck volume-time and maps violations of that bound onto the size of a fault-tolerant quantum computer. It argues that around 500 logical qubits would already challenge laboratory-confined classical descriptions, while accounting for communication and operational costs up to cosmological scales sets an upper bound near 1,600 logical qubits. The treatment is resource-oriented, focusing on logical qubit count as the operational variable that determines how strongly classicality is challenged. The result positions future large-scale quantum hardware as an experimental probe of foundational physics constraints.
main_result: It derives logical-qubit thresholds, from roughly 500 to 1,600, at which quantum computation would test classical Planck-scale operational bounds.
application_angle: Although foundational, the analysis gives hardware roadmaps a concrete physical significance by linking scalable logical-qubit counts to experimentally meaningful limits.

title: Inverse design of waveguide grating mode converters using artificial neural networks
abstract: Machine learning techniques, notably various deep neural network methods, are instrumental in processing extensive and intricate data sets in engineering and scientific fields. This paper shows how deep neural networks can inversely design cascaded-mode converting systems, particularly the waveguide gratings that implement selective mode conversion upon reflection. Neural networks can map the grating's physical features to scattering parameters of the modes reflected from the grating. The trained networks can then be utilized to inversely design the gratings based on the desired values of the scattering parameters. The process of the inverse design involves using the technique of gradient descent of a defined loss function. Minimizing this loss function leads to calculating more accurate features fulfilling the desired scattering parameters.
one_liner: Uses neural-network inverse design to generate waveguide grating mode converters from target modal scattering responses.
why_it_matters: It can shorten the design cycle for integrated photonic mode converters, a key component in scalable photonic routing and multiplexing.
detailed_summary: The authors train deep neural networks to learn the mapping from grating geometry parameters to reflected modal scattering parameters in cascaded mode-converting waveguide systems. Once trained, the model is used in inverse mode by gradient descent on a loss defined over desired scattering targets, yielding grating feature values that realize those targets. The approach is aimed at selective mode conversion upon reflection, which is relevant for compact multimode integrated photonics. Technically, it replaces repeated full-wave optimization loops with a differentiable surrogate model for rapid design-space navigation.
main_result: It demonstrates ANN-based inverse design of grating geometries that produce specified reflected-mode scattering characteristics in cascaded converters.
application_angle: The method is useful for practical photonic chip design, especially where fast synthesis of custom mode converters enables denser routing and multiplexed functionality.

title: Directional and correlated optical emission from a waveguide-engineered molecule with local control
abstract: Radiative coupling between quantum emitters leads to a range of spectacular emission phenomena. Dicke studied the foundations of collectively enhanced and suppressed decay, commonly referred to as super- and subradiance. Collective effects can further result in directionality of the emission, thus offering a complimentary implementation of chiral quantum optics. Waveguide quantum electrodynamics (QED) allows coupling between spatially separated emitters, enabling selective driving. In this work, we control the emission direction for a pair of quantum dots embedded in a bidirectional photonic crystal waveguide offering independent electrical tuning. Notably the emitters are 13 \micro m apart, which corresponds to 26 effective wavelengths, but are nevertheless radiatively coupled. The directionality arises from a dispersive dipole-dipole interaction, which shifts the energy of the collective states, so that the emitter pair effectively forms an artificial molecule. We show that the emission direction can be switched from left- to rightwards by manipulating the relative driving phase while collectively exciting the emitters. In addition, we observe directional photon statistics under continuous driving, with, for example, single photons detected on one output port, and photon pairs on the other. With pulsed excitation, both emitters are fully inverted and correlated photon pairs are observed in time-resolved intensity correlation measurements. This work demonstrates a novel implementation of chiral quantum optics using quantum dots coupled via a non-chiral waveguide, and reports key steps for scaling up as a multi-emitter waveguide QED platform.
one_liner: Two electrically tuned quantum dots in a photonic-crystal waveguide show switchable directional emission and port-dependent photon correlations.
why_it_matters: It realizes chiral-like functionality without a chiral waveguide, offering a scalable route to directional quantum-light sources and multi-emitter networks.
detailed_summary: This experiment couples two quantum dots separated by 13 micrometers in a bidirectional photonic-crystal waveguide and tunes them independently with local electrical control. Despite the non-chiral waveguide, radiative coupling and a dispersive dipole-dipole interaction create collective states of an artificial molecule whose emission direction depends on the relative driving phase. Under continuous drive, the two output ports exhibit different photon statistics, including single photons in one direction and photon pairs in the other. Under pulsed excitation, both emitters are inverted and time-resolved measurements show correlated photon-pair generation.
platform_or_system: quantum dots in a photonic-crystal waveguide
main_result: It demonstrates phase-switchable left/right emission and direction-dependent photon statistics from a radiatively coupled two-emitter waveguide-QED system.
application_angle: The platform is promising for on-chip nonreciprocal-style quantum photonic functions such as directional single-photon sources, correlated-pair routing, and scalable emitter networks.

title: Strong nonlocality with more imaginarity and less entanglement
abstract: Complex numbers are central to the formulation of quantum mechanics, yet their role as a genuine resource is only beginning to be understood. In this work, we demonstrate that quantum states with intrinsically complex amplitudes provide a fundamental advantage in state discrimination. We construct a set of five orthogonal three qubit pure states and show that the set is strongly nonlocal if and only if it includes imaginary components. Such a set becomes locally indistinguishable not only under local measurements but also against bipartite joint measurements. This enhanced robustness makes imaginarity a valuable resource for quantum cryptography since information encoded in these states remains secure against collaborative group attacks. Our results highlight a new operational role of complex numbers in quantum theory and establish imaginarity as a key enabler of cryptographic security. However, we reconstruct the set by replacing the only product state with a biseparable state whose shared entanglement between two parties nullifies the effect of imaginarity in exhibiting strong nonlocality. In fact, we show how entangling correlations between two distant parties can dilute the effect of imaginarity, and conversely, how imaginarity itself can mimic the role of entanglement. Nevertheless, the set spans a locally indistinguishable subspace, while its complement, in turn, produces distillable genuine entanglement. Notably, this is the smallest possible Unextendible Biseparable Basis (UBB) that resolves the open problem regarding the existence of a UBB of cardinality $d^2+d-1$ in $d^{\otimes 3}$. Our construction yields a highly powerful set, rich in resources from multiple perspectives of quantum information theory, including many-copy discrimination, unambiguous identification, entanglement creation from product state, and non-entangling perturbations.
one_liner: Finds that intrinsically complex amplitudes enable strong nonlocality in a three-qubit state set even with less entanglement.
why_it_matters: It identifies imaginarity as a cryptographic resource for locally secure encoding against both local and bipartite joint attacks.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs five orthogonal three-qubit pure states and proves that the set is strongly nonlocal exactly when intrinsically imaginary amplitudes are present. In that regime the states are not only locally indistinguishable, but remain indistinguishable even under bipartite joint measurements, which strengthens their security value for distributed encoding. The authors also show that replacing the product state with a biseparable state removes this imaginarity-enabled strong nonlocality, clarifying a tradeoff between entanglement and complex-phase resources. The construction further yields the smallest known unextendible biseparable basis of size d^2+d-1 in d^{imes 3}, resolving an open existence question.
main_result: It proves that imaginary amplitudes are necessary and sufficient for strong nonlocality in the constructed five-state three-qubit ensemble.
application_angle: The result suggests new quantum communication and cryptographic protocols that encode information in imaginarity-driven indistinguishability rather than relying solely on entanglement.

title: High-Dimensional Quantum Photonics: Roadmap
abstract: The field of high-dimensional quantum photonics involves the use of multimode photonic degrees-of-freedom such as the spatial, temporal, or spectral structure of light to encode multi-level quantum states. Recent years have seen rapid progress in the development of methods to generate, manipulate, and distribute such quantum states of light and their use in a range of quantum technology applications that offer practical advantages over conventional qubit-based approaches. High-dimensional quantum states of light encoded in photonic time-bins, frequency-bins, transverse-spatial modes, waveguide paths, and temporal modes have enabled noise-robust fundamental tests of quantum mechanics, error-resilient and high-capacity quantum communication protocols, andas well as efficient approaches for quantum information processing, to name just a few examples. However, research in this field has progressed fairly independently, with little exchange across different photonic degrees-of-freedom or between experiment and theory and no comprehensive comparison between degrees-of-freedom. This roadmap aims to bridge this gap by surveying progress in each area and identifying shared challenges and opportunities that cut across two or more photonic degrees-of-freedoms. We review early work and state-of-the-art experimental techniques under development for high-dimensional quantum states encoded in single and entangled photons, as well as theoretical tools for their measurement and certification. We outline the main outstanding challenges for theory and each experimental degree-of-freedom, identifying promising future directions of research that may enable these to be overcome. We end by discussing interconnections and shared challenges centered around their distribution, measurement, and manipulation, with a view towards their integration into next-generation quantum technology platforms and applications.
one_liner: A roadmap comparing high-dimensional photonic encodings and charting how multimode light can scale quantum communication, sensing, and processing.
why_it_matters: It consolidates fragmented progress across time, frequency, spatial, path, and temporal-mode platforms into a practical agenda for deployable high-dimensional photonic quantum tech.
detailed_summary: This roadmap surveys high-dimensional quantum state generation, manipulation, transmission, and measurement across multiple photonic degrees of freedom, including time bins, frequency bins, transverse spatial modes, waveguide paths, and temporal modes. It compares experimental state of the art for single-photon and entangled-photon implementations with theoretical tools for measurement and certification. A central technical contribution is identifying shared bottlenecks across platforms, especially state distribution, scalable measurement, and cross-platform interoperability. The paper frames these issues in terms of next-generation integrated quantum technology stacks rather than isolated encoding demonstrations.
main_result: The paper provides a cross-platform technical roadmap for high-dimensional quantum photonics, highlighting common methods, limitations, and future directions.
application_angle: It gives a practical design map for higher-capacity quantum links, more noise-robust protocols, and photonic systems that exploit qudits instead of only qubits.

title: Discrete-variable assisted error correction of continuous-variable quantum information
abstract: Robust continuous-variable (CV) quantum information processing requires correcting realistic errors in bosonic systems, but all existing schemes rely on auxiliary Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states which the preparation and operation are demanding in many platforms. In this work, we propose a novel CV quantum error correction (QEC) scheme that utilizes a broadly accessible resource: discrete-variable (DV) ancilla. Our scheme extracts information about CV displacement to the DV ancilla, measuring that allows counteracting the unwanted displacement error. We show that a simple single-qubit ancilla can already suppress CV infidelity by more than 20%. By concatenating with DV QEC codes, our scheme is robust against the physical errors in hybrid CV-DV systems, and yields a new class of oscillator-in-oscillator code that does not involve GKP states. Our work facilitates the implementation of CV QEC on realistic platforms.
one_liner: A hybrid CV-DV error-correction scheme uses simple discrete-variable ancillas to suppress bosonic displacement errors without GKP states.
why_it_matters: Replacing hard-to-prepare GKP resources with qubit ancillas lowers the barrier to practical bosonic error correction in realistic quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a continuous-variable error-correction protocol that maps information about oscillator displacement errors onto a discrete-variable ancilla and uses the ancilla measurement to apply corrective action. They show that even a single-qubit ancilla can reduce CV infidelity by more than 20%, establishing meaningful protection without relying on auxiliary GKP states. The scheme can be concatenated with DV error-correcting codes, yielding a hybrid oscillator-in-oscillator code family that is robust to physical errors in both subsystems. This directly targets experimentally accessible architectures where qubits are easier to prepare and control than nonclassical bosonic grid states.
platform_or_system: Hybrid bosonic mode with qubit ancilla
main_result: A GKP-free hybrid QEC protocol is introduced that uses DV ancillas to correct CV displacement noise and can substantially improve bosonic-state fidelity.
application_angle: The approach could make error-corrected bosonic memories and processors more feasible on superconducting, photonic, or trapped-ion platforms that already support qubit-oscillator coupling.

title: Quantum target ranging with Hetero-Homodyne detection
abstract: Quantum target ranging, which estimates a target position using entangled photon pairs, is known to offer an error-probability advantage over classical ranging strategies. Yet, realizing this advantage in practice remains challenging, as an existing receiver design relies on collective measurements and requires an impractically large number of quantum memories and linear passive components. In this work, we propose the hetero-homodyne receiver, a practically implementable architecture that achieves quantum advantage in target ranging using only local measurements. The receiver requires only one heterodyne setup, a single homodyne setup, and a delay line, making the implementation scalable and experimentally feasible. Our results establish a realistic framework for demonstrating quantum advantage in target ranging and contribute toward practical quantum radar systems.
one_liner: A hetero-homodyne receiver achieves quantum target ranging with only local measurements using one heterodyne, one homodyne, and a delay line.
why_it_matters: It removes the impractical collective-measurement overhead that has blocked experimental demonstrations of quantum-ranging advantage.
detailed_summary: The work revisits entanglement-based target ranging, where quantum correlations can reduce ranging error probability relative to classical methods. Instead of relying on collective receivers requiring many quantum memories and large passive-optics networks, the authors propose a hetero-homodyne architecture based entirely on local measurements. The receiver uses a single heterodyne setup, a single homodyne setup, and a delay line, which significantly simplifies scaling and physical implementation. This makes the predicted quantum advantage far more accessible to near-term optical experiments.
main_result: The paper introduces a practically implementable local-measurement receiver that preserves quantum advantage in target ranging without large collective detection hardware.
application_angle: It offers a credible experimental path toward quantum-enhanced lidar or radar-like ranging systems using standard photonic detection components.

title: Environment-Assisted Decoherence Suppression of Optical Non-Gaussian States
abstract: Optical loss is a common bottleneck in photonic quantum information processing, undermining the quantum advantage over classical approaches. Although several countermeasures, such as quantum distillation and error correction, have been proposed, they typically require experimentally demanding non-Gaussian operations. Here, we demonstrate a Gaussian-only scheme that suppresses loss-induced decoherence for general, unknown optical quantum states. By injecting a squeezed vacuum state into an environment of the loss channel and performing feedforward based on environmental monitoring, the scheme effectively suppresses loss-induced noise. Our programmable loop-based optical circuit allows us to implement the scheme for several types of loss-sensitive non-Gaussian states under various loss conditions for up to five steps, and directly compare the results with the unsuppressed case. Our results show that the scheme consistently mitigates state degradation, preserving higher fidelity and Wigner negativity than without suppression. This approach can be applied to mitigating a broad class of errors in optical systems and extending quantum memory lifetimes. Moreover, it is compatible with other loss-suppression techniques and extendable to physical platforms beyond optics, offering a promising route toward reducing the overhead required for fault-tolerant quantum information processing.
one_liner: A Gaussian-only feedforward scheme suppresses optical-loss decoherence in unknown non-Gaussian states by monitoring the environment.
why_it_matters: Loss mitigation without non-Gaussian correction operations could reduce overhead for photonic quantum memories, communication, and fault-tolerant optical processing.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate decoherence suppression for lossy optical channels by injecting squeezed vacuum into the environment mode and applying feedforward conditioned on environment measurements. The protocol is Gaussian-only yet targets general unknown optical states, including loss-sensitive non-Gaussian states. Using a programmable loop-based optical circuit, they implement up to five successive suppression steps and show improved fidelity and preserved Wigner negativity relative to unsuppressed evolution. The method is compatible with other suppression strategies and is framed as a low-overhead route to extending optical-state lifetimes.
platform_or_system: Loop-based optical circuit with environmental monitoring
main_result: The experiment shows that Gaussian environmental engineering and feedforward can consistently mitigate loss-induced degradation of optical non-Gaussian states.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to longer-lived photonic memories, more robust photonic links, and lower-cost protection layers for optical quantum processors and repeaters.

title: Attosecond quantum spectroscopy with entangled photon pairs
abstract: Bright squeezed light from parametric down-conversion in the infrared (IR) frequency range has triggered the emergence of attosecond quantum optics -- a new research field at the interface of quantum optics, strong-field physics, and attosecond technology. Two challenges arise at this interface: transferring quantum features of the IR light sources to the ultraviolet (UV) and extreme ultraviolet (XUV) frequency range via strong-field nonlinearities, and exploiting quantum optical properties of the nonlinear optical response as a new probe in ultrafast dynamics. Here, we address both by driving high-harmonic generation (HHG) in solids with entangled photon pairs either in degenerate or non-degenerate frequency modes. In the degenerate mode, single-shot measurements of harmonics up to the 10th order reveal strong photon bunching whose $g^{(2)}$ first grows and then decreases with the harmonic order. We show that this behavior tracks different microscopic mechanisms responsible for harmonic emission, demonstrating the potential of attosecond quantum optical spectroscopy. In the non-degenerate case, the harmonics retain quantum-induced correlations, verified by wavelength-resolved second-order cross-correlation maps. Our findings demonstrate transfer of quantum photon correlations into the XUV domain and open a pathway toward quantum-enhanced attosecond spectroscopy and control of ultrafast dynamics in solids.
one_liner: Entangled-photon-pair-driven HHG transfers quantum correlations into the XUV and enables attosecond quantum spectroscopy of solids.
why_it_matters: It links quantum optics with attosecond science, creating a new way to probe ultrafast solid-state dynamics using photon statistics and correlations.
detailed_summary: The paper studies high-harmonic generation in solids driven by entangled photon pairs from infrared parametric down-conversion, addressing whether quantum optical features survive strong-field nonlinear conversion. In the degenerate case, single-shot measurements up to the 10th harmonic show pronounced bunching, with harmonic-order-dependent g^(2) behavior that tracks different microscopic emission mechanisms. In the non-degenerate case, wavelength-resolved cross-correlation maps show that harmonics retain quantum-induced correlations between modes. Together these results establish both quantum-correlation transfer into the XUV and a spectroscopy modality where quantum statistics reveal ultrafast material dynamics.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that entangled-photon correlations survive high-harmonic generation in solids and can be read out in UV/XUV harmonics for attosecond-scale spectroscopy.
application_angle: This could enable quantum-enhanced probes of ultrafast electronic processes and new nonlinear optical metrology tools beyond classical strong-field spectroscopy.

title: Towards National Quantum Communication in Europe: Planning and Sizing Terrestrial QKD Networks
abstract: The European Union is developing the European Quantum Communication Infrastructure (EuroQCI) as a pan-European network to provide secure communication capabilities across Member States, including governmental and critical-infrastructure domains. While the strategic objective is defined at EU level, the required scale and structure of national quantum key distribution (QKD) networks remain largely unspecified. This work addresses the question of how to plan and size national terrestrial QKD networks to support critical infrastructure and public authorities. We propose a reproducible planning methodology that estimates network size, total fiber length, and the number of required QKD components based on a small set of explicit assumptions. The approach is demonstrated for Austria, where a synthetic but structured network model is constructed and evaluated using Monte Carlo simulation. The model focuses on terrestrial QKD infrastructure and explicitly excludes space-based segments. It estimates endpoint counts, trusted repeater node requirements, and hop-length distributions under realistic operational constraints. The Austrian case is then used as a baseline to derive scaling rules for other EU Member States based on population and geographic extent. The results provide first-order planning estimates for national QKD backbone sizes across Europe. These estimates are not intended as deployment designs but as planning-level references that support early-stage cost assessment and infrastructure dimensioning under the EuroQCI framework.
one_liner: A reproducible planning framework sizes terrestrial national QKD networks and derives first-order EuroQCI scaling estimates from an Austrian model.
why_it_matters: It turns high-level quantum-communication policy into network-dimensioning numbers needed for budgeting, infrastructure planning, and deployment sequencing.
detailed_summary: This work develops a methodology for estimating terrestrial national QKD backbone requirements from a compact set of assumptions about endpoints, routing constraints, and trusted-node operation. Using Austria as a structured case study, the authors simulate network size, total fiber length, repeater-node counts, and hop-length distributions via Monte Carlo methods. They then extrapolate planning rules to other EU member states using population and geography as scaling variables. The paper is explicit that these are planning-level references rather than final deployment designs, but it provides a quantitative baseline for infrastructure sizing.
main_result: The paper presents a reproducible sizing method for national terrestrial QKD networks and uses it to generate first-order scaling estimates for European deployments.
application_angle: It is immediately useful for governments and operators evaluating backbone cost, component counts, and rollout scope for secure quantum communication infrastructure.

title: Self-Assembled Telecom Color Centers in Silicon and Their Growth Environment
abstract: Artificial atoms based on color centers in silicon (SiCCs) have recently emerged as promising candidates for highly integrable and scalable key components in photonic quantum technology, including telecom single-photon sources and spin memory devices. A novel all-epitaxial fabrication technique for SiCCs, based on ultra-low-temperature (ULT) molecular beam epitaxy (MBE), addresses limitations of conventional fabrication via ion implantation, such as vertical ion straggle and collateral crystal lattice damage. This method solely relies on self-assembly of SiCCs during kinetically-limited growth of (carbon-doped) Si(:C) at ULTs <~350°C. The latter requires an extraordinary pristine growth environment to prevent unintended defect formation caused by the incorporation of impurities from the background vapor; however, so far, no study has specifically addressed how exactly the vacuum conditions during epitaxy influence SiCC formation, their optical properties, and the quality of the surrounding crystal matrix. Here, we investigate the impact of the growth pressure and the substrate temperature on the self-assembly and photoluminescence (PL) properties of important SiCCs, such as W, G, G', and T centers. Further, we use PL and Doppler broadening variable energy positron annihilation spectroscopy to emphasize the role of the growth pressure in suppressing the luminescence background, which is crucial for advancing quantum photonics applications.
one_liner: This study shows how ultra-clean ultra-low-temperature MBE conditions govern self-assembled telecom silicon color-center formation and optical quality.
why_it_matters: It identifies epitaxial process windows that suppress unwanted defects and background luminescence, directly improving scalable silicon quantum-photonic emitters.
detailed_summary: The paper examines how growth pressure and substrate temperature during ultra-low-temperature molecular beam epitaxy affect self-assembled silicon color centers, including W, G, G', and T centers. Using photoluminescence together with Doppler broadening variable-energy positron annihilation spectroscopy, the authors correlate vacuum cleanliness with defect formation in the surrounding Si(:C) matrix. A key technical result is that lower background impurity incorporation suppresses parasitic luminescence, which is critical for isolating telecom-band single-defect emission.
platform_or_system: Self-assembled silicon color centers in epitaxial Si(:C)
main_result: The authors show that epitaxial growth pressure and temperature strongly control silicon color-center formation and that cleaner growth conditions reduce luminescence background from unintended defects.
application_angle: The work provides a process roadmap for wafer-compatible telecom single-photon and spin-memory devices in silicon photonics.

title: A hardware efficient quantum residual neural network without post-selection
abstract: We propose a hardware efficient quantum residual neural network which implements residual connections through a deterministic linear combination of identity and variational unitaries, enabling fully differentiable training. In contrast to the previous implementation of residual connections, our architecture avoids post-selection while preserving residual learning. Furthermore, we establish trainability of our model, mitigating barren plateaus which are considered as a major limitation of variational quantum learning models. In order to show the working of our model, we report its application to image classification tasks by training it for MNIST, CIFAR, and SARFish datasets, achieving accuracies of 99% and 80% for binary and multi-class classifications, respectively. These accuracies are comparable to previously achieved from the standard variational models, however our model requires 10x fewer gates making it better suited for resource constraint near-term quantum processors. In addition to high accuracies, the proposed architecture also demonstrates adversarial robustness which is another desirable parameter for quantum machine learning models. Overall our architecture offers a new pathway for developing accurate, robust, trainable and hardware efficient quantum machine learning models.
one_liner: A deterministic quantum residual neural network achieves competitive classification with about 10x fewer gates and no post-selection.
why_it_matters: Removing post-selection while preserving trainability and robustness makes quantum ML architectures more realistic for near-term hardware constraints.
detailed_summary: The authors implement residual connections through a deterministic linear combination of the identity and variational unitaries, making the architecture fully differentiable without post-selection overhead. They argue and numerically support improved trainability relative to standard variational models by mitigating barren plateaus. On MNIST, CIFAR, and SARFish tasks, the network reaches 99% binary and 80% multi-class accuracy while using roughly an order of magnitude fewer gates, and it also exhibits adversarial robustness.
main_result: The proposed residual QNN matches standard model accuracy on benchmark image tasks while eliminating post-selection and cutting gate counts by about 10x.
application_angle: This design is better aligned with noisy, resource-limited quantum processors where circuit depth and deterministic operation are major deployment constraints.

title: Telecom C-band single-photon sources with a semiconductor-dielectric microresonator
abstract: Secure communications with quantum key distribution over fiber-optic links is one of the few recognized applications of quantum physics at the level of individual quanta -- single C-band photons. Currently, the widely used sources of such photons are highly attenuated laser pulses, featured by a low probability of single photon occurrence. Here, we present an efficient source with an InAs/GaAs quantum dot on a metamorphic buffer layer inside a micropillar-shaped microcavity. The key innovation is the use of different semiconductor and dielectric materials to form the lower (GaAs/AlGaAs) and upper (Si/SiO$_2$) Bragg reflectors. Compatibility of these materials in a monolithic source is achieved by depositing a small amount of Si/SiO$_2$ pairs on an incomplete micropillar made from a coherent heterostructure grown by molecular beam epitaxy. This design enables resonant excitation with $π$-pulses and generation of polarized photons with a record-breaking end-to-end efficiency of 11%.
one_liner: A hybrid semiconductor-dielectric micropillar delivers resonantly driven telecom C-band single photons with 11% end-to-end efficiency.
why_it_matters: Efficient deterministic C-band emitters are a key missing component for deployable fiber-based quantum networks and QKD systems.
detailed_summary: The device embeds an InAs/GaAs quantum dot on a metamorphic buffer inside a micropillar microcavity that uses asymmetric mirrors: a GaAs/AlGaAs lower Bragg reflector and a Si/SiO2 upper reflector. The hybrid mirror stack is realized by depositing a limited number of dielectric pairs onto an incomplete semiconductor micropillar, enabling monolithic integration despite material mismatch. Under resonant pi-pulse excitation, the source produces polarized telecom-band photons with a reported record 11% end-to-end efficiency.
platform_or_system: InAs/GaAs quantum dot in a hybrid micropillar microcavity
main_result: The authors demonstrate a telecom C-band single-photon source with polarized output and record-breaking 11% end-to-end efficiency under resonant excitation.
application_angle: The source targets direct use in existing low-loss fiber infrastructure, improving practical single-photon generation for metropolitan and long-haul quantum links.

title: Millisecond spin relaxation times of distinct electron and hole subensembles in MA$_x$FA$_{1-x}$PbI$_3$ perovskite crystals
abstract: The unique combination of outstanding optical quality and attractive spin properties opens new avenues for optical spin control in hybrid organic-inorganic perovskite semiconductors. Using the optically detected magnetic resonance technique, we study the spins of electrons and holes in mixed-cation MA$_x$FA$_{1-x}$PbI$_3$ single crystals with $x = 0.4$ and 0.8. Multiple distinct spin subensembles with $g$-factors spanning from 2.9 to 3.6 for electrons and from 0.5 to 1.2 for holes are resolved, revealing diverse localization environments. We measure the longitudinal spin relaxation times, $T_1$, reaching 2 ms and remaining in the $μ$s range even for weakly localized carriers at the cryogenic temperature of 1.6 K. The magnetic-field dependence of $T_1$ is dominated by the random nuclear (Overhauser) fields with strengths of $\sim 0.4-0.8$ mT for electrons and $\sim 4-12$ mT for holes, corresponding to $μ$s-long correlation times of the hyperfine field determined by carrier hopping between shallow localization sites. The temperature dependence of $T_1$ reveals a weak localization potential of the charge carriers and shows a correlation between $T_1$ and the inhomogeneity of the spin ensemble. These results establish mixed-A-site perovskite single crystals as a promising solid-state platform with long-lived spin states for quantum information applications.
one_liner: Mixed-cation lead-halide perovskite crystals host multiple electron and hole spin ensembles with spin-lattice relaxation times up to 2 ms.
why_it_matters: Millisecond-scale spin lifetimes in optically active perovskites make these materials relevant for spin-photon interfaces and magnetic sensing concepts.
detailed_summary: Using optically detected magnetic resonance, the authors resolve several distinct electron and hole subensembles in MAxFA1-xPbI3 single crystals, with electron g-factors from 2.9 to 3.6 and hole g-factors from 0.5 to 1.2. They measure longitudinal relaxation times T1 up to 2 ms at 1.6 K and find microsecond-scale T1 even for weakly localized carriers. The field and temperature dependence indicate that Overhauser fields and carrier hopping between shallow localization sites dominate the spin dynamics, with inferred nuclear-field strengths of about 0.4-0.8 mT for electrons and 4-12 mT for holes.
main_result: The work demonstrates long-lived, spectrally distinguishable electron and hole spin subensembles in perovskite crystals, with T1 reaching 2 ms at cryogenic temperature.
application_angle: These spin properties support the case for perovskites as low-cost, optically addressable materials for quantum spin control and sensitive spin-based measurements.

title: Phase-locked phonon laser enhanced ultra-weak force measurement
abstract: Optically levitated micro- and nanoparticles are an ideal optomechanical platform for precision measurements, particularly enabling the detection of ultraweak forces. Nevertheless, quantum backaction and inherent instabilities induced by the trapping laser fundamentally restrict further improvements in force sensitivity and resolution. To circumvent these bottlenecks, we actively drive the levitated nanoparticle's mechanical motion in a phase-locked phonon laser mode and integrate a carrier-modulation measurement architecture to enhance force sensing capabilities. The stable and high-amplitude oscillation of the phonon laser allows for the robust trapping under 1 mW-level laser power, which in turn reduces the force noise to 4.0(3)*10^-22 N/Hz^1/2. Furthermore, by using phase-locked phonon laser, the measurement system achieves active stabilization and extended coherence time with the measured signal to 12,500 seconds, realizing a measurement resolution of 8(4)*10^-24 N with a sensitivity of 9.3(7)*10^-22 N/Hz^1/2 under a loaded force. These results establish the phonon laser as a low-noise, long-coherence-time, self-stabilizing platform for precision measurements, as well as in quantum and fundamental physics tests.
one_liner: A phase-locked phonon laser in a levitated nanoparticle enables ultra-weak force sensing with 10^-22 N/Hz^1/2 noise and 12,500 s coherence.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical route to lower-noise, longer-coherence optomechanical sensors for weak-force detection and precision fundamental tests.
detailed_summary: The experiment actively drives the motion of an optically levitated micro- or nanoparticle into a phase-locked phonon-laser regime, then reads out forces using a carrier-modulation architecture. Stable high-amplitude oscillation permits trapping at around milliwatt optical power, reducing backaction and technical noise. The system achieves force noise of 4.0(3) x 10^-22 N/Hz^1/2, extends coherence to 12,500 s, and reaches a measured force resolution of 8(4) x 10^-24 N under applied load.
main_result: The authors demonstrate self-stabilized ultra-weak force measurement with long coherence time and sub-10^-23 N resolution using a phase-locked phonon-laser mode.
application_angle: The technique is immediately relevant to next-generation inertial sensors, weak-force metrology, and optomechanical platforms for quantum-limited measurements.

title: Quantum MIMO Channel Modeling in Turbulent Free-Space Optical Links
abstract: Free-space optical (FSO) links supporting spatial multiplexing provide a natural physical realization of Quantum MIMO channels. We develop a first-principles model for Quantum MIMO channels derived directly from wave-optical propagation through three-dimensional atmospheric turbulence. The framework explicitly accounts for intermodal crosstalk, finite detection apertures, and the system-bath separation induced by spatial-mode projection. We distinguish between distinguishable and indistinguishable photon regimes, showing that indistinguishability leads to intrinsically many-body interference effects described by matrix permanents. To obtain a completely positive and trace-preserving logical description, we introduce an erasure-extended encoding in which turbulence-induced leakage and photon loss are mapped to flagged erasure states. The resulting Quantum MIMO channel naturally reduces to a correlated n-qubit erasure channel, with correlations arising from the shared turbulent medium. Limiting regimes in which correlated Pauli channels emerge as effective approximations are also identified.
one_liner: This paper derives a wave-optics-based quantum MIMO channel model for turbulent free-space links, including crosstalk, loss, and many-photon interference.
why_it_matters: A physically grounded channel model is essential for designing spatially multiplexed free-space quantum communication systems and error-mitigation strategies.
detailed_summary: Starting from three-dimensional wave propagation through atmospheric turbulence, the authors build a first-principles model of spatially multiplexed quantum free-space optical links. The framework explicitly includes intermodal crosstalk, finite receive apertures, and system-bath separation induced by spatial-mode projection, and it distinguishes distinguishable from indistinguishable photon regimes. By introducing erasure-extended encoding, they obtain a completely positive trace-preserving logical channel that reduces naturally to a correlated n-qubit erasure model, with correlated Pauli-channel limits in certain regimes.
main_result: The work provides a CPTP logical description of turbulent quantum MIMO links by mapping propagation-induced leakage and loss into correlated erasure channels derived from wave optics.
application_angle: This model can guide coding, receiver design, and architecture choices for high-rate free-space quantum networking with spatial multiplexing.

title: Tensor-network simulation of quantum transport in many-quantum-dot systems
abstract: Transport through correlated nanoscale systems underpins the operation of quantum-dot and molecular-scale devices, yet accurate simulations of large open quantum systems remain computationally challenging as system size increases. Tensor-network methods offer a promising route past this scaling barrier by efficiently compressing quantum states.
