Here we extend a tensor-based solver with a jump-counting estimator that enables direct computation of steady-state electron currents from lead-induced tunneling events. We benchmark the resulting currents against the state-of-the-art master-equation solver QmeQ across a range of lead-dot and inter-dot coupling parameters and find quantitative agreement in the tractable regime. Compared with classical approaches, TJM reduces memory requirements and wall-clock time by orders of magnitude, enabling simulations of interacting quantum-dot arrays far beyond the range accessible to density-matrix-based transport solvers and systematic studies of size-dependent nonequilibrium transport in larger arrays. Our approach allow us to model quantum transport in an array of up to fifty (50) quantum dots.
one_liner: A tensor-jump method simulates steady-state transport in interacting quantum-dot arrays up to 50 dots with major speed and memory gains.
why_it_matters: It pushes nonequilibrium quantum-transport modeling into device sizes inaccessible to standard density-matrix solvers, aiding realistic quantum-dot hardware design.
detailed_summary: The paper extends a tensor-network solver with a jump-counting estimator that extracts steady-state electron current from lead-induced tunneling events. Currents are benchmarked against the QmeQ master-equation package over lead-dot and inter-dot coupling regimes, showing quantitative agreement where both methods are tractable. The tensor-jump method then reaches much larger interacting arrays, with orders-of-magnitude reductions in memory use and runtime relative to density-matrix approaches. This enables systematic studies of size-dependent nonequilibrium transport in arrays as large as 50 quantum dots.
platform_or_system: Interacting quantum-dot arrays coupled to leads
main_result: The authors demonstrate accurate steady-state current simulations for open, interacting quantum-dot arrays up to 50 dots using a tensor-network jump-counting approach.
application_angle: Scalable transport simulation is directly useful for designing and optimizing quantum-dot and molecular-scale electronic devices where correlations and open-system effects matter.

title: Nanobeam Laser Cavities with High Quality-factor and Near-Unity Outcoupling Efficiency
abstract: Cavities with high quality (Q) factor and small mode-volume are crucial to realize high performance nanolasers suitable for optical interconnects. In this work, we propose a novel one-dimensional photonic crystal nanobeam cavity design with fins for controlled electron-injection into the active region. An effective optimization algorithm using first-order perturbation theory of quasinormal modes is implemented and shown to strongly enhance the cavity quality factor. The one-dimensional geometry of the cavity lends itself to unidirectional coupling of the resonant mode into the waveguide by introducing asymmetry of the mirror. The resulting design is shown to achieve high extraction efficiencies ($ >90\% $) while maintaining a high Q-factor ($ > 10 \cdot 10^3$). Through an analysis of the cavity's decay channels, we find that the introduced asymmetry induces unexpected interactions between the cavity's decay channels. Passive InP cavities are fabricated and experimentally characterized, demonstrating record high quality factors exceeding $170 \cdot 10^3$ for designs without fins and up to $70 \cdot 10^3$ for designs with fins, confirming the efficacy of the optimization method and quality of the fabrication process.
one_liner: An optimized nanobeam cavity design achieves high Q and near-unity outcoupling for compact nanolasers and optical interconnects.
why_it_matters: It addresses the usual tradeoff between confinement and extraction, enabling more practical on-chip light sources with efficient waveguide coupling.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a one-dimensional photonic-crystal nanobeam cavity with fins for controlled electron injection and uses first-order quasinormal-mode perturbation theory for optimization. By asymmetrizing the mirrors, the resonant mode is funneled preferentially into a waveguide while retaining strong confinement. Simulations indicate extraction efficiencies above 90% with Q factors above 10,000, and fabricated passive InP devices reach Q above 170,000 without fins and up to 70,000 with fins. The study also analyzes how asymmetry modifies and couples the cavity decay channels.
main_result: The design combines >90% outcoupling efficiency with high Q, and experiments validate record-high Q factors in fabricated passive InP nanobeam cavities.
application_angle: Such cavities are well suited to energy-efficient nanolasers and chip-scale optical interconnects that need both low threshold and efficient output routing.

title: Microresonators induced at the optical fiber intersections
abstract: A widely tunable free spectral range (FSR) is essential for many optical microresonator applications, but achieving it remains a significant challenge. Recently, it has been experimentally demonstrated that side-coupling between two optical fibers can induce a high-Q whispering-gallery-mode (WGM) microresonator. In contrast to broadly explored monolithic optical microresonators, this configuration enables extensive tuning of the microresonator FSR through fiber bending, tilting, and twisting. Beyond fundamental interest, this class of microresonators is particularly important for a range of critical applications, including tunable delay lines, frequency comb generators, and reconfigurable optical sensors. Here, we develop the theory of such microresonators, which has remained largely unexplored. We consider weakly twisted fibers, whose geometry can be decomposed into tilting and bending. We show that an extremely small curvature of fibers critically affects the shape and spectrum of the induced microresonators. We discuss the physical origin of this curvature and show that taking it into account leads to excellent agreement between the developed theory and the experimental results.
one_liner: A theory of fiber-intersection microresonators explains how tiny curvature controls spectrum and tunable free spectral range.
why_it_matters: It gives a design framework for highly reconfigurable resonators that can support delay lines, comb generation, and optical sensing.
detailed_summary: This paper develops the theory of high-Q microresonators induced by side-coupling at optical-fiber intersections, a geometry distinct from monolithic resonators. The analysis treats weakly twisted fibers in terms of tilt and bend and shows that extremely small curvature strongly reshapes the effective resonator and its spectral response. By identifying the physical origin of the curvature and incorporating it into the model, the authors obtain excellent agreement with experiment. The framework clarifies how bending, tilting, and twisting can widely tune the free spectral range.
main_result: The authors show that minute fiber curvature is a decisive control parameter for the geometry and spectrum of fiber-intersection microresonators and that their theory matches experiments closely.
application_angle: Mechanically reconfigurable fiber microresonators could enable tunable delay elements, adaptable comb sources, and field-deployable optical sensors without complex fabrication.

title: Scalable on-chip integration of diamond color centers for cryogenic quantum photonics
abstract: Chip integration of quantum emitters is a crucial milestone for scalable quantum photonic information processing. Among optically active defect centers for quantum photonics, diamond color centers are promising because of their long spin coherence times and high photon emission rates. However, for a coherent-photon emission, they typically require a cryogenic environment to protect optical coherence from thermal phonons, which makes chip integration challenging. In this paper, we develop a chip-integrated diamond photonic crystal cavity embedding an ensemble of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers. We confirm cryogenic operation by observing Purcell enhancement of NV-center emission via an edge-coupled optical fiber. This result demonstrates successful integration of diamond color centers, a photonic crystal cavity, and an optical waveguide-fiber package, representing a key step toward scalable diamond-based quantum communication platforms.
one_liner: Diamond NV centers are integrated with a cryogenic photonic crystal cavity and fiber-coupled waveguide on chip, showing Purcell-enhanced emission.
why_it_matters: It is a concrete packaging and integration step toward scalable cryogenic quantum-photonic nodes based on diamond color centers.
detailed_summary: The authors build a chip-integrated diamond photonic crystal cavity containing an ensemble of nitrogen-vacancy centers and interface it to an edge-coupled optical fiber through an on-chip waveguide. Under cryogenic operation, they observe Purcell enhancement of NV-center emission, confirming cavity-coupled coherent-photon-compatible functionality in the integrated device. The work combines emitter, cavity, waveguide, and fiber packaging in a single platform rather than treating these as separate components. This addresses a key systems-integration challenge for diamond quantum photonics.
platform_or_system: Cryogenic diamond NV-center photonic crystal cavity chip
main_result: The device demonstrates cryogenic Purcell-enhanced NV-center emission in a fully integrated diamond cavity-waveguide-fiber architecture.
application_angle: Integrated, fiber-coupled diamond emitters are directly relevant to scalable quantum-network nodes, photon sources, and cryogenic quantum-photonic interconnects.

title: A Simple and Robust Balanced Homodyne Detector for High-Repetition-Rate Pulsed Sources
abstract: We design and experimentally characterize a balanced homodyne detector optimized for high-repetition-rate (100 MHz) pulsed optical sources. Unlike conventional transimpedance-amplifier architectures, which suffer from nonlinearities and dynamic instabilities with ultrashort pulses, our approach allows to directly amplify the photocurrent extracted at the common photodiode node without feedback loops. A theoretical model describing the detector response, noise, and pulse-to-pulse correlations is developed, providing quantitative predictions for the signal variance, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and inter-pulse correlations. Implemented with two matched InGaAs photodiodes illuminated by a 1030 nm mode-locked laser at 100 MHz, the detector exhibits excellent linearity and shot-noise-limited scaling of the signal variance with optical power. Optimizing the temporal integration window yields a maximum SNR of about 14 dB, while correlation measurements confirm negligible inter-pulse correlations. These results demonstrate that the proposed architecture offers a robust and simple solution for high-speed pulsed homodyne detection, suitable for quantum optics and continuous-variable quantum information applications.
one_liner: A simple balanced homodyne detector reaches shot-noise-limited 100 MHz pulsed operation with negligible pulse-to-pulse correlations.
why_it_matters: Reliable high-rate homodyne readout is a core building block for continuous-variable quantum optics, metrology, and fast photonic experiments.
detailed_summary: The detector avoids the usual transimpedance-feedback architecture and instead directly amplifies photocurrent taken from the common node of two matched photodiodes. A quantitative model is developed for the detector response, noise, signal-to-noise ratio, and inter-pulse correlations under pulsed excitation. Using a 1030 nm mode-locked laser at 100 MHz with InGaAs photodiodes, the experiment shows linear response, shot-noise-limited variance scaling, and a peak SNR around 14 dB after optimizing the integration window. Correlation measurements indicate negligible memory between pulses.
platform_or_system: 100 MHz pulsed InGaAs balanced homodyne detector
main_result: The authors demonstrate a robust feedback-free homodyne architecture with shot-noise-limited performance and negligible inter-pulse correlations at 100 MHz.
application_angle: The design simplifies deployment of fast homodyne receivers for continuous-variable quantum information, squeezed-light measurements, and high-repetition-rate quantum optics.

title: Complete coherent control of spin qubits in self-assembled InAs quantum dots under oblique magnetic fields
abstract: We demonstrate complete coherent control of a single spin qubit confined in a self-assembled InAs negatively charged quantum dot subjected to an Oblique magnetic field, and directly compare this regime with the conventional Voigt geometry. In the Oblique-field configuration, the groundstate spin eigenstates are found to be unequal superpositions of the bare electron spin, with their composition tunable via the orientation of the applied field. This tunable spin mixing provides an additional degree of freedom to engineer the spin basis and associated optical couplings in the charged quantum dot system. Although this geometry has a distinct structure with important implications, it provides a regime in which we can fully and coherently control the tailored spin qubit. We observe Rabi oscillations and Ramsey fringes, and demonstrate arbitrary single-qubit rotations, enabling a direct comparison with the Voigt case. Our results establish that spin-qubit control does not necessarily require a pure Voigt geometry and can instead be achieved under Oblique magnetic fields. This relaxes constraints on device and field alignment and offers a versatile route to design and optimize quantum information processing architectures in semiconductor quantum dots.
one_liner: Single-spin qubits in self-assembled InAs quantum dots are fully controlled under oblique magnetic fields, not just standard Voigt geometry.
why_it_matters: It relaxes alignment constraints and adds a tunable spin-mixing knob for engineering semiconductor spin-photon interfaces and qubit control.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a negatively charged self-assembled InAs quantum dot in an oblique magnetic field, where the spin eigenstates become unequal superpositions of bare electron-spin states. By tuning field orientation, the spin mixing and optical couplings can be engineered while preserving full coherent control. Experimentally, the authors observe Rabi oscillations, Ramsey fringes, and arbitrary single-qubit rotations, enabling direct comparison with the conventional Voigt-field case. The results show that high-fidelity control is possible without requiring a purely transverse field geometry.
platform_or_system: Single-electron spin in a self-assembled InAs quantum dot
main_result: The work demonstrates complete coherent single-qubit control of a charged InAs quantum-dot spin under oblique magnetic fields, including arbitrary rotations.
application_angle: More flexible magnetic-field geometries simplify device integration and can improve the practicality of semiconductor spin-photon hardware for quantum processors and networked nodes.

title: Postquantum steering in scenarios with multiple characterised parties
abstract: The study of stronger-than-quantum phenomena (i.e., postquantum) has enabled a deeper understanding of the scope of quantum theory. Much is known about the case of correlations in Bell scenarios, where the device-independent framework allowed us to explore its possibilities independently of the formalism of quantum theory. However, less is known about the phenomenon of Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen steering. Here, the `characterised parties' are assumed to describe their systems locally through the quantum formalism, which inconveniences a theory-independent description. In addition, a theorem by Gisin and Hughston, Josza and Wootters further hindered the discovery of the phenomenon. The study of postquantum steering, initiated about a decade ago, has been quite fruitful, including: the development of mathematical formalisms that frame the effect, resource theories that quantify it as a resource, and activation protocols that relate it to Bell correlations. However, all these results have a limitation in common: they apply to scenarios with only one quantum party. Here we articulate the concept of postquantum steering for scenarios with multiple quantum parties, bringing in the missing piece to the puzzle. We provide an algorithm to certify postquantumness, which in some cases also certifies quantumness. We also define a hierarchy of semidefinite programs that bounds the set of quantum assemblages from the outside. Moreover, we show that the study of postquantum steering is fundamentally relevant since it is not just a mere mathematical curiosity allowed by the no-signalling principle, but it may arise within compositional theories beyond quantum theory. Our work further discovers a peculiarity of steering: its theory-independent description fundamentally prevents a direct connection with Bell nonlocality -- e.g., nonclassical Bell correlations do not imply nonclassical steering.
one_liner: This work extends postquantum EPR steering to scenarios with multiple characterized quantum parties and gives algorithms to certify it.
why_it_matters: It closes a major gap in steering theory and provides computational tools for testing nonquantum behavior in multipartite semi-device-independent settings.
detailed_summary: The paper generalizes the notion of postquantum steering beyond the previously studied single-quantum-party case to scenarios with multiple characterized parties. It introduces an algorithm for certifying postquantumness and, in some instances, quantumness, together with an outer-approximation hierarchy of semidefinite programs for the quantum assemblage set. The authors also show that postquantum steering can arise in compositional theories beyond quantum theory and that Bell nonclassicality does not directly imply steering nonclassicality in a theory-independent description.
platform_or_system: multipartite steering scenarios with characterized quantum parties
main_result: The authors provide the first certification framework and SDP hierarchy for postquantum steering with multiple quantum parties.
application_angle: These tools are relevant for semi-device-independent verification protocols where certifying physically allowed correlations is important for quantum networked sensing and information tasks.

title: Quantum coherent transceivers toward Holevo-limited communications
abstract: The Holevo limit bounds the channel capacity of a communication channel in which information is encoded in quantum states in a Hilbert space at the transmitter and decoded using quantum measurements at the receiver. Saturating the Holevo limit requires quantum-limited transceivers that either generate quantum states of light or employ quantum-limited measurements. Here, we demonstrate an integrated photonic-electronic quantum-limited coherent receiver (QRX) achieving 14.0 dB shot noise clearance (SNC), 520 $μ$W knee power, 2.57 GHz 3-dB bandwidth, 3.50 GHz shot-noise-limited bandwidth, and 90.2 dB common-mode rejection ratio ($\mathrm{CMRR}$). We scale this design to a 32-channel QRX array with median 26.6 dB $\mathrm{SNC}$, and automatic $\mathrm{CMRR}$ correction yielding a median 76.8 dB $\mathrm{CMRR}$ at minimum. Using the integrated QRX and fiber-optic transmitter, we measure $0.15\pm0.01$ dB of squeezing below the shot noise limit, limited by off-chip losses. We propose a squeezed light communication scheme that can surpass the Shannon limit, with a path toward the Holevo limit.
one_liner: An integrated quantum-limited coherent receiver and 32-channel array show shot-noise-limited performance toward Holevo-limited optical communications.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates hardware-level ingredients for practical squeezed-light links that can exceed Shannon-limited classical receivers and approach ultimate quantum channel capacity.
detailed_summary: The authors realize an integrated photonic-electronic quantum receiver with 14.0 dB shot-noise clearance, 520 uW knee power, 2.57 GHz 3-dB bandwidth, 3.50 GHz shot-noise-limited bandwidth, and 90.2 dB CMRR. They then scale the architecture to a 32-channel receiver array, obtaining a median 26.6 dB shot-noise clearance and median corrected CMRR of at least 76.8 dB. Using the receiver with a fiber-optic transmitter, they directly measure 0.15 plus/minus 0.01 dB squeezing below shot noise, and outline a communication scheme using squeezed light to surpass the Shannon limit.
main_result: The work demonstrates a scalable quantum-limited coherent receiver platform and validates sub-shot-noise operation needed for squeezed-light communication.
application_angle: This is a concrete transceiver path for high-capacity fiber links and quantum-enhanced classical communication operating closer to the Holevo bound.

title: Continuous-variable two-dimensional cluster states in the microwave domain
abstract: We demonstrate the experimental realization of two-dimensional, continuous variable (CV) cluster states between 191 microwave frequency modes. This result is obtained by exposing vacuum fluctuations to the input of a Josephson Parametric Amplifier, parametrically pumped by a sum of coherent tones around twice its resonant frequency. By carefully tuning pump frequencies, amplitudes, and phases we engineer the interference between mixing products and realize honeycomb and square lattice CV cluster states with three and four pump tones respectively. We prove the presence of the cluster states with a suitable nullifier test, reaching up to $-1.2$ dB of squeezing of the cluster state's nullifiers. We study hidden entanglement (HE) and show no hidden entanglement up to $\sim -1$ dB of squeezing and negligible HE at optimal squeezing.
one_liner: A Josephson parametric amplifier generates 2D continuous-variable cluster states across 191 microwave modes in honeycomb and square lattices.
why_it_matters: Large multimode microwave cluster states are a key resource for measurement-based continuous-variable quantum computing on superconducting hardware.
detailed_summary: Vacuum fluctuations injected into a Josephson Parametric Amplifier are processed by multi-tone parametric pumping near twice the resonant frequency to engineer interference among mixing products. With three and four pump tones, the experiment realizes honeycomb and square-lattice continuous-variable cluster states spanning 191 microwave frequency modes. Nullifier measurements verify the cluster-state structure with up to -1.2 dB nullifier squeezing, and analysis of hidden entanglement finds none up to about -1 dB and negligible contributions at optimal squeezing.
main_result: The experiment creates and verifies 191-mode two-dimensional continuous-variable cluster states in the microwave domain.
application_angle: The result advances scalable resource-state generation for superconducting continuous-variable processors and microwave quantum networking architectures.

title: Symmetry-Engineered Magnetic Dipole Emission in Plasmonic Core-Satellite Resonators
abstract: Magnetic dipole (MD) transitions are intrinsically weak and highly sensitive to emitter orientation and position, making their controlled enhancement at optical frequencies particularly challenging. Here we show that structural symmetry provides a powerful route to robust magnetic light-matter interactions. We systematically investigate plasmonic core-satellite resonators composed of N metallic nanoparticles arranged on a dielectric core. We evaluate their performance using a unified figure of merit that accounts for magnetic Purcell enhancement, electric dipole suppression, quantum efficiency, and robustness to emitter orientation and fabrication tolerances. We find that the optimal structures correspond to the highest-symmetry geometries, which naturally produce spatially homogeneous and orientation-independent magnetic Purcell enhancement. In particular, the dodecapod configuration yields strong magnetic emission with Purcell factors approaching 250, high radiative efficiency, and suppressed electric dipole contributions. Quasinormal-mode and complex mode-volume analysis reveal that symmetry enforces uniform magnetic modal confinement within the core, explaining both the enhancement and its robustness. These results establish symmetry as a guiding principle for designing nanophotonic resonators with controlled multipolar light-matter interactions and provide a practical route toward bright and selective magnetic dipole emitters.
one_liner: High-symmetry plasmonic core-satellite resonators strongly and robustly enhance optical magnetic-dipole emission while suppressing electric-dipole channels.
why_it_matters: Reliable enhancement of weak magnetic transitions would enable brighter, more selective nanophotonic emitters for spectroscopy, quantum optics, and optical interfaces.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes core-satellite resonators made of N metallic nanoparticles on a dielectric core using a figure of merit that combines magnetic Purcell enhancement, electric-dipole suppression, quantum efficiency, and robustness. The highest-symmetry geometries perform best, with the dodecapod configuration reaching magnetic Purcell factors near 250 while maintaining high radiative efficiency and reduced electric-dipole contribution. Quasinormal-mode and complex mode-volume analysis attribute this to symmetry-enforced, spatially uniform magnetic modal confinement inside the core.
main_result: The paper identifies symmetry-engineered dodecapod resonators as robust platforms for strong, orientation-insensitive magnetic-dipole emission enhancement.
application_angle: Such resonators could improve magnetic-transition-based single-photon sources, optical antennas, and selective emitter interfaces for nanophotonic communication hardware.

title: Robust and High-Fidelity Controlled Two-Qubit Gates via Asymmetric Parallel Resonant Excitation
abstract: Implementing high-fidelity controlled two-qubit gates in dipole-dipole interacting systems, such as rare-earth-ion crystals, in hindered by spectral inhomogeneity and weak coupling. Existing method often rely on detuned pulses, making them susceptible to frequency errors and AC Stark shifts. We propose a robust resonant scheme for arbitrary controlled two-qubit gates that utilizes asymmetric excitation and pulse engineering to achieve decoupled, parallel qubit control. Simulations on rare-earth-ion ensemble qubits demonstrate gate fidelities exceeding 99% within a 170 kHz detuning range with off-resonant excitation below 0.2%. This approach offers a robust, scalable route for quantum computing in spectrally crowded systems.
one_liner: An asymmetric parallel resonant-excitation scheme enables robust arbitrary controlled two-qubit gates in spectrally crowded dipole-coupled systems.
why_it_matters: It targets a core bottleneck in rare-earth and similar hardware by reducing sensitivity to detuning errors and AC Stark shifts while keeping gate fidelity high.
detailed_summary: The proposal replaces detuned control strategies with a resonant scheme based on asymmetric excitation and pulse engineering, allowing decoupled parallel control of interacting qubits. In simulations of rare-earth-ion ensemble qubits, the method achieves gate fidelities above 99% over a 170 kHz detuning range while keeping off-resonant excitation below 0.2%. The approach is designed to remain effective in the presence of spectral inhomogeneity and weak dipole-dipole interactions.
main_result: The scheme delivers simulated arbitrary controlled two-qubit gates above 99% fidelity with strong robustness to detuning in dipole-coupled ensembles.
application_angle: This improves the practicality of scalable solid-state quantum processors built from spectrally crowded rare-earth platforms and related hardware.

title: Defect-free arrays at the thousand-atom scale in a 4-K cryogenic environment
abstract: We report on a cryogenic platform at 4 K incorporating high numerical aperture optics for the generation of large-scale tweezers arrays, and compatible with Rydberg-state manipulation. We achieve trapping lifetimes of around 5000 s, significantly extending the available experimental time for the preparation of large-scale arrays. By combining two trapping lasers at different wavelengths and by minimizing other atom losses during the rearrangement and imaging processes, we demonstrate the preparation of defect-free arrays with up to 1024 atoms. Our cryogenic design opens exciting prospects for analog and digital quantum computing.
one_liner: A 4 K cryogenic tweezer platform produces defect-free neutral-atom arrays with up to 1024 atoms and hour-scale trap lifetimes.
why_it_matters: Combining thousand-atom scale with long lifetime and Rydberg compatibility addresses a major systems challenge for useful neutral-atom quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The platform integrates high-NA optics inside a 4 K cryogenic environment to support large optical tweezer arrays and Rydberg-state manipulation. Trap lifetimes reach about 5000 s, greatly extending the time budget for loading, rearrangement, and computation. By using two trapping wavelengths and minimizing losses during rearrangement and imaging, the experiment assembles defect-free arrays containing as many as 1024 atoms.
main_result: The authors demonstrate defect-free neutral-atom arrays of up to 1024 atoms with roughly 5000 s trapping lifetime in a cryogenic setup.
application_angle: This is a strong systems-level step toward large analog simulators and digital neutral-atom quantum computers with lower loss and more reliable preparation.

title: Fock State Generation and SWAP using a Rabi-Driven Qubit
abstract: The deterministic generation and SWAP of Fock states in isolated high-Q modes form a core foundation for architectures in bosonic quantum computing. Conventionally, these operations necessitate strong coupling to a qubit, which inherently compromises the required cavity isolation. To address this trade-off, we introduce a tunable mechanism wherein a weakly coupled qubit, which preserves mode isolation, is driven to induce a strong interaction on demand. By leveraging a Rabi-driven, qubit-mediated sideband interaction, we realize on-demand Jaynes-Cummings coupling between a transmon and a long-lived cavity mode. Using a superconducting flute cavity with two high-Q modes, we deterministically demonstrate Fock state preparation up to n=5 at operation times of less than 2 microseconds per photon. We also demonstrate and characterize single-photon SWAP in approximately 2 microseconds. Finally, we adapt our SWAP method to generate a dual-rail Bell state. While current performance is constrained by baseline coherence rather than fundamental methodological limits, the protocol scales inherently to accommodate higher photon numbers and faster operational regimes. By enabling complex operations on modes that remain strictly weakly coupled to qubits, this approach establishes a robust pathway for advancing scalable bosonic quantum computing.
one_liner: A Rabi-driven weakly coupled transmon enables fast on-demand Fock-state creation and photon SWAPs in long-lived superconducting cavity modes.
why_it_matters: It removes the usual tradeoff between strong qubit coupling and cavity coherence, which is central for scalable bosonic quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The work engineers an on-demand Jaynes-Cummings interaction between a weakly coupled transmon and a long-lived cavity mode by Rabi driving the qubit to create a qubit-mediated sideband coupling. In a superconducting flute cavity with two high-Q modes, the authors deterministically prepare Fock states up to n=5 with sub-2 microsecond per-photon operation times. They also perform a single-photon SWAP in about 2 microseconds and adapt the protocol to generate a dual-rail Bell state. The reported limitations are set by current coherence rather than the method itself, suggesting headroom for faster and higher-photon-number operation.
platform_or_system: transmon coupled to high-Q superconducting flute cavity
main_result: The paper demonstrates deterministic cavity Fock-state generation up to five photons and single-photon SWAP operations using a driven weak-coupling architecture.
application_angle: This approach supports bosonic processors that keep memories highly isolated while still performing fast control, a practical requirement for hardware-scalable logical qubits and photonic encodings.

title: TurPy: a physics-based and differentiable optical turbulence simulator for algorithmic development and system optimization
abstract: Developing optical systems for free-space applications requires simulation tools that accurately capture turbulence-induced wavefront distortions and support gradient-based optimization. Here we introduce TurPy, a GPU-accelerated, fully differentiable wave optics turbulence simulator to bridge high fidelity simulation with end-to-end optical system design. TurPy incorporates subharmonic phase screen generation, autoregressive temporal evolution, and an automated screen placement routine balancing Fourier aliasing constraints and weak-turbulence approximations into a unified, user-ready framework. Because TurPy's phase screen generation is parameterized through a media-specific power spectral density, the framework extends to atmospheric, oceanic, and biological propagation environments with minimal modification. We validate TurPy against established atmospheric turbulence theory by matching 2nd order Gaussian beam broadening and 4th order plane wave scintillation to closed-form models with 98% accuracy across weak to strong turbulence regimes, requiring only the medium's refractive index structure constant and power spectral density as inputs. To demonstrate TurPy as a gradient-based training platform, we optimize a dual-domain diffractive deep neural network (D2NN) in a two-mask dual-domain architecture to recover a Gaussian beam from a weakly turbulent path and achieving over 20x reduction in scintillation relative to an uncompensated receiver in simulation. TurPy is released as an open-source package to support synthetic data generation, turbulence-informed algorithm development, and the end-to-end design of optical platforms operating in turbulent environments.
one_liner: TurPy is a differentiable GPU turbulence simulator that matches theory and enables end-to-end optimization of free-space optical systems.
why_it_matters: It gives researchers a practical tool to co-design optics and learning-based compensation for turbulent channels in sensing and optical communication.
detailed_summary: TurPy combines subharmonic phase-screen synthesis, autoregressive temporal evolution, and automated screen placement into a differentiable wave-optics framework. The simulator is parameterized by medium-specific power spectral density, allowing atmospheric, oceanic, and biological propagation models with the same core machinery. Validation against closed-form turbulence theory shows 98% agreement for second-order Gaussian beam broadening and fourth-order plane-wave scintillation across weak and strong regimes. As a design example, the authors optimize a two-mask dual-domain diffractive neural network to reconstruct a Gaussian beam after weak turbulence and reduce scintillation by more than 20x in simulation.
main_result: The paper delivers an open-source differentiable turbulence simulator validated to 98% accuracy and shows gradient-based optical compensation with over 20x scintillation reduction in simulation.
application_angle: It is directly useful for designing and training robust free-space links, imagers, and adaptive optical receivers under realistic turbulent propagation.

title: Quantifying and detecting quantum-state texture
abstract: Quantum-state texture is a recently proposed quantum resource that characterizes the inhomogeneity of a quantum state's matrix element distribution in the computational basis, enriching our understanding of quantum state structure. To expand its quantification toolkit and establish detection methods, in this article, we investigate the resource theory of texture from both quantitative and detection perspectives. First, we construct a texture measure $\mathcal{T}^{\text{GR}}_{α,z}(ρ)$ based on the $α$-$z$ Rényi relative entropy and present some of its inherent properties. Second, we analyze the mathematical relationships between several existing texture measures, revealing connections among different quantifiers. Finally, drawing on the witness concept from other resource theories, we systematically introduce texture witnesses into the texture theory and provide examples of texture witnesses with special properties.
one_liner: This paper builds new measures and witness methods for quantum-state texture, a resource describing basis-space inhomogeneity of quantum states.
why_it_matters: It formalizes how to quantify and detect a newly proposed state resource, which could inform future structure-aware photonic state analysis.
detailed_summary: The authors define a texture quantifier based on the alpha-z Renyi relative entropy, denoted T^(GR)_{alpha,z}(rho), and analyze its basic resource-theoretic properties. They also derive mathematical relations among several existing texture measures to clarify how different quantifiers compare. In addition, they introduce texture witnesses as operational detection tools and provide explicit examples with special properties. The work is primarily theoretical but supplies a structured toolbox for certifying nonuniform matrix-element patterns in a chosen computational basis.
main_result: The paper establishes a Renyi-relative-entropy texture measure and a witness framework for detecting quantum-state texture.
application_angle: These tools could support experimental state certification workflows when basis-dependent structure matters, including engineered photonic state preparation and verification.

title: Physics-Informed Discrete-Event Simulation of Polarization-Encoded Quantum Networks
abstract: We extend the SeQUeNCe discrete-event simulator with physics-based models for polarization-encoded photonic quantum networks. Our framework integrates Jones-calculus optical components, including an SPDC Bell-state source, wave plates, and polarizing beam splitters, together with a multi-section fiber model capturing polarization mode dispersion, chromatic dispersion, and Raman noise from coexisting classical traffic. We validate the simulator by reproducing experimentally reported spectra, polarization correlations, quantum state tomography, and dispersion- and Raman-induced noise. The resulting platform enables hardware-parameterized prediction of entanglement distribution performance under realistic deployment conditions.
one_liner: A physics-informed extension of SeQUeNCe models polarization-encoded quantum networks with realistic optics, fiber impairments, and classical-traffic noise.
why_it_matters: It closes the gap between idealized network simulation and deployable quantum-link engineering for entanglement distribution over real fiber infrastructure.
detailed_summary: The framework adds Jones-calculus models of polarization optics, including an SPDC Bell-pair source, wave plates, and polarizing beam splitters, to the SeQUeNCe discrete-event simulator. It also incorporates a multi-section fiber channel model with polarization-mode dispersion, chromatic dispersion, and Raman noise from co-propagating classical traffic. Validation reproduces reported spectra, polarization correlations, tomography results, and impairment-driven noise signatures. This creates a hardware-parameterized simulator for predicting entanglement distribution performance under realistic deployment conditions.
main_result: The paper demonstrates a validated discrete-event simulator that captures realistic polarization-network physics and predicts entanglement performance in impaired fiber links.
application_angle: Network designers can use it to size components, assess coexistence with classical traffic, and optimize practical quantum communication deployments before field testing.

title: Rotation of the Transition Dipole in Single hBN Quantum Emitters via Vibronic Coupling
abstract: The design of polarization-encoded quantum interfaces relies on the assumption that solid-state emitters possess static transition dipoles defined by the host lattice symmetry. Here, we demonstrate the vibronic breakdown of this static dipole approximation in hexagonal boron nitride quantum emitters. Through high-resolution energy-resolved spectroscopy, we reveal a continuous, spectral rotation of the emission dipole orientation reaching up to 40$^{\circ}$, driven by coupling to the phonon bath. This spectral gradient is significantly suppressed at cryogenic temperatures (6 K), identifying thermally activated lattice vibrations as the primary driver of the dipole reorientation. First-principles calculations on two representative defect types indicate the microscopic origin of this phenomenon as a coordinate-dependent transition dipole, where phonon-induced atomic displacements fundamentally perturb the electronic wavefunctions. By comparing the distinct defect environments, we demonstrate that the magnitude of the polarization rotation scales with the strength of the vibronic coupling. Our results not only identify a fundamental limit for polarization fidelity in solid-state quantum networks but also suggest a new class of strain-tunable quantum photonic devices based on vibronic dipole reorientation.
one_liner: Single hBN emitters show spectrally varying dipole orientation, revealing vibronic rotation that can reach about 40 degrees.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete mechanism limiting polarization fidelity in solid-state quantum interfaces while suggesting new strain- and phonon-engineered device concepts.
detailed_summary: Using high-resolution energy-resolved spectroscopy, the study finds that the emission dipole orientation of single hexagonal boron nitride quantum emitters rotates continuously across the spectrum by up to 40 degrees. The effect is strongly reduced at 6 K, indicating that thermally activated phonons drive the reorientation. First-principles calculations on two defect classes attribute the behavior to a coordinate-dependent transition dipole caused by phonon-induced atomic displacements that perturb the electronic wavefunctions. The magnitude of the rotation scales with vibronic coupling strength across different defect environments.
platform_or_system: single quantum emitters in hexagonal boron nitride
main_result: The paper shows that vibronic coupling causes large, spectrally dependent transition-dipole rotation in hBN emitters, breaking the static-dipole approximation.
application_angle: This matters for polarization-encoded quantum networking and could be exploited for tunable quantum photonic devices based on strain or phonon control.

title: Observation of genuine $2+1$D string dynamics in a U$(1)$ lattice gauge theory with a tunable plaquette term on a trapped-ion quantum computer
abstract: Quantum simulations of high-energy physics in $2+1$D can probe dynamical phenomena nonexistent in one spatial dimension and access regimes that are challenging for existing classical simulation methods. For string dynamics -- relevant to hadronization -- a plaquette term is required to realize genuine $2+1$D behavior, as it endows the gauge field with dynamics and enables the propagation of photon-like excitations. Here, we realize a U$(1)$ quantum link model of quantum electrodynamics in two spatial dimensions with a tunable plaquette term on a \texttt{Quantinuum System Model H2} quantum computer. We implement, to our knowledge, the largest quantum simulation of string-breaking dynamics reported to date, on a $5 \times 4$ matter-site square lattice using $51$ qubits. The simulation uses a shallow circuit design with a two-qubit gate depth of $28$ per Trotter step and up to $1540$ entangling gates. Starting from far-from-equilibrium string configurations, we measure the probability for the string to propagate within the lattice plane and find signatures of genuine $2+1$D dynamics only when the plaquette term is present. In a resonant regime, we observe the annihilation of string segments accompanied by the production of electron--positron pairs that screen them. We further find that, only with a nonzero plaquette term, matter creation extends across the lattice plane rather than remaining confined to the initial string path. These results experimentally realize string breaking and demonstrate the emergence of dynamical gauge fields in two spatial dimensions, establishing a route to photon-like propagation in programmable quantum simulators of gauge theories.
one_liner: A 51-qubit trapped-ion processor realizes 2+1D U(1) lattice gauge dynamics with tunable plaquette interactions and string breaking.
why_it_matters: It pushes programmable quantum hardware into larger-scale nontrivial gauge-theory dynamics that are difficult for classical methods and relevant to high-energy physics simulation.
detailed_summary: The experiment implements a 2+1D U(1) quantum link model with a tunable plaquette term on the Quantinuum System Model H2 trapped-ion computer. It simulates far-from-equilibrium string dynamics on a 5x4 matter-site lattice using 51 qubits, a two-qubit gate depth of 28 per Trotter step, and up to 1540 entangling gates. With a nonzero plaquette term, the data show genuine 2D string propagation and matter creation spreading across the lattice plane rather than remaining on the initial string path. In a resonant regime, the authors observe string-segment annihilation accompanied by electron-positron pair production that screens the string.
main_result: The paper experimentally observes genuine 2+1D string dynamics and string breaking in a U(1) lattice gauge theory with dynamical plaquette interactions.
application_angle: It demonstrates that current trapped-ion hardware can tackle classically hard gauge-dynamics problems, advancing quantum processors as useful simulators for quantum field theories.

title: Ten-Second Electron-Spin Coherence in Isotopically Engineered Diamond
abstract: Solid-state spin defects are a promising platform for quantum networks. A key requirement is to combine long ground-state spin-coherence times with a coherent optical transition for spin-photon entanglement. Here, we investigate the spin and optical coherence of single nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centres in (111)-grown isotopically engineered diamond. Our diamond-growth process yields a precisely controlled $^{13}\mathrm{C}$ concentration and low-ppb nitrogen concentrations. Combined with the mitigation of 50 Hz noise using a real-time feedforward scheme and tailored decoupling sequences, this enables record defect-electron-spin coherence times of $T_2 = 6.8(1)$ ms for a Hahn echo and of $T_2^{DD} = 11.2(8)$ s under dynamical decoupling. In addition, we observe coherent optical transitions with a near-lifetime-limited homogeneous linewidth of 16.9(4) MHz and characterize the spectral diffusion dynamics. These results provide new avenues to investigate the incorporation of impurities in diamond and new opportunities for improved spin-qubit control for quantum networks and other quantum technologies.
one_liner: Isotopically engineered (111) diamond NV centers reach over 11 s electron-spin coherence while retaining near-lifetime-limited optical transitions.
why_it_matters: It directly improves the spin-memory and spin-photon interface needed for scalable quantum networks, repeaters, and precision solid-state sensing.
detailed_summary: The work studies single NV centers in (111)-grown isotopically engineered diamond with controlled 13C content and low-ppb nitrogen background. Using real-time 50 Hz noise feedforward plus tailored dynamical decoupling, the authors measure a Hahn-echo coherence time of 6.8 ms and a decoupled coherence time of 11.2 s. They also observe coherent optical transitions with a 16.9 MHz homogeneous linewidth, close to the lifetime limit, and characterize spectral diffusion. The combined spin and optical performance is unusually strong for network-grade solid-state qubits.
platform_or_system: single NV centers in isotopically engineered diamond
main_result: Single NV centers show 11.2 s dynamical-decoupled electron-spin coherence together with near-lifetime-limited optical linewidths.
application_angle: Long-lived spins with coherent optical access are directly relevant to spin-photon entanglement, quantum repeater nodes, and robust diamond quantum sensors.

title: When is randomization advantageous in quantum simulation?
abstract: We study the regimes in which Hamiltonian simulation benefits from randomization. We introduce a sparse-QSVT construction based on composite stochastic decompositions, where dominant terms are treated deterministically and smaller contributions are sampled stochastically. Crucially, we analyze how stochastic and approximation errors propagate through block-encoding and QSVT procedures. To benchmark this approach, we construct ensembles of random Hamiltonians with controlled coefficient dispersion, locality, and number of terms, designed to favor randomization, and therefore providing an upper bound on its practical advantage. For Hamiltonians with many terms and highly inhomogeneous coefficient distributions, randomized methods reduce gate counts by up to an order of magnitude. However, this advantage is confined to moderate-precision regimes: as the target error decreases, deterministic methods become more efficient, with a crossover near $\varepsilon \sim 10^{-3}$. Although this regime partially overlaps with quantum chemistry Hamiltonians, realistic systems exhibit additional structure, such as commutation patterns, not captured by our model, which are expected to further favor deterministic approaches.
one_liner: A stochastic sparse-QSVT framework identifies when randomized Hamiltonian simulation actually lowers gate counts.
why_it_matters: It gives concrete operating regimes for simulation compilers, showing when randomization helps and when deterministic methods win at higher precision.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a composite stochastic decomposition for sparse-QSVT, treating large Hamiltonian terms deterministically while sampling weaker terms. It tracks both stochastic sampling error and approximation error through block-encoding and QSVT, which is the key technical contribution. On random Hamiltonian ensembles with many terms and highly uneven coefficients, randomized methods cut gate counts by up to an order of magnitude. That advantage disappears as target precision tightens, with a crossover around epsilon about 1e-3 where deterministic methods become preferable.
main_result: Randomized simulation can reduce gate counts substantially for broad, inhomogeneous Hamiltonians, but only in moderate-precision regimes.
application_angle: The results inform practical quantum algorithm design by indicating when randomized compilation is worth using for chemistry or many-term simulation workloads.

title: Operational criteria for quantum advantage in latency-constrained nonlocal games
abstract: Remote entanglement enables coordinated decision making without communication and produces correlations beyond those achievable by any classical strategy, representing a practical quantum advantage in time-critical distributed decision-making problems. However, existing analyses of quantum-classical gaps in such latency-constrained tacit coordination (LCTC) have focused on idealized models that neglect the finite stationary window of the LCTC, finite operation times, and limited entanglement generation rates, leaving fundamental constraints unaccounted for. In this work, we develop a comprehensive framework to quantitatively analyze quantum advantage in LCTC that explicitly incorporates finite-duration and finite-rate operations, as well as generalized utility structures with a limited stationary window. These advances are made possible by adapting statistical certification methods for nonlocal games to the decision-making scenarios of LCTC, identifying operational criteria that must be satisfied by the hardware implementations to realize quantum advantage with sufficient statistical significance. To meet the stringent criteria, we propose time-multiplexed, event-ready operations of cavity-assisted trapped-atom quantum network nodes that provide a continuous stream of entangled qubit pairs, with decision latencies of a microsecond and decision rates of $8\times 10^3~\text{s}^{-1}$ per channel for a representative metropolitan-scale $50$-km fiber network to keep up with the fast-changing environment, such as financial markets and electric grid networks. These results bridge the gap between the theoretical notions of the quantum-classical gap in nonlocal games and concrete implementations that meet the stringent operational criteria for achieving robust quantum advantage in realistic coordination tasks.
one_liner: This work derives hardware-level criteria for statistically significant quantum advantage in latency-constrained nonlocal decision tasks.
why_it_matters: It connects Bell-type quantum advantage claims to concrete network rates, latencies, and entanglement-generation requirements for real deployments.
detailed_summary: The authors build a framework for latency-constrained tacit coordination that includes finite stationary windows, nonzero operation durations, and finite entanglement generation rates. They adapt statistical certification tools from nonlocal games to define operational criteria that hardware must satisfy to demonstrate advantage with significance. As a candidate implementation, they propose time-multiplexed event-ready cavity-assisted trapped-atom nodes delivering entangled pairs continuously over a representative 50 km metropolitan fiber link. The projected performance is microsecond decision latency and about 8x10^3 decisions per second per channel.
main_result: The paper specifies quantitative latency and rate thresholds for realizing certifiable quantum advantage and maps them onto a feasible trapped-atom network architecture.
application_angle: It provides an implementation path for quantum-enabled coordination in fast distributed settings such as grid control, markets, and metropolitan networked systems.

title: Free-space quasi-phase matched second harmonic generation in crystalline quartz
abstract: We report experimental results on second-harmonic generation in a z-cut quartz crystal under conditions of free-space quasi-phase matching in a multi-pass cell. In a 62-pass configuration, an efficiency of 0.027% or 1.4x10-4 %/MW/cm2 was achieved, delivering 1 uJ of the second harmonic at 3.7 mJ pump pulse. This corresponds to an enhancement factor of more than 1000 in conversion efficiency as compared to a single pass. The generated second-harmonic beam demonstrates high beam quality M2=1.1 and linear polarization. The scaling of the output power with the number of passes is in good agreement with the calculated values. Further increasing the pump intensity, number of passes, and amount of plates opens the way to scaling the conversion efficiency to values on the order of tens of percent.
one_liner: A 62-pass free-space quasi-phase-matched quartz setup boosts second-harmonic conversion by more than three orders of magnitude.
why_it_matters: It points to a simple route for scaling nonlinear frequency conversion in robust crystalline quartz without conventional periodic poling.
detailed_summary: The experiment demonstrates free-space quasi-phase-matched second-harmonic generation in z-cut quartz using a multi-pass cell. In a 62-pass geometry, the authors obtain 0.027% conversion efficiency, corresponding to 1 microjoule of second-harmonic output for a 3.7 mJ pump pulse. The efficiency is enhanced by more than 1000 relative to a single pass, while preserving high beam quality with M2 = 1.1 and linear polarization. Measured scaling with pass number agrees with calculation, and the authors outline parameter increases that could push efficiency toward the tens-of-percent range.
main_result: Free-space quasi-phase matching in a 62-pass quartz cell yields >1000x enhancement in second-harmonic efficiency versus single-pass operation.
application_angle: Efficient, high-quality wavelength conversion in a simple solid crystal platform is useful for photonic sources, laser engineering, and free-space optical systems.

title: Annular beams for reliable intersatellite optical communications
abstract: Free-space optical communications (FSOC) are a key enabling technology for future high-capacity space-based networks. Particularly, the backbone of global communication relies on intersatellite optical links. In a previous study, the authors proposed a method to mitigate the impact of transmitter pointing jitter by using a superposition of orthogonally polarized Gaussian and higher-order Laguerre-Gaussian (LG) beams. In this study, we experimentally characterize the proposed system using a spiral phase plate (SPP) to generate higher-order annular beams. We demonstrate that such superpositions can be reliably generated in a realistic optical setup, quantify the associated beam-shaping errors and losses, and assess their impact on intersatellite optical communication performance. It is found that the proposed beam-shaping approach can still yield power savings on the order of 20% compared to a conventional Gaussian beam under the considered conditions.
one_liner: Annular beam superpositions generated with a spiral phase plate cut pointing-jitter penalties in intersatellite optical links.
why_it_matters: It experimentally validates a beam-shaping method that can improve FSOC link robustness and save transmitter power in space networks.
detailed_summary: The paper experimentally tests a previously proposed free-space optical communication scheme based on superposing orthogonally polarized Gaussian and higher-order Laguerre-Gaussian modes. Using a spiral phase plate in a realistic optical setup, the authors quantify beam-shaping errors, insertion losses, and their effect on intersatellite link performance. Despite practical imperfections, the annular-beam approach remains reliable and provides about 20% power savings relative to a standard Gaussian beam under the studied conditions. The result supports structured-light mitigation of transmitter pointing jitter in space links.
platform_or_system: structured annular beams for intersatellite FSOC
main_result: Experimentally generated annular beam superpositions retain a communication advantage, yielding roughly 20% power savings under pointing-jitter-limited conditions.
application_angle: The method can reduce power budgets and improve reliability for high-capacity satellite-to-satellite optical backbone networks.

title: Dynamics of Transverse Spin and Longitudinal Fields of Cylindrical Vector Beams in Optically Active Media
abstract: Due to the inhomogeneous polarisation across the beam profile, cylindrical vector beams interact with optically active media in a complex manner. Here, we analyse evolution of polarisation of cylindrical vector beams propagating in an isotropic optically-active medium. After identifying polarisation normal modes of three-dimensional electromagnetic fields, we predict periodic inter-conversion between azimuthally- and radially-polarised modes of the beams accompanied by rotation of the transverse optical spin and pulsing field during the propagation. Theory and simulations are validated by experimental observations. The observed effects maybe important for imaging in biological chiral media, enhanced chiral sensing and enantioselective spectroscopy, nonlinear optics in chiral media, and generally enhanced spin-orbit coupling and nanoscale vector field engineering.
one_liner: Cylindrical vector beams in optically active media show periodic radial-azimuthal conversion and rotating transverse spin.
why_it_matters: It reveals controllable vector-field dynamics in chiral media that could enhance imaging, chiral sensing, and vectorial light-matter interactions.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze and experimentally verify the propagation of cylindrical vector beams through isotropic optically active media. By identifying the polarization normal modes of full three-dimensional fields, they predict periodic conversion between azimuthal and radial polarization states. This evolution is accompanied by rotation of the transverse optical spin and pulsing longitudinal field components during propagation. The combined theory, simulation, and experiment establish a controllable mechanism for spin-orbit and vector-field engineering in chiral environments.
platform_or_system: cylindrical vector beams in optically active media
main_result: Propagation through an optically active medium drives periodic radial-azimuthal mode conversion together with rotating transverse spin and longitudinal-field dynamics.
application_angle: These controllable chiral-field effects are relevant for bio-imaging, enantioselective spectroscopy, and higher-sensitivity optical sensing in complex media.

title: Quantum Frequency Resolved Optical Gating of Few-Cycle Squeezed Vacuum
abstract: Offering terahertz of bandwidths and femtosecond timescales, ultrafast optics is enabling both the study of fundamental quantum optical phenomena and the advancement of quantum-enhanced applications. However, unlocking the full potential of ultrafast quantum optics requires accessing the temporal characteristics of ultrashort quantum pulses across ultrabroad bandwidths. This is particularly important in the near-infrared and visible range of the optical spectrum, which, unlike the terahertz and long-wave infrared, has remained beyond the reach of current techniques. Here, we break this barrier by translating frequency-resolved optical gating (FROG), a widely used technique for ultrafast classical pulse characterization, to the quantum regime. We show how such a quantum FROG can measure complex temporal modes and sub-optical-cycle quadrature covariances in the near-infrared, enabling complete characterization of microscopic Gaussian states. We experimentally use the quantum-FROG to report the measurement of quadrature correlations, complex temporal modes, and squeezing levels of multimode ultrafast squeezed vacuum states generated on a nanophotonic chip. We access multimode squeezing levels of a femtosecond quantum pulse approaching 7 dB and demonstrate FROG-based measurement bandwidths exceeding 100 THz. Quantum FROG enables measurement of previously inaccessible quantum features of ultrashort pulses at the sub-optical-cycle regime and highlights a practical path to accessing terahertz of bandwidths in quantum optics for applications in computing, sensing, and imaging.
one_liner: A quantum version of FROG characterizes femtosecond squeezed vacuum with sub-cycle temporal resolution over more than 100 THz bandwidth.
why_it_matters: It gives ultrafast quantum optics a practical state-characterization tool for broadband squeezed light needed in photonic computing, sensing, and imaging.
detailed_summary: The paper translates frequency-resolved optical gating into the quantum regime to recover complex temporal modes and sub-optical-cycle quadrature covariances of microscopic Gaussian states. Experimentally, the authors apply it to multimode femtosecond squeezed vacuum generated on a nanophotonic chip, measuring quadrature correlations, modal structure, and squeezing. They report multimode squeezing approaching 7 dB and measurement bandwidths above 100 THz in the near-infrared. The result extends ultrafast quantum-state metrology into a spectral and temporal regime that standard techniques could not access.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates quantum-FROG measurement of multimode squeezed vacuum with nearly 7 dB squeezing and over 100 THz characterization bandwidth.
application_angle: Broadband temporal-state readout of ultrashort quantum pulses can directly support scalable photonic quantum processors and ultrafast quantum-enhanced sensors or imagers.

title: Exponential quantum advantage in processing massive classical data
abstract: Broadly applicable quantum advantage, particularly in classical data processing and machine learning, has been a fundamental open problem. In this work, we prove that a small quantum computer of polylogarithmic size can perform large-scale classification and dimension reduction on massive classical data by processing samples on the fly, whereas any classical machine achieving the same prediction performance requires exponentially larger size. Furthermore, classical machines that are exponentially larger yet below the required size need superpolynomially more samples and time. We validate these quantum advantages in real-world applications, including single-cell RNA sequencing and movie review sentiment analysis, demonstrating four to six orders of magnitude reduction in size with fewer than 60 logical qubits. These quantum advantages are enabled by quantum oracle sketching, an algorithm for accessing the classical world in quantum superposition using only random classical data samples. Combined with classical shadows, our algorithm circumvents the data loading and readout bottleneck to construct succinct classical models from massive classical data, a task provably impossible for any classical machine that is not exponentially larger than the quantum machine. These quantum advantages persist even when classical machines are granted unlimited time or if BPP=BQP, and rely only on the correctness of quantum mechanics. Together, our results establish machine learning on classical data as a broad and natural domain of quantum advantage and a fundamental test of quantum mechanics at the complexity frontier.
one_liner: The authors prove a polylog-size quantum computer can classify and compress massive classical datasets with exponential size advantage over classical machines.
why_it_matters: If realized with fewer than 60 logical qubits, this would define a concrete, application-facing route to quantum advantage on mainstream data tasks.
detailed_summary: The work proves that a quantum device of polylogarithmic size can perform classification and dimension reduction on streaming classical samples, while any equally accurate classical model requires exponentially larger size. The approach combines quantum oracle sketching with classical shadows to avoid standard data-loading and readout bottlenecks. The paper claims this separation persists even if classical algorithms have unlimited runtime or under assumptions such as BPP=BQP. Real-world validation is reported on single-cell RNA-seq and sentiment-analysis tasks, with four to six orders of magnitude size reduction using fewer than 60 logical qubits.
main_result: The main result is a provable exponential memory-size advantage for quantum processing of massive classical data, with application examples fitting within sub-60-logical-qubit hardware.
application_angle: This frames near-term fault-tolerant quantum hardware as potentially useful for high-value data analytics without requiring very large logical processors.

title: Controllable Chirality Sorting of Particles via Topological Optical Quasiparticles
abstract: The manipulation and sorting of chiral nanoparticles are of fundamental importance in multidisciplinary fields ranging from biochemistry to nanophotonics. In this study, we propose a novel and controllable chirality sorting mechanism for continuous particle separation using focused topological optical quasiparticles. Specifically, we investigate the sorting dynamics driven by tight-focused optical skyrmions and bimerons consisting of tailored spatial modes. By highly focusing free-space topological structure light fields, we generate intricate non-paraxial focal fields with tailored intensity and topological polarization textures. The sorting dynamics are systematically evaluated under the dipole approximation for fused silica nanoparticles. Our analytical calculation demonstrate that optical forces exert opposite directional pushes on particles of opposite chiralities, enabling highly efficient spatial separation. Notably, we demonstrate that this sorting process is controllable; by tuning the topological charges, the sorting distance can be flexibly tailored and expanded. The dynamic sorting process in customized topological structures introduces a promising new paradigm for tunable, wide-range chirality sorting of micro- and nano-particles.
one_liner: Focused optical skyrmions and bimerons are proposed to continuously sort chiral nanoparticles with tunable separation distance.
why_it_matters: It offers a controllable optical route to enantiomer-sensitive particle handling, relevant to biochemical analysis and nanophotonic manipulation.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes chirality-dependent optical forces produced by tightly focused topological optical quasiparticles built from tailored spatial modes. In the dipole approximation for fused-silica nanoparticles, opposite chiralities experience forces in opposite directions, enabling continuous spatial separation. The focal fields are nonparaxial and carry engineered polarization-topology textures derived from skyrmion and bimeron structures. By tuning the topological charges, the predicted sorting distance can be adjusted and expanded in a controlled way.
platform_or_system: focused topological light fields for chiral nanoparticles
main_result: The paper shows analytically that optical skyrmion and bimeron focal fields can exert opposite directional forces on opposite chiralities, enabling tunable separation.
application_angle: A reconfigurable all-optical chirality sorter could be useful for lab-on-chip separation, nanoscale manipulation, and enantiomer-sensitive photonic processing.

title: Ghost imaging with zero photons
abstract: Ghost imaging was first demonstrated with entangled photon pairs and well-known for its peculiar properties. The signal beam that illuminates the object possesses no spatial resolution, whereas the reference beam, which never interacts with the object, is spatially resolved. Either beam alone cannot retrieve the image, which can only be obtained when the signal and reference beams are correlated. Here we will report a ghost imaging experiment with even more peculiar properties, in which the image can be reconstructed when no photon interacts with the object or even no photon in neither signal nor reference beam. All the photons interacted with the object are discarded. Only the time bins with zero photon are employed to retrieve the image, a process referred to as "ghost imaging with zero photons" hereafter. The reason why ghost image can be retrieved with zero photons is jointly determined by photon-number projection measurement and photon statistics of thermal light. The results are helpful to resolve the debate on the physics of ghost imaging and understand the relation between quantum and classical correlations.
one_liner: Ghost images are reconstructed using only zero-photon time bins, even when photons that hit the object are discarded.
why_it_matters: The result sharpens the physics of ghost imaging and suggests low-exposure imaging modes based on correlations and measurement conditioning rather than detected flux.
detailed_summary: The experiment reconstructs a ghost image from thermal-light correlations while using only time bins in which zero photons are registered. Photons that interacted with the object are explicitly discarded, and image recovery relies on photon-number projection together with the photon statistics of thermal light. Either arm alone remains insufficient to retrieve the image, so the reconstruction still depends on correlated measurements across the setup. The work provides a technically sharp testbed for distinguishing how classical and quantum correlation mechanisms contribute in ghost imaging.
main_result: The main result is an experimental demonstration that conditioned zero-photon events alone can recover a ghost image after discarding all object-interacting photons.
application_angle: This could inform ultra-low-dose or covert imaging protocols where minimizing illumination or detected photons is operationally important.

title: Inverse Laplace and Mellin integral transforms modified for use in quantum communications
abstract: Integral transformations are useful mathematical tool to work out signals and wave-packets in electronic devices. They may be used in software protocols. Necessary knowledge may come from quantum field theory, in particular from quantum chromodynamics, in which the optic theorem and the renormalization group equation can be solved by a unique contour integral written in two different "dual" ways related between themselves by a complex map in the complex plane of Mellin variable. The inverse integral transformation should be modified to be applied for these contour integral solutions. These modified inverse transformations may be used in security protocols for quantum computers. Here we do a brief review of the basic integral transforms and propose their modification for the extended domains.
one_liner: This paper proposes modified inverse Laplace and Mellin transforms for extended-domain signal processing in quantum communication protocols.
why_it_matters: It suggests mathematical tools for waveform and protocol analysis, though the contribution appears more foundational than experimentally validated.
detailed_summary: The article reviews standard integral transforms and introduces modified inverse Laplace and Mellin constructions motivated by contour-integral techniques from quantum field theory. The proposed changes target extended domains relevant to signals and wave-packets in electronic or software-based communication contexts. The framing links dual contour representations through a complex Mellin-plane map and suggests possible use in quantum-computing security protocols. The contribution is primarily mathematical and conceptual rather than an implemented communication system demonstration.
platform_or_system: mathematical signal-processing framework for quantum comms
main_result: The main result is a proposed modification of inverse Laplace and Mellin transforms intended for extended-domain use in quantum communication settings.
application_angle: If developed further, these transforms could support protocol analysis or signal reconstruction methods in software-defined quantum communication and security stacks.

title: Complexity phase transition for continuous-variable cluster state
abstract: Continuous-variable (CV) cluster states offer a promising platform for large-scale measurement-based quantum computations (MBQC). However, finite squeezing inevitably introduces Gaussian noise during MBQC. While fault-tolerant MBQC schemes exist in principle, they require the scalable incorporation of non-Gaussian resources, such as GKP states, which remain experimentally challenging. Consequently, a central question at this stage is how finite squeezing fundamentally constrains the intrinsic computational power of CV cluster states themselves. In this work, we address this question by analyzing the classical complexity of measurement-based linear optics (MBLO) implemented with such states, motivated by its near-term feasibility and recent experimental progress. We develop an explicit MBLO framework and examine how the squeezing level governs the complexity of the classical simulation of the resulting output states. Specifically, we identify squeezing-level thresholds that delineate classically tractable and intractable regimes, thereby revealing a squeezing-driven complexity phase transition. These findings advance our understanding of the squeezing resources necessary for meaningful quantum computation in current experimental regimes. Furthermore, they underscore the critical need to either scale the squeezing level or integrate error-correction schemes to achieve reliable, large-scale quantum computation with CV cluster states.
one_liner: Finite squeezing induces a complexity phase transition in measurement-based linear optics on continuous-variable cluster states.
why_it_matters: It quantifies when today's imperfect CV photonic resources remain classically simulable versus when they can support genuinely hard quantum photonic computations.
detailed_summary: The paper studies measurement-based linear optics implemented on continuous-variable cluster states under realistic finite squeezing. It develops an explicit MBLO framework and analyzes how squeezing-dependent Gaussian noise changes the classical simulability of output states. The authors identify thresholds in squeezing that separate classically tractable and intractable regimes, establishing a squeezing-driven complexity phase transition. This directly connects an experimentally tunable photonic resource to the computational power of near-term CV measurement-based quantum computing.
main_result: The work identifies squeezing thresholds that mark a transition between classically simulable and classically hard regimes for CV-cluster-state MBLO.
application_angle: These thresholds give experimentalists concrete resource targets for useful CV photonic computing and clarify when higher squeezing or error correction becomes mandatory.

title: Analysis of State Teleportation using Noisy Quantum Gates
abstract: Noise is a major challenge in quantum computing, affecting the reliability of quantum protocols. In this work, we analytically study the impact of various noise processes, such as depolarization, bit flip, and phase flip, on the quantum state teleportation protocol. Each noise process is modeled as a quantum channel and is applied individually to all qubits after the corresponding unitary operations to simulate realistic conditions. We evaluate the fidelity between the ideal and noisy teleported states to quantify the effect of noise. Our analysis shows that the fidelity decreases polynomially, in general, as the noise strength increases for all noise types, highlighting the sensitivity of state teleportation to different noise mechanisms. However, in the low noise regime, the fidelity decreases only linearly, indicating the robustness of the teleportation protocol. These results provide insight into error characterization and can inform strategies for noise mitigation in practical quantum computing applications.
one_liner: Analytical noise-channel study quantifies how imperfect gates degrade quantum teleportation fidelity across common error models.
why_it_matters: It gives protocol-level error sensitivity trends that can guide teleportation benchmarking and noise-mitigation design in practical quantum communication links.
detailed_summary: The paper models depolarizing, bit-flip, and phase-flip noise as quantum channels inserted after the unitary steps of the standard state-teleportation circuit. It derives the resulting fidelity between ideal and teleported states as a function of noise strength and shows a generally polynomial fidelity decay as noise increases. In the low-noise regime, the degradation is approximately linear, indicating limited robustness before higher-order effects dominate. The analysis isolates how different gate-level error mechanisms propagate through the teleportation workflow.
