main_result: The authors show that teleportation fidelity falls polynomially with noise strength overall, but only linearly in the low-noise regime for the studied error channels.
application_angle: This can inform error budgeting and mitigation strategies for near-term teleportation-based quantum networking and distributed computing experiments.

title: Hardware-Aware Quantum Support Vector Machines
abstract: Deploying quantum machine learning algorithms on near-term quantum hardware requires circuits that respect device-specific gate sets, connectivity constraints, and noise characteristics. We present a hardware-aware Neural Architecture Search (NAS) approach for designing quantum feature maps that are natively executable on IBM quantum processors without transpilation overhead. Using genetic algorithms to evolve circuit architectures constrained to IBM Torino native gates (ECR, RZ, SX, X), we demonstrate that automated architecture search can discover quantum Support Vector Machine (QSVM) feature maps achieving competitive performance while guaranteeing hardware compatibility. Evaluated on the UCI Breast Cancer Wisconsin dataset, our hardware-aware NAS discovers a 12-gate circuit using exclusively IBM native gates (6 ECR, 3 SX, 3 RZ) that achieves 91.23 % accuracy on 10 qubits-matching unconstrained gate search while requiring zero transpilation. This represents a 27 percentage point improvement over hand-crafted quantum feature maps (64 % accuracy) and approaches the classical RBF SVM baseline (93 %). We show that removing architectural constraints (fixed RZ placement) within hardware-aware search yields 3.5 percentage point gains, and that 100 % native gate usage eliminates decomposition errors that plague universal gate compilations. Our work demonstrates that hardware-aware NAS makes quantum kernel methods practically deployable on current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices, with circuit architectures ready for immediate execution without modification.
one_liner: A hardware-aware NAS method finds IBM-native QSVM feature maps that match unconstrained performance without transpilation.
why_it_matters: It directly addresses NISQ deployability by co-designing quantum ML circuits around native gates, connectivity, and noise rather than idealized abstractions.
detailed_summary: The work uses a genetic-algorithm neural architecture search to evolve QSVM feature maps constrained to IBM Torino native operations: ECR, RZ, SX, and X. On the UCI Breast Cancer Wisconsin dataset, the search finds a 12-gate, 10-qubit circuit composed entirely of native gates that reaches 91.23% accuracy with zero transpilation overhead. This matches unconstrained gate-search performance and significantly outperforms hand-designed feature maps reported at 64%. The authors also show that relaxing some architectural constraints within the hardware-aware search improves performance further, underscoring the value of device-specific co-optimization.
main_result: The method discovers a fully native 12-gate QSVM feature map achieving 91.23% accuracy while eliminating transpilation and decomposition errors.
application_angle: It provides an immediately executable path for running quantum kernel methods on real superconducting hardware with less compilation overhead and fewer implementation mismatches.

title: Fast and Coherent Transfer of Atomic Qubits in Optical Tweezers using Fiber Array Architecture
abstract: Programmable neutral-atom arrays offer a promising route toward scalable quantum computing, where coherent qubit transfer enables non-local connectivity and reduces resource overhead. However, transfer speed and motional heating remain key bottlenecks for fast and deep quantum circuits. Here, we employ a fiber array neutral-atom quantum computing architecture with site-resolved control of trap depths to realize smooth amplitude exchange between static and moving traps, thereby enabling fast and coherent qubit transfer with ultralow motional heating. With a 10 $μ$s in situ transfer between static and moving traps, we obtain a per-cycle heating rate of 0.156(9) $μ$K, sustain over 500 cycles with negligible atom loss, and achieve a quantum state fidelity of 0.99992(5) per cycle. For inter-site transfer between two separated static traps, the operation takes 120 $μ$s with 0.783(17) $μ$K heating per transfer, and remains negligible atom loss for up to 100 repeated cycles with a fidelity of 0.9998(1) per transfer. Furthermore, through experimental studies of parallel transfer, we establish a model that elucidates the relationship between array inhomogeneity and the transfer heating rate. This fast, low-heating coherent transfer capability provides a practical route for improving both speed and fidelity in atom-shuttling based quantum computing.
one_liner: Fiber-array optical tweezers enable ultralow-heating, high-fidelity neutral-atom qubit transfer at microsecond timescales.
why_it_matters: Fast, coherent atom shuttling is a key bottleneck for scalable neutral-atom processors, especially when nonlocal connectivity is needed.
detailed_summary: Using a fiber-array neutral-atom architecture with site-resolved trap-depth control, the authors implement smooth amplitude exchange between static and moving tweezers. They demonstrate in situ transfer in 10 microseconds with a per-cycle heating rate of 0.156(9) microkelvin and state fidelity of 0.99992(5), sustaining more than 500 cycles with negligible atom loss. For transfer between separated static traps, they achieve 120-microsecond operation time, 0.783(17) microkelvin heating, and 0.9998(1) fidelity per transfer over 100 repeated cycles. Parallel-transfer measurements are used to build a model linking array inhomogeneity to heating.
main_result: The experiment achieves repeated coherent atom transfer with sub-microkelvin heating and per-cycle fidelities up to 0.99992 in a neutral-atom tweezer array.
application_angle: This capability can reduce routing overhead and improve circuit depth, speed, and connectivity in atom-shuttling quantum computing architectures.

title: A Review of Variational Quantum Algorithms: Insights into Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing
abstract: Variational quantum algorithms (VQAs) have established themselves as a central computational paradigm in the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era. By coupling parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) with classical optimization, they operate effectively under strict hardware limitations. However, as quantum architectures transition toward early fault-tolerant (EFT) and ultimate fault-tolerant (FT) regimes, the foundational principles and long-term viability of VQAs require systematic reassessment. This review offers an insightful analysis of VQAs and their progression toward the fault-tolerant regime. We deconstruct the core algorithmic framework by examining ansatz design and classical optimization strategies, including cost function formulation, gradient computation, and optimizer selection. Concurrently, we evaluate critical training bottlenecks, notably barren plateaus (BPs), alongside established mitigation strategies. The discussion then explores the EFT phase, detailing how the integration of quantum error mitigation and partial error correction can sustain algorithmic performance. Addressing the FT phase, we analyze the inherent challenges confronting current hybrid VQA models. Furthermore, we synthesize recent VQA applications across diverse domains, including many-body physics, quantum chemistry, machine learning, and mathematical optimization. Ultimately, this review outlines a theoretical roadmap for adapting quantum algorithms to future hardware generations, elucidating how variational principles can be systematically refined to maintain their relevance and efficiency within an error-corrected computational environment.
one_liner: A technical review maps how variational quantum algorithms may evolve from NISQ hardware into early and full fault-tolerant regimes.
why_it_matters: It frames which VQA ingredients remain useful as hardware improves and where error mitigation or correction changes the algorithmic trade space.
detailed_summary: This review synthesizes the current VQA stack, covering parameterized ansatz construction, cost-function design, gradient estimation, and classical optimizer choices. It examines core training pathologies such as barren plateaus and surveys mitigation techniques relevant to near-term devices. The paper then discusses how early fault-tolerant and fully fault-tolerant hardware alter the assumptions behind hybrid variational workflows, including the role of error mitigation and partial correction. It closes with an application-oriented survey spanning chemistry, many-body physics, machine learning, and optimization.
main_result: The paper provides a structured roadmap for adapting VQAs from NISQ settings to early and full fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
application_angle: It helps hardware and algorithm teams prioritize which variational methods are worth carrying forward as error-corrected quantum systems mature.

title: Belief Propagation Convergence Prediction for Bivariate Bicycle Quantum Error Correction Codes
abstract: Decoding Bivariate Bicycle (BB) quantum error correction codes typically requires Belief Propagation (BP) followed by Ordered Statistics Decoding (OSD) post-processing when BP fails to converge. Whether BP will converge on a given syndrome is currently determined only after running BP to completion. We show that convergence can be predicted in advance by a single modulo operation: if the syndrome defect count is divisible by the code's column weight w, BP converges with high probability (100% at p <= 0.001, degrading to 87% at p = 0.01); otherwise, BP fails with probability >= 90%. The mechanism is structural: each physical data error activates exactly w stabilizers, so a defect count not divisible by w implies the presence of measurement errors outside BP's model space. Validated on five BB codes with column weights w = 2, 3, and 4, mod-w achieves AUC = 0.995 as a convergence classifier at p = 0.001 under phenomenological noise, dominating all other syndrome features (next best: AUC = 0.52). The false positive rate scales empirically as O(p^2.05) (R^2 = 0.98), confirming the analytical bound from Proposition 2. Among BP failures on mod-w = 0 syndromes, 82% contain weight-2 data error clusters, directly confirming the dominant failure mechanism. The prediction is invariant under BP scheduling strategy and decoder variant, including Relay-BP - the strongest known BP enhancement for quantum LDPC codes. These results apply directly to IBM's Gross code [[144, 12, 12]] and Two-Gross code [[288, 12, 18]], targeted for deployment in 2026-2028.
one_liner: A single mod-w syndrome test predicts belief-propagation convergence for bivariate bicycle quantum LDPC decoding.
why_it_matters: Fast convergence prediction can bypass wasted decoder effort and improve practical control flow for large quantum error-correction stacks.
detailed_summary: The authors show that for BB codes, whether BP will converge can be predicted before decoding completes by checking if the syndrome defect count is divisible by the column weight w. Across five codes under phenomenological noise, this simple classifier reaches AUC = 0.995 at p = 0.001 and strongly outperforms other syndrome-derived features. The structural explanation is that each physical data error activates exactly w stabilizers, so non-divisibility indicates measurement-error content outside BP's model. They also identify weight-2 data-error clusters as the dominant failure mode among mod-w = 0 cases where BP still fails.
main_result: A one-step modulo test predicts BP convergence with high accuracy, enabling early discrimination between likely BP success and likely failure cases.
application_angle: This could reduce decoding latency and allocate heavier post-processing only when needed in future LDPC-based quantum processors such as IBM-targeted Gross-code deployments.

title: Harnessing dark states: coherent control in coupled cavity-Rydberg-atom systems
abstract: The dark-state effect, caused by destructive interference, not only is an important fundamental research topic in atomic physics and quantum optics, but also has wide potential application in quantum physics and quantum information science. Using the arrowhead-matrix method, here we study the dark-state effect in a coupled cavity-Rydberg-atom system, in which $N$ Rydberg atoms with the dipole-dipole interactions are coupled to a single-mode cavity field. We obtain the numbers and form of the dark states in certain excitation-number subspaces for the two-, three-, and four-atom cases, as well as in the single-excitation subspace for a general $N$-atom case. We also suggest to characterize the dark states by inspecting the populations of some specific quantum states, which can be detected in experiments. Furthermore, we analyze the dark-state effect in a realistic case, where both the atomic dipole-dipole interaction strengths and the atom-cavity-field coupling strengths depend on the position of the atoms. Our findings pave the way for studying dark-state physics and applications in the cavity-Rydberg-atom platform.
one_liner: Dark states in coupled cavity-Rydberg systems are characterized analytically, including experimentally accessible signatures.
why_it_matters: Controllable dark states are useful for robust state preparation, interference-based control, and quantum-optical device engineering.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a single-mode cavity coupled to interacting Rydberg atoms and derives the number and structure of dark states in several excitation subspaces using an arrowhead-matrix approach. Explicit results are given for two-, three-, and four-atom systems, along with the general N-atom single-excitation case. The authors propose identifying dark states through populations of specific basis states that are experimentally measurable. They further examine realistic position-dependent atom-cavity couplings and dipole-dipole interactions to assess dark-state behavior beyond idealized symmetry assumptions.
main_result: The study derives and characterizes dark-state manifolds in interacting cavity-Rydberg platforms and proposes measurable observables for identifying them experimentally.
application_angle: These results support coherent-control protocols and interference-based quantum functionalities in cavity-QED and Rydberg-atom hardware.

title: On-Chip Interferometric Excitation of an Infinity-Loop Microresonator
abstract: Integrated photonics is a powerful platform for exploring Hermitian and non-Hermitian physics. Beyond device geometry, controlling how resonators are driven is crucial to access and tailor their modes. Coherent excitation via multiple input ports (interferometric excitation) enables such control, but its accurate description requires extending standard temporal coupled-mode theory to include interference between excitation pathways. Experimental realizations have so far been limited by phase-unstable, off-chip interferometers. Here we implement a fully integrated, phase-stable interferometric excitation scheme for an infinity-loop-microresonator, an established structure operating on an exceptional surface, and use it to test the extended theory. Phase-resolved measurements in the linear and thermo-optic nonlinear regimes show that the relative phase between inputs governs the intracavity energy distribution, enabling up to a twofold increase of the circulating power compared to single-port excitation. This integrated platform enables reproducible studies of phase-dependent effects and coherent-control schemes in non-Hermitian photonic devices.
one_liner: An integrated interferometric drive scheme controls mode excitation in an infinity-loop microresonator with phase-stable on-chip inputs.
why_it_matters: It provides reproducible phase control of non-Hermitian microresonators and boosts circulating power without off-chip interferometer instability.
detailed_summary: The work implements fully integrated, phase-stable interferometric excitation of an infinity-loop microresonator and extends temporal coupled-mode theory to include interference between excitation pathways. Phase-resolved measurements validate the theory in both linear and thermo-optic nonlinear regimes. By tuning the relative input phase, the authors control intracavity energy distribution and obtain up to a twofold increase in circulating power versus single-port driving.
main_result: Relative-phase tuning between two on-chip inputs deterministically reshapes cavity excitation and can double the circulating power compared with single-port excitation.
application_angle: The scheme is useful for robust coherent control in integrated non-Hermitian photonic circuits, including tunable filters, sensors, and mode-engineered resonator devices.

title: Photon pairs, squeezed light and the quantum wave mixing effect in a cascaded qubit system
abstract: We develop a theoretical description of quantum wave mixing (QWM) in a cascaded waveguide-QED system of two superconducting qubits, where the probe is driven by an external coherent tone and by the resonance fluorescence of a strongly driven source qubit. Starting from the field correlation functions of the source emission, we derive an effective master-equation treatment for the probe and identify the regime in which the incident fluorescence is characterized by anomalous correlations. When the coherent Rayleigh component of the source spectrum is suppressed, the probe equations of motion become equivalent to those for a qubit driven by a coherent tone and broadband squeezed light. This equivalence implies a selection rule for the peaks of the QWM spectrum, with a strong suppression of sidebands associated with processes involving an odd number of photons taken from the source field. Numerical simulations of the full cascaded two-qubit model for different ratios of radiative decay rates unambiguously confirm the participation of correlated photon pairs in QWM processes. The current research illustrates that the analysis of peak amplitudes can be used to probe photon statistics in the incident nonclassical field.
one_liner: A cascaded two-qubit waveguide-QED model shows quantum wave mixing signatures of correlated photon pairs and squeezed-light-like driving.
why_it_matters: It links measurable wave-mixing spectra to nonclassical photon statistics, offering a practical probe of pair correlations in superconducting quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a theoretical treatment of quantum wave mixing in a cascaded system of two superconducting qubits, where one qubit is driven by both a coherent tone and resonance fluorescence from a strongly driven source qubit. In the regime where the coherent Rayleigh part is suppressed, the probe dynamics become equivalent to a qubit driven by a coherent tone plus broadband squeezed light. This yields a spectral selection rule that strongly suppresses sidebands involving odd numbers of source photons, and full numerical simulations confirm the role of correlated photon pairs.
platform_or_system: cascaded superconducting qubits in waveguide QED
main_result: The authors show that anomalously correlated fluorescence from a source qubit produces distinctive quantum wave-mixing spectra with odd-photon sideband suppression consistent with pair-driven processes.
application_angle: The approach can serve as a hardware-level diagnostic for nonclassical microwave fields and engineered squeezed resources in superconducting quantum networks.

title: A spectropolarimeter for vacuum-ultraviolet emission lines
abstract: We have developed a vacuum-ultraviolet spectropolarimeter to measure the linear polarization of spectral lines around the Lyman-$α$ wavelength. The main components for polarimetry are a rotatable MgF$_2$ waveplate and a SiO$_2$/MgF$_2$ multilayer-coated fused silica plate that functions as a reflective polarizer. A grazing-incidence grating is mounted between them to provide wavelength dispersion. The polarization is determined from the intensity modulation of the spectral line as the waveplate is rotated. The performance of the spectropolarimeter was demonstrated by measuring the polarization of the $2s$--$2p_{3/2}$ transition in Li-like N$^{4+}$ (124~nm) excited by a 1000~eV electron beam in an electron beam ion trap. Clear modulation of the line intensity was observed as a function of the waveplate rotation angle. From the measured modulation amplitude, the degree of linear polarization was determined to be $P=-(0.178^{+0.012}_{-0.005})$, with the negative sign indicating that the emission is polarized predominantly perpendicular to the electron beam. This result demonstrates the capability of the present spectropolarimeter to determine polarizations with an absolute uncertainty $ΔP$ on the order of $0.01$. This instrument provides a useful tool for polarization diagnostics of vacuum-ultraviolet emission lines from laboratory plasmas.
one_liner: A new vacuum-ultraviolet spectropolarimeter measures linear polarization near Lyman-alpha with about 0.01 absolute uncertainty.
why_it_matters: It adds a precise polarization diagnostic for VUV emission lines, enabling better laboratory plasma and atomic-physics measurements in a hard spectral band.
detailed_summary: The instrument combines a rotatable MgF2 waveplate, a SiO2/MgF2 multilayer reflective polarizer, and a grazing-incidence grating to measure wavelength-resolved linear polarization around 124 nm. Its performance was demonstrated on the 2s-2p3/2 transition in Li-like N4+ excited by a 1000 eV electron beam in an electron beam ion trap. The measured modulation yielded a polarization degree of P = -(0.178^{+0.012}_{-0.005}), demonstrating absolute polarization accuracy on the order of 0.01.
main_result: The device resolved VUV line polarization at 124 nm and measured the N4+ transition polarization with clear modulation and roughly 0.01 absolute uncertainty.
application_angle: This is directly relevant for polarization-resolved diagnostics of laboratory plasmas and VUV atomic emission where conventional optics are difficult to deploy.

title: Comparative performance of three optical biosensing platforms for SARS-CoV-2 antibodies detection in human serum
abstract: This study presents a rigorous comparative analysis of two label-free optical biosensing platforms, Bloch surface wave (BSW) and microring resonator (MRR), for the detection of SARS-CoV-2 antibodies in human serum. To ensure direct comparability, a new BSW readout system was established alongside an existing MRR platform, allowing assays to be conducted under nearly identical experimental conditions. Both sensors were functionalized with various SARS-CoV-2 Spike and Nucleocapsid protein variants to capture specific host antibodies. The results demonstrate that both platforms provide rapid, quantitative, and sensitive detection of anti-Spike and anti-Nucleocapsid antibodies without the need for secondary labels. Furthermore, the platforms show excellent agreement with longitudinal serology benchmarks and high repeatability across different biochip batches. This work establishes both BSW and MRR technologies as powerful, low-cost candidates for next-generation clinical diagnostics and serological surveillance.
one_liner: Bloch surface wave and microring resonator biosensors are benchmarked head-to-head for label-free SARS-CoV-2 antibody detection in serum.
why_it_matters: It validates two scalable optical sensor platforms under matched conditions, strengthening their case for low-cost clinical serology and surveillance.
detailed_summary: The study performs a controlled comparison of Bloch surface wave and microring resonator platforms using nearly identical assay conditions and matched SARS-CoV-2 antigen functionalization. Both platforms detect anti-Spike and anti-Nucleocapsid antibodies directly in human serum without secondary labels. The measurements show rapid, quantitative response, strong repeatability across biochip batches, and close agreement with longitudinal serology benchmarks.
main_result: Both label-free photonic biosensing platforms achieved sensitive and repeatable antibody detection in serum with strong agreement to serology references.
application_angle: The results support practical deployment of compact photonic chips for clinical diagnostics, decentralized testing, and population-level immune surveillance.

title: Evaluating the performance of a weak-field homodyne receiver in quadrature phase-shift keying optical communication
abstract: Quantum communication protocols require efficient detection schemes to maximize the information transfer rate between the sender and the receiver. To this aim, we have demonstrated that weak-field receivers, merging wave-like and particle-like features, can be considered as a valid alternative to already existing receivers, such as optical homodyne detection. To better emphasize the potential of our receiver, in this work we consider a proof of concept for quaternary communication based on coherent states with the same amplitude and different phase values. The encoding in phase requires a fine control of phase noise obtained through a feedback system. The results achieved in terms of mutual information and secret key generation rate encourage further increase of the alphabet towards an approximately continuous phase modulation.
one_liner: A weak-field homodyne receiver is assessed for QPSK optical communication using phase-stabilized coherent-state encoding.
why_it_matters: It indicates that hybrid wave-particle receivers can be competitive for quantum communications and potentially useful for higher-order phase modulation.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a weak-field receiver for quaternary coherent-state communication with quadrature phase-shift keying, where phase noise is controlled through an active feedback system. The receiver blends homodyne-like interference with photon-counting features, enabling performance assessment beyond standard homodyne detection. Reported mutual information and secret-key-rate results support the feasibility of this architecture and motivate extension toward larger, near-continuous phase alphabets.
main_result: The demonstrated receiver supports QPSK coherent-state communication with performance metrics that are promising for information transfer and key generation.
application_angle: This could improve practical optical and quantum communication receivers where efficient discrimination of low-energy phase-encoded signals is critical.

title: Optimized Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill Error Correction via Tunable Preprocessing
abstract: The Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code is a promising bosonic candidate for realizing fault-tolerant quantum computation. Among existing error-correction protocols for GKP code, the Steane-type scheme is a canonical and widely adopted paradigm, yet its intrinsic noise propagation pattern limits further performance improvement. In this work, we propose a preprocessing-based Steane-type (P-Steane) scheme, which introduces a tunable preprocessing stage with squeezing parameters $a$ and $b$ to actively reshape noise propagation, thereby constituting a parameter framework. This framework spans a spectrum of protocols beyond existing methods, reproducing the performance of both the ME-Steane scheme ($a=1$, $b=1$) and the teleportation-based scheme ($a=1/\sqrt{2}$, $b=\sqrt{2}$) as special cases. Crucially, in the small-noise regime and when the data qubit is noisier than the ancilla qubits, P-Steane scheme achieves the minimum product of position- and momentum-quadrature output noise variances when $2a = b$, and consistently outperforms the ME-Steane scheme within a specific squeezing-parameter range under this condition.
one_liner: A tunable preprocessing stage improves Steane-type GKP error correction by reshaping quadrature noise before correction.
why_it_matters: It offers a more flexible bosonic error-correction protocol that can outperform standard Steane schemes in realistic asymmetric-noise settings.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a preprocessing-based Steane-type protocol for GKP error correction with tunable squeezing parameters a and b that modify how noise propagates through the correction circuit. The framework contains both the ME-Steane and teleportation-based schemes as special cases. In the small-noise regime, especially when the data qubit is noisier than the ancillas, the condition 2a = b minimizes the product of output position and momentum noise variances and yields performance better than ME-Steane over a defined parameter range.
main_result: The proposed P-Steane protocol identifies tunable squeezing settings that reduce output quadrature noise and outperform standard ME-Steane correction in relevant regimes.
application_angle: Better GKP correction directly supports more practical bosonic fault-tolerant processors and long-distance quantum communication architectures based on continuous-variable encoding.

title: Yellow whispering-gallery-mode lasing from amorphous fluoride microspheres
abstract: Compact, low-noise coherent light sources in the visible remain challenging due to limited gain platforms and inefficient pumping. We report a new route to visible microlasing based on direct, one-photon blue pumping and an amorphous fluoride gain material platform. Dysprosium doped fluoride microspheres are fabricated via plasma-torch-induced, pressureless amorphization of single crystals, enabling compositions beyond conventional glass-forming limits while ensuring ultrasmooth morphology, low phonon energy, and homogeneous dopant distribution. We demonstrate the first fiber-coupled whispering-gallery-mode lasing from an amorphous fluoride microsphere in the yellow (573 nm), with an ultralow threshold of $190 μ$W despite spin-forbidden Dy$^{3+}$ transitions. Lasing is evidenced by characteristic light-light curve indicating a low spontaneous emission factor, narrow-linewidth emission, and relaxation oscillations yielding a loaded quality factor of $Q = 3.5 \times 10^6$. This platform is readily extendable to other rare-earth emitters, enabling entire visible spectral coverage beyond the limitations of upconversion pumping, with prospects for color-tunable and white-light emission. Finally, fiber-based amplification of the WGM signal demonstrates a pathway toward compact, fiber-integrated visible microlasers with controllable noise and linewidth.
one_liner: Dy-doped amorphous fluoride microspheres enable fiber-coupled yellow WGM lasing at 573 nm with a 190 uW threshold.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical route to compact visible microlasers with low threshold, high Q, and fiber compatibility beyond standard glass and upconversion limits.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates the first fiber-coupled whispering-gallery-mode lasing from an amorphous fluoride microsphere, using Dy3+ ions and direct one-photon blue pumping to generate yellow emission at 573 nm. The microspheres are formed by plasma-torch-induced pressureless amorphization of fluoride single crystals, yielding ultrasmooth cavities with low phonon energy and homogeneous rare-earth distribution. Despite relying on spin-forbidden dysprosium transitions, the device reaches an ultralow lasing threshold of 190 uW and a loaded quality factor of 3.5 x 10^6, with narrow linewidth and relaxation oscillations confirming coherent operation. The authors also show fiber-based amplification of the WGM output, supporting integration into compact visible photonic systems.
main_result: A fiber-coupled amorphous fluoride microsphere laser produced 573 nm yellow WGM lasing with a 190 uW threshold and Q = 3.5 x 10^6.
application_angle: The platform is relevant for integrated visible light sources, low-noise sensing, and color-tunable rare-earth microlasers compatible with fiber photonics.

title: A Model Context Protocol Server for Quantum Execution in Hybrid Quantum-HPC Environments
abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into scientific research is accelerating the realization of autonomous ``AI Scientists.'' While recent advancements have empowered AI to formulate hypotheses and design experiments, a critical gap remains in the execution of these tasks, particularly in the domain of quantum computing (QC). Executing quantum algorithms requires not only generating code but also managing complex computational resources such as QPUs and high-performance computing (HPC) clusters. In this paper, we propose an AI-driven framework specifically designed to bridge this execution gap through the implementation of a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. Our system enables an LLM agent to process natural language prompts submitted as part of a job, autonomously executing quantum computing workflows by invoking our tools via the MCP. We demonstrate the framework's capability by performing essential quantum algorithmic primitives, including sampling and computation of expectation values. Key technical contributions include the development of an MCP server for quantum execution, a pipeline for interpreting OpenQASM code, an automated workflow with CUDA-Q for the ABCI-Q hybrid platform, and an asynchronous execution pipeline for remote quantum hardware using the Quantinuum emulator via CUDA-Q. This work validates that AI agents can effectively abstract the complexities of hardware interaction through an MCP-based architecture, thereby facilitating the automation of practical quantum research.
one_liner: An MCP server lets LLM agents launch quantum jobs across hybrid HPC-QPU systems from natural-language task requests.
why_it_matters: It addresses the execution bottleneck for autonomous quantum workflows by abstracting hardware access, scheduling, and remote job handling into a usable control layer.
detailed_summary: This work introduces a Model Context Protocol server that allows an LLM agent to interpret natural-language tasks and execute quantum workflows on hybrid computing infrastructure. The system includes an OpenQASM interpretation pipeline, CUDA-Q integration for the ABCI-Q platform, and asynchronous remote execution support targeting Quantinuum emulation through CUDA-Q. Demonstrations cover core primitives such as sampling and expectation-value estimation, showing that the agent can orchestrate resource use without exposing low-level hardware-management complexity. Technically, the contribution is less about new quantum algorithms and more about a control-plane architecture for practical autonomous quantum execution.
main_result: The authors built and demonstrated an MCP-based execution framework that lets LLM agents run quantum workloads across local and remote hybrid quantum computing resources.
application_angle: It is relevant for automated labs and quantum cloud operations where researchers need reliable orchestration of QPUs, emulators, and HPC backends.

title: Scalable Neural Decoders for Practical Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for scalable quantum computing. However, it requires classical decoders that are fast and accurate enough to keep pace with quantum hardware. While quantum low-density parity-check codes have recently emerged as a promising route to efficient fault tolerance, current decoding algorithms do not allow one to realize the full potential of these codes in practical settings. Here, we introduce a convolutional neural network decoder that exploits the geometric structure of QEC codes, and use it to probe a novel "waterfall" regime of error suppression, demonstrating that the logical error rates required for large-scale fault-tolerant algorithms are attainable with modest code sizes at current physical error rates, and with latencies within the real-time budgets of several leading hardware platforms. For example, for the $[144, 12, 12]$ Gross code, the decoder achieves logical error rates up to $\sim 17$x below existing decoders - reaching logical error rates $\sim 10^{-10}$ at physical error $p=0.1\%$ - with 3-5 orders of magnitude higher throughput. This decoder also produces well-calibrated confidence estimates that can significantly reduce the time overhead of repeat-until-success protocols. Taken together, these results suggest that the space-time costs associated with fault-tolerant quantum computation may be significantly lower than previously anticipated.
one_liner: A geometry-aware CNN decoder sharply improves qLDPC decoding accuracy and throughput, reaching logical error rates near 1e-10.
why_it_matters: Fast, accurate real-time decoding is a central hardware bottleneck for fault tolerance, and this result lowers both latency and space-time overhead estimates.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a convolutional neural-network decoder tailored to the geometric structure of quantum error-correcting codes, targeting practical low-latency deployment. On the [144, 12, 12] Gross code, it achieves up to about 17x lower logical error rate than prior decoders while improving throughput by 3 to 5 orders of magnitude. At a physical error rate of 0.1%, the decoder reaches logical error rates around 10^-10, entering a waterfall regime compatible with large-scale fault-tolerant workloads. The model also outputs calibrated confidence estimates, which could reduce overhead in repeat-until-success protocols and adaptive control flows.
platform_or_system: Neural decoder for qLDPC fault-tolerant stacks
main_result: The decoder delivers substantially better logical suppression and vastly higher throughput, including about 10^-10 logical error at p = 0.1% on the Gross code.
application_angle: This directly impacts scalable quantum processors by making real-time classical decoding feasible within hardware timing budgets.

title: Per-Shot Evaluation of QAOA on Max-Cut: A Black-Box Implementation Comparison with Goemans-Williamson
abstract: The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) has emerged as a promising approach for addressing combinatorial optimization problems on near-term quantum hardware. In this work, we conduct an empirical evaluation of QAOA on the Max-Cut problem, using the Goemans-Williamson (GW) algorithm as a classical baseline for comparison. Unlike many prior studies, our methodology treats QAOA implementations as black-box optimizers, relying solely on default parameter settings without manual fine-tuning. We evaluate specific off-the-shelf QAOA implementations under default settings, not the algorithmic potential of QAOA with optimized parameters. This reflects a more realistic use case for end users who may lack the resources or expertise for instance-specific optimization. To facilitate fair and informative evaluation, we construct benchmark instances using well-known graph generation models that emulate practical graph structures, avoiding synthetic constructions tailored to either quantum or classical algorithms. A central component of our analysis is a per-shot statistical framework, which tracks the quality of QAOA outputs as a function of the number of circuit executions. This enables probabilistic comparisons with the GW algorithm by examining when and how frequently QAOA surpasses classical performance baselines such as the GW expectation and lower bound. Our results provide insight into the practical applicability of QAOA for Max-Cut and highlight its current limitations, offering a framework that can guide the assessment and development of future QAOA implementations.
one_liner: A per-shot benchmarking framework shows how default QAOA stacks compare probabilistically against Goemans-Williamson on Max-Cut.
why_it_matters: It evaluates QAOA the way users actually encounter it, exposing practical performance limits without instance-specific tuning or idealized settings.
detailed_summary: The authors benchmark off-the-shelf QAOA implementations for Max-Cut under default settings, treating them strictly as black-box optimizers rather than hand-tuned algorithmic exemplars. They introduce a per-shot statistical analysis that tracks cut quality as a function of circuit executions and compares the probability of beating Goemans-Williamson reference levels. Benchmark graphs are drawn from standard generation models intended to mimic practical structure rather than synthetic cases tailored to favor either method. The study provides a realistic measurement framework for near-term optimization performance and clarifies where current turnkey QAOA remains uncompetitive.
main_result: The work establishes a per-shot evaluation method that quantifies when default QAOA runs do or do not surpass Goemans-Williamson baselines on realistic Max-Cut instances.
application_angle: It is useful for users deciding whether current quantum optimization software is viable for real workloads under limited tuning and shot budgets.

title: Closing the Loop in Epitaxy with Machine Learning: Joint Optimization of Growth and Geometry in On-Chip Lasers
abstract: Achieving device-to-device reproducibility is a critical bottleneck for scalable photonic integrated circuits, as subtle variations in bottom-up epitaxial growth and fabrication severely limit yield. We present a machine learning workflow for III-V multi-quantum well microring lasers that first optimizes growth and geometry parameters via multi-objective Bayesian optimization, then leverages variational autoencoders (VAEs) to attribute residual device-to-device variability to its underlying sources. By explicitly targeting threshold variance alongside absolute performance, we demonstrate 100% lasing yield across all designs. The optimized multi-quantum well microring laser fields achieved a median lasing threshold of $16~μ\mathrm{J}\,\mathrm{cm}^{-2}\,\mathrm{pulse}^{-1}$, a $73\%$ reduction in threshold variance relative to the previously reported best values, and a median emission wavelength of $1333~\mathrm{nm}$, in the telecommunications O-band. Furthermore, to diagnose residual performance dispersion under nominally identical conditions, VAEs were used to isolate the key components of device morphology that impact performance. This analysis successfully decoupled geometric from material disorder, quantitatively linking previously unmeasured morphological variations to population-level threshold fluctuations. This data-driven workflow bridges the gap between fundamental epitaxy and reliable manufacturing, establishing a generalizable blueprint for designing and yield-optimizing complex, non-linear optoelectronic devices.
one_liner: Bayesian optimization plus VAEs boosted III-V microring laser yield to 100% while cutting threshold variance by 73%.
why_it_matters: Yield and reproducibility are major blockers for scalable photonic integration, and this workflow ties process optimization directly to manufacturable laser performance.
detailed_summary: This paper applies a machine-learning workflow to III-V multi-quantum-well microring lasers, jointly optimizing epitaxial growth and device geometry using multi-objective Bayesian optimization. By targeting threshold variance as well as absolute performance, the optimized designs achieved 100% lasing yield, a median threshold of 16 uJ cm^-2 pulse^-1, and a 73% reduction in threshold variance relative to prior best values. Variational autoencoders were then used to separate geometric from material disorder and connect latent morphological variations to residual threshold spread. The result is a closed-loop methodology linking growth, structure, and device statistics in telecom-band on-chip lasers.
main_result: The optimized process-design workflow achieved full lasing yield and substantially reduced threshold variability for O-band on-chip microring lasers.
application_angle: It offers a practical route to higher-yield photonic integrated circuits by making laser reproducibility and process tuning data-driven.

title: Small-scale photonic Kolmogorov-Arnold networks using standard telecom nonlinear modules
abstract: Photonic neural networks promise ultrafast inference, yet most architectures rely on linear optical meshes with electronic nonlinearities, reintroducing optical-electrical-optical bottlenecks. Here we introduce small-scale photonic Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (SSP-KANs) implemented entirely with standard telecommunications components. Each network edge employs a trainable nonlinear module composed of a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, semiconductor optical amplifier, and variable optical attenuators, providing a four-parameter transfer function derived from gain saturation and interferometric mixing. Despite this constrained expressivity, SSP-KANs comprising only a few optical modules achieve strong nonlinear inference performance across classification, regression, and image recognition tasks, approaching software baselines with significantly fewer parameters. A four-module network achieves 98.4\% accuracy on nonlinear classification benchmarks inaccessible to linear models. Performance remains robust under realistic hardware impairments, maintaining high accuracy down to 6-bit input resolution and 14 dB signal-to-noise ratio. By using a fully differentiable physics model for end-to-end optimisation of optical parameters, this work establishes a practical pathway from simulation to experimental demonstration of photonic KANs using commodity telecom hardware.
one_liner: Standard telecom nonlinear modules can implement compact all-photonic KANs with strong inference performance and hardware robustness.
why_it_matters: It replaces optical-electrical-optical nonlinearities with deployable telecom components, making photonic neural inference more experimentally realistic.
detailed_summary: The authors propose small-scale photonic Kolmogorov-Arnold networks built entirely from commodity telecom components, where each trainable edge uses a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, semiconductor optical amplifier, and variable attenuators. The nonlinear transfer function arises from gain saturation and interferometric mixing, yielding a compact four-parameter optical primitive suitable for end-to-end differentiable training. Even with only a few modules, the system achieves strong results across classification, regression, and image tasks, including 98.4% accuracy on a nonlinear classification benchmark that linear models cannot solve. Simulations indicate resilience to realistic impairments, retaining high performance down to 6-bit inputs and 14 dB SNR.
main_result: A four-module all-photonic KAN achieved 98.4% accuracy on nonlinear classification while remaining robust under realistic noise and quantization limits.
application_angle: Because it uses standard telecom hardware, the approach is well positioned for near-term experimental photonic AI accelerators and edge inference systems.

title: Floquet Engineering of a Quasiequilibrium Superradiant Phase Transition in Landau Polaritons
abstract: Superradiant phase transitions (SRPTs), characterized by photon condensation and macroscopic matter polarization, are forbidden in equilibrium for homogeneous fields by no-go theorems. Here, we show that Floquet driving can circumvent this constraint in a Landau polariton system consisting of a two-dimensional electron gas coupled to a terahertz cavity in a DC magnetic field. An off-resonant AC magnetic field modulates the cyclotron frequency and light--matter coupling strength while leaving the diamagnetic term unchanged, generating an additional DC coupling contribution. This drives the system across a critical threshold into a superradiant phase, characterized by photon condensation and Landau-level polarization in the ground state of the Floquet Hamiltonian. This quasiequilibrium approach offers a route to SRPTs distinct from driven-dissipative schemes.
one_liner: Floquet driving in a Landau-polariton platform induces a quasiequilibrium superradiant phase transition otherwise forbidden in equilibrium.
why_it_matters: It outlines a realistic route to photon-condensed many-body phases in THz cavity matter systems without relying on driven-dissipative instability.
detailed_summary: The work studies a two-dimensional electron gas in a DC magnetic field coupled to a terahertz cavity, with an off-resonant AC magnetic field periodically modulating the cyclotron frequency and light-matter coupling. Because the diamagnetic term is left unchanged, the Floquet drive creates an additional effective DC coupling that can exceed the critical threshold for a superradiant phase transition. In the Floquet ground state, the transition is marked by photon condensation and macroscopic Landau-level polarization. The proposal is notable because it bypasses standard equilibrium no-go constraints through quasiequilibrium Floquet engineering rather than dissipation-driven effects.
platform_or_system: 2DEG Landau polaritons in a THz cavity
main_result: The paper shows that off-resonant Floquet modulation can drive a Landau-polariton system into a quasiequilibrium superradiant phase with ground-state photon condensation.
application_angle: It suggests a practical control knob for engineering collective light-matter phases in solid-state THz photonic hardware and quantum materials platforms.

title: Increased endurance of nonvolatile photonics enabled by nanostructured phase-change materials
abstract: The rapid rise of artificial intelligence, and in-memory computing has reinvigorated research on scalable, energy-efficient, and reconfigurable photonic hardware. Non-volatile phase-change materials (PCMs) are attractive, as they offer large refractive index contrast, wavelength-scale footprints, and zero static power consumption. However, current PCM-based electrically controlled photonic devices are plagued by high insertion loss and low endurance. One prevalent hypothesis for these material limitations come from electromagnetic scattering in the interface and large programming volumes, respectively. Here, we validate this hypothesis by showing that nano-structuring of PCM minimizes optical loss and enhances the endurance. By tapering both ends of a wide bandgap PCM Sb2Se3 segment on a silicon waveguide, we suppressed the insertion loss by ~94% (resulting in a loss of ~0.1 dB per π phase shift). Through combining tapering and segmentation, we achieved high optical modulation amplitude (~70%), low loss (~0.5 dB per π phase shift), low-voltage (< 5V) actuation, and record high endurance greater than 100 million cycles. This work showcases the substantial advantage of nanopatterning PCMs to attain low loss and high cyclability.
one_liner: Nanostructured Sb2Se3 phase-change sections on silicon waveguides sharply cut loss while pushing endurance past 100 million cycles.
why_it_matters: It addresses the two main blockers for nonvolatile photonic hardware, insertion loss and cycling reliability, with a fabrication-compatible design strategy.
detailed_summary: The authors redesign electrically controlled PCM photonic devices by tapering and segmenting a wide-bandgap Sb2Se3 section integrated on a silicon waveguide. The nanostructuring suppresses interface scattering and reduces programming volume, yielding about 94% lower insertion loss and roughly 0.1 dB per pi phase shift in the tapered design. Combining tapering with segmentation delivers about 70% optical modulation amplitude, about 0.5 dB per pi phase shift, sub-5 V switching, and endurance exceeding 100 million cycles. The results validate the hypothesis that optical loss and poor cyclability are strongly tied to electromagnetic scattering and large switched volumes.
main_result: Nanopatterned PCM photonic elements achieve low-loss, low-voltage, high-contrast modulation with record endurance above 10^8 switching cycles.
application_angle: This makes nonvolatile photonic memory and programmable photonic accelerators more viable for long-lived in-memory computing and reconfigurable optical hardware.

title: High-efficiency graphene-silicon slot-waveguide microring modulator at 1.5 μm and 2 μm wavelength bands
abstract: Electro-optic (E/O) modulators are crucial for optical communication but face a trade-off between modulation bandwidth and efficiency. A small footprint could reduce the capacitance and increase the bandwidth, however, this usually results in a low modulation efficiency. Here, we present an integrated E/O modulator that simultaneously achieves wideband large bandwidth and high modu- lation efficiency operation by embedding a partially overlapped double-layer graphene on a compact silicon slot waveguide microring resonator. At 1550 nm, the graphene-silicon slot-waveguide demon- strates a high phase modulation efficiency of Vπ L = 220 V μm, and the corresponding microring modulator has a large bandwidth of over 70 GHz, a compact active length of 10 μm, and an optical modulation amplitude (OMA) of -1.97 dBm under a 3-V voltage swing. The modulator operates at a data rate of 50 Gbit/s with an open eye diagram under a 2-V Vpp RF drive voltage. The graphene modulator operation is broadband, and we also characterize its performance at 2 μm wavelength band. At 2 μm wavelength band, the microring modulator has a large bandwidth of over 20 GHz, an OMA of -3.36 dBm under a 6-V voltage swing, and an open eye diagram at 20 Gbit/s with a 2-V Vpp RF drive voltage. The difference in performance is caused by the bandwidth limit of the 2 μm wavelength band measurement setup. The broadband, large bandwidth, compact, highly effi- cient, and energy efficient graphene E/O modulator has the potential to enable large-scale graphene photonic integrated circuits, facilitating a broad range of applications such as optical interconnects, optical neural networks, and programmable photonic circuits.
one_liner: A double-layer graphene silicon slot-waveguide microring modulator combines compact footprint, high efficiency, and >70 GHz bandwidth at 1.55 um.
why_it_matters: It pushes graphene modulators closer to deployable optical interconnects by reducing the usual tradeoff between footprint, efficiency, and speed.
detailed_summary: The device embeds partially overlapped double-layer graphene into a compact silicon slot-waveguide microring resonator to boost electro-optic phase efficiency without sacrificing speed. At 1550 nm it reaches VpiL = 220 V um, over 70 GHz bandwidth, 10 um active length, and open-eye operation at 50 Gbit/s under a 2 Vpp RF drive. The same architecture also operates in the 2 um band, where the measured bandwidth exceeds 20 GHz and supports 20 Gbit/s open-eye transmission, with the authors attributing the lower speed mainly to the measurement setup. The work demonstrates genuinely broadband graphene modulation on a highly compact integrated platform.
main_result: The modulator delivers high phase efficiency and large bandwidth in a 10 um-scale device, supporting 50 Gbit/s at 1.55 um and operation in the 2 um band.
application_angle: Its small footprint and energy-efficient high-speed operation are directly relevant to dense photonic ICs for interconnects, optical neural networks, and programmable photonics.

title: Dispersion Control in Micromechanical Evanescent Optical Modulators
abstract: Efficient, low-loss, and versatile optical modulators are a critical ingredient for practical integrated photonic systems. Modulators based on micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) have unique advantages over more traditional thermal, electro-optic, or plasma dispersion modulators. In this work, we show that evanescent MEMS modulators (in which a dielectric slab is mechanically inserted into a waveguide's evanescent field) can exhibit anomalously dispersive modulation. That is, despite positive modulation of a waveguide mode's effective index, the modulator brings about a negative change in group index. We experimentally demonstrate these unique capabilities using a novel MEMS actuator design. The new theory and results here reveal that evanescent MEMS modulators possess a capability for control of wavelength dispersion not accessible to nearly any other type of modulator. These new capabilities may enable on-chip integration of systems for various optical applications, including broadband switching, photonic true time delay, pulse shaping, or phase matching of nonlinear processes.
one_liner: Evanescent MEMS optical modulators are shown to provide anomalous dispersion control, including negative group-index shifts from positive index tuning.
why_it_matters: It adds a rare on-chip mechanism for directly engineering dispersion, useful where phase and delay control matter as much as amplitude modulation.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes and experimentally demonstrates evanescent MEMS modulators in which a dielectric slab is mechanically moved into the evanescent field of a guided optical mode. Unlike conventional modulators, these devices can increase effective index while producing a negative change in group index, i.e. anomalously dispersive modulation. A new actuator design is used to verify this behavior experimentally and establish dispersion as a directly tunable device parameter. This extends MEMS photonics beyond low-loss phase tuning into a regime relevant for wavelength-dependent signal processing.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a MEMS photonic modulator that independently accesses unusual dispersion behavior, including negative group-index modulation.
application_angle: The capability is useful for broadband switching, true time delay, pulse shaping, and nonlinear phase matching in integrated photonic systems.

title: Dynamical control of non-hermitian coupling between sub-threshold nanolasers enables Q-switched pulse generation
abstract: Non-Hermitian photonics provides a framework to engineer the gain and loss of optical modes in open systems, enabling control of their spectral and dynamical properties. In particular, the ability to dynamically tune modal losses offers a route to implement functionalities traditionally relying on cavity Q-factor modulation, such as Q-switching, within nanophotonic platforms. Here, we demonstrate the generation of short optical pulses in a pair of phase-coupled photonic crystal nanolasers exploiting non-Hermitian coupling. Two waveguide-coupled nanocavities are operated below their individual lasing thresholds and subjected to asymmetric optical pumping, such that a transient carrier-induced detuning modifies the interference conditions between them. This dynamically controls the gain and loss of the collective modes, and, upon crossing a resonance condition, leads to the rapid release of stored carrier energy as an optical pulse. A rate-equation model captures the interplay between carrier dynamics and modal coupling and reproduces the observed behavior. Experiments performed on an indium phosphide platform show pulse generation from cavities that do not lase efficiently on their own in continuous-wave operation, with temporal characteristics governed by carrier dynamics. These results indicate that non-Hermitian coupling can be used to control the effective cavity losses in time, providing a route to pulse generation in integrated photonic systems.
one_liner: Dynamic non-Hermitian coupling between two sub-threshold photonic-crystal nanolasers enables integrated Q-switched pulse generation.
why_it_matters: It shows that time-dependent loss engineering can create pulsed nanophotonic sources even when individual cavities do not lase efficiently in CW operation.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses two waveguide-coupled photonic crystal nanocavities on indium phosphide, both biased below standalone lasing threshold and driven with asymmetric optical pumping. Transient carrier-induced detuning changes the interference condition between the cavities, dynamically modulating collective-mode gain and loss through non-Hermitian coupling. Crossing the resonance condition triggers rapid release of stored carrier energy as a short optical pulse, and a rate-equation model reproduces the observed pulse dynamics. The result reframes Q-switch-like operation as dynamic loss control in an open nanophotonic system.
main_result: Sub-threshold coupled nanolasers generate short pulses by dynamically tuning effective modal loss via non-Hermitian coupling.
application_angle: This offers a compact route to integrated pulsed light sources and dynamic cavity control in nanophotonic signal-processing and sensing chips.

title: Measurement-induced state transitions across the fluxonium qubit landscape
abstract: Understanding the mechanisms that limit high-fidelity readout in circuit quantum electrodynamics is essential for its optimization. Multi-photon resonances are understood to be a limiting factor, causing population transfer from the computational states to higher-energy states under drive. This effect, known as measurement-induced state transitions, has been extensively studied for the transmon qubit. While this exploration has begun for the fluxonium qubit, a systematic study of this effect is lacking. Here, we bridge this gap by theoretically studying measurement-induced state transitions in the fluxonium qubit over a wide range of parameters, comprising essentially all experimentally explored ranges. We find that lighter fluxoniums are less susceptible to these state transitions when compared to their heavier counterparts. We attribute this effect to the combination of lower density of multi-photon resonances, a smaller requisite coupling for a given dispersive shift, and a more harmonic-like structure of the charge operator. We confirm the validity of our analysis by performing time-dependent readout simulations. Finally, we consider the impact of the superinductor's array modes on measurement-induced state transitions over a large range of parameters.
one_liner: A broad theoretical survey maps when fluxonium qubits undergo measurement-induced state transitions during dispersive readout.
why_it_matters: It gives hardware designers a concrete basis for choosing fluxonium parameters that preserve readout fidelity and suppress leakage.
detailed_summary: The paper systematically studies measurement-induced state transitions in fluxonium across essentially the full experimentally relevant parameter landscape, extending prior understanding beyond transmons. It identifies lighter fluxoniums as less vulnerable to leakage during readout because they exhibit a lower density of multiphoton resonances, require smaller coupling for a given dispersive shift, and have a more harmonic-like charge operator. Time-dependent readout simulations support the analysis, and the role of superinductor array modes is also evaluated over a wide range. The result is a practical map linking circuit design choices to readout robustness.
main_result: The study finds that lighter fluxonium designs are systematically less susceptible to measurement-induced leakage during dispersive readout than heavier variants.
application_angle: It informs parameter selection for scalable superconducting quantum processors where fast, high-fidelity readout is a central hardware bottleneck.

title: Fresnel zone plates for reconfigurable atomic waveguides
abstract: Fresnel zone plates (FZPs), with patterns of $1\,μ$m resolution, allow the formation of clean, diffraction-limited foci -- but have a static phase profile. Spatial light modulators (SLMs) allow dynamic control of spatial beam intensity and phase -- but are bulky and currently limited to roughly $10\,μ$m pixel sizes and $1\,$Mega-pixel formats. Here, we present a new `best-of-both' kind of FZP, scalable to large area rings currently incompatible with direct SLM generation. It is equivalent to a plano-convex donut lens, whereby light's local intensity and global phase at the FZP map directly onto the image plane. The same FZP under different SLM illumination can generate: rings and arcs, double-rings, phase windings and ring lattices (or dynamic combinations thereof). The smooth and adaptable near-field waveguide this enables will be ideal for Sagnac interferometry with ultracold atoms.
one_liner: A hybrid Fresnel-zone-plate/SLM approach creates dynamically reconfigurable, diffraction-limited atomic waveguides such as rings, arcs, and lattices.
why_it_matters: It combines micron-scale focusing with programmable illumination, enabling large-area atom-optics geometries that are hard to realize with SLMs alone.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a Fresnel zone plate architecture that behaves like a plano-convex donut lens, mapping local intensity and global phase from the illumination pattern into the image plane. Unlike a conventional static FZP, the same element can generate rings, arcs, double rings, phase windings, and ring lattices when driven by different SLM patterns. The design leverages 1 um-scale FZP resolution while retaining runtime reconfigurability from the SLM. This yields smooth near-field optical potentials tailored for guiding ultracold atoms in interferometric geometries.
platform_or_system: SLM-illuminated Fresnel zone plates for ultracold atoms
main_result: They show that a single FZP under different SLM illumination can realize multiple reconfigurable atomic-waveguide geometries with diffraction-limited focusing.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to compact atom-interferometer hardware, especially ring-waveguide layouts for Sagnac rotation sensing.

title: Decoding coherent errors in toric codes on honeycomb and square lattices: duality to Majorana monitored dynamics and symmetry classes
abstract: Topological stabilizer codes, such as the toric and surface codes, are leading candidates for fault-tolerant quantum computation. While their decodability under stochastic noise has been extensively studied, the effects of coherent errors, which involve quantum interference, remain less explored. In this work, we study the decodability of toric codes on honeycomb and square lattices subject to $X$- and $Z$-type coherent errors generated by the $X$- and $Z$-rotations on each qubit. We establish a duality between these decoding problems and 1+1D monitored dynamics of non-interacting Majorana fermions. This duality shows that the Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry class of the dual Majorana dynamics governs the universal structure of the decodability phase diagram. We show that the honeycomb-lattice toric code (hTC) with $X$-type error is dual to class-DIII dynamics, while the hTC with $Z$-type error and the square-lattice toric code (sTC) with both error types are dual to class-D dynamics. The key distinction arises from time-reversal symmetry. In class DIII, the generic transition out of the decodable phase is dual to a measurement-induced transition between dynamical phases with area-law and logarithmic entanglement scaling. In contrast, in class D, the generic decodability transition corresponds to a transition between two topologically distinct area-law phases. To explore these transitions in microscopic models, we consider hTC and sTC with $X$-type errors as representatives and introduce a minimal two-parameter coherent error model with spatially varying rotation angles. Using analytical and numerical methods, we map out the decodability phase diagrams and characterize the universal behavior of the transitions. We find that the decodability of sTC is more vulnerable to spatially varying coherent errors than uniform ones.
one_liner: Coherent-error decoding in toric codes is mapped to monitored Majorana dynamics, revealing symmetry-class-dependent decodability transitions.
why_it_matters: It gives a universal framework for predicting how coherent control errors affect fault-tolerant code performance on realistic lattice layouts.
detailed_summary: The authors establish a duality between decoding toric codes under coherent X- and Z-rotation errors and 1+1D monitored dynamics of non-interacting Majorana fermions. They show that the governing Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry class determines the universality of the decodability phase diagram: honeycomb X-errors map to class DIII, while honeycomb Z-errors and square-lattice cases map to class D. Using analytical and numerical methods, they chart phase diagrams for minimal two-parameter coherent-error models with spatially varying rotation angles. A key hardware-relevant conclusion is that square-lattice toric codes are more vulnerable to spatially varying coherent errors than to uniform ones.
platform_or_system: Toric codes on honeycomb and square superconducting-style lattices
main_result: The main result is a symmetry-based dual description that classifies coherent-error decoding transitions and quantifies lattice-dependent vulnerability.
application_angle: This informs decoder design and calibration strategies for fault-tolerant quantum processors where coherent, spatially structured control errors are unavoidable.

title: High-Fidelity Transmon Reset with a Multimode Acoustic Resonator
abstract: Achieving sufficiently low residual excited-state populations remains a key challenge in superconducting quantum circuits, particularly for protocols operating close to noise limits or requiring repeated qubit initialization. Existing protocols primarily address this challenge through sophisticated control, engineered dissipation, or feedback mechanisms. Here, we demonstrate an alternative approach in which a superconducting qubit is reset using a physically distinct, intrinsically colder phononic bath. Specifically, we interface a transmon with a high-overtone bulk acoustic resonator (HBAR), enabling cooling of the qubit into GHz-frequency modes. Using this approach, we achieve a residual excited-state population of the qubit below $10^{-4}$, representing an improvement of one to two orders of magnitude compared to existing reset schemes. These results highlight the potential of phononic baths as a resource for high-fidelity qubit initialization in superconducting circuits.
one_liner: A transmon coupled to a multimode acoustic resonator is reset via a colder phononic bath, reaching residual excitation below 1e-4.
why_it_matters: Ultra-low reset error is critical for repeated syndrome extraction and low-noise superconducting quantum computing workflows.
detailed_summary: This work uses a high-overtone bulk acoustic resonator as a physically distinct cold bath for a superconducting transmon. By coupling the qubit to GHz-frequency phononic modes, the authors cool the qubit to a residual excited-state population below 10^-4. That performance is reported as one to two orders of magnitude better than existing reset schemes. The result demonstrates that multimode acoustic environments can serve as engineered dissipation resources rather than parasitic elements.
platform_or_system: Transmon coupled to a high-overtone bulk acoustic resonator
main_result: They demonstrate transmon reset with residual excited-state population below 10^-4 using a multimode acoustic resonator as an intrinsic cold phononic bath.
application_angle: The method is practical for fast, high-fidelity initialization in superconducting processors, especially for repeated QEC cycles and near-noise-floor protocols.

title: Hardware-Efficient Erasure Qubits With Superconducting Transmon Qutrits
abstract: Quantum error correction using erasure qubits offers higher fault-tolerant thresholds and improved scaling by converting dominant physical errors into detectable erasures. In superconducting circuits, erasure qubits can be constructed using the dual-rail approach, which, however, requires additional qubit-count overhead and tailored coupling elements. Here, we demonstrate a hardware-efficient scheme that operates transmon qutrits as erasure qubits, which is compatible with standard superconducting circuit-QED hardware. The logical states $\ket{0_\text{L}}$ and $\ket{1_\text{L}}$ are represented by the ground and second excited states, while the dominant relaxation errors can be detected via an ancilla qubit using a microwave-activated two-qutrit SWAP gate. We demonstrate a logical qubit $T_1$ lifetime exceeding $500\,μ\mathrm{s}$, post-selected with repeated mid-circuit erasure detection, which is ten times longer than the $T_1$ time of the transmon physical qubit. Coherence times beyond $300\,μ\mathrm{s}$ are achieved using dynamical decoupling. Single-qubit gate operations reach average Clifford gate infidelity on the order of $10^{-4}$. We further demonstrate dual-purposing an ancilla qubit for both erasure detection and parity checking, showing heralded generation of Bell states between erasure qubits. These results suggest that mainstream architectures of transmon qubit arrays may already be capable of implementing erasure-based QEC strategies for hardware-efficient fault-tolerant quantum computing.
one_liner: Standard transmon qutrits are operated as erasure qubits, extending logical lifetimes past 500 us with mid-circuit erasure detection.
why_it_matters: It shows a low-overhead route to erasure-biased QEC using mainstream superconducting hardware rather than specialized dual-rail devices.
detailed_summary: The scheme encodes logical states in the |0> and |2> levels of a transmon qutrit, while dominant relaxation events are converted into detectable erasures using an ancilla-mediated microwave-activated two-qutrit SWAP gate. With repeated mid-circuit erasure detection and post-selection, the authors obtain a logical T1 above 500 us, roughly ten times the physical transmon T1. They also report coherence times beyond 300 us under dynamical decoupling and single-qubit Clifford infidelity on the order of 10^-4. In addition, the same ancilla is reused for both erasure detection and parity checking to herald Bell states between erasure qubits.
main_result: They realize hardware-efficient erasure qubits in standard circuit-QED transmons with long logical lifetime, low gate error, and heralded entanglement generation.
application_angle: This makes erasure-based fault tolerance more deployable on existing superconducting-chip stacks without the qubit-count overhead of dual-rail encodings.

title: The MQT Compiler Collection: A Blueprint for a Future-Proof Quantum-Classical Compilation Framework
abstract: As the capabilities of quantum computing hardware continue to rise, algorithms that exploit them are becoming increasingly complex. These developments increase the need for sophisticated compilation frameworks that translate high-level algorithms into executable code. In the past, most solutions were built with a quantum-first approach and handled mostly pure quantum programs without classical elements such as structured control flow. However, developments in quantum algorithms, error correction, and optimization, as well as the integration into high-performance computing (HPC) environments, depend on such classical elements. As quantum-first approaches increasingly struggle to handle these concepts, classical-first approaches are becoming a promising alternative. In this work, we present the MQT Compiler Collection, a blueprint for a future-proof quantum-classical compilation framework built on the Multi-Level Intermediate Representation (MLIR). After years of experience with the quantum-first approach and its shortcomings, we propose a framework that embraces core MLIR concepts to support the full compilation pipeline from high-level algorithms to hardware-specific instructions. The proposed architecture is designed from the ground up to support complex optimizations beyond, e.g., simple gate cancellation. It is publicly available at https://github.com/munich-quantum-toolkit/core.
one_liner: The MQT Compiler Collection proposes an MLIR-based quantum-classical compilation stack spanning high-level code down to hardware-specific instructions.
why_it_matters: Scalable quantum systems need compilers that handle structured classical control, optimization, and backend targeting in one coherent framework.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a compilation blueprint built on MLIR rather than a quantum-first IR stack, motivated by the increasing need to support classical control flow, error correction logic, and HPC integration. The architecture is intended to cover the full toolchain from high-level algorithm representation to hardware-specific instruction generation. The authors position it as a foundation for richer optimizations than simple gate cancellation, with explicit support for mixed quantum-classical programs. A public implementation is released through the Munich Quantum Toolkit core repository.
main_result: The main result is a concrete, open-source compiler architecture that unifies high-level quantum-classical programs with backend-aware lowering and optimization.
application_angle: This is practically relevant for deploying real workloads on heterogeneous quantum hardware where control flow, calibration constraints, and device-specific compilation all matter.

title: Arqon: A suite of control applications enabling a reliable quantum network
abstract: A quantum network's purpose is to enable users to execute applications on end nodes. This requires the network to provide the service of creating entangled links between those nodes. Users of mature networks, such as the internet or the telephone network expect accepted service demands to be met reliably. We first define reliability requirements that extend classical computer network concepts to quantum network service delivery. We then introduce Arqon, a suite of control applications designed to deliver reliable service in centrally controlled quantum networks. We demonstrate through both analytic and numerical evaluation that Arqon satisfies all reliability requirements for accepted demands. These evaluations consider static network topologies. We provide a complete Python implementation and perform complexity analysis showing that admission control scales as $O(k^3)$ in the number of incoming demands $k$ and schedule computation scales as ${O(N^3)}$ in the number of accepted demands to schedule $N$.
one_liner: Arqon is a control-application suite for centrally managed quantum networks that guarantees reliable delivery of accepted entanglement-service demands.
why_it_matters: Reliability is a prerequisite for useful quantum networking, and this work turns that requirement into concrete control software with scaling analysis.
detailed_summary: The paper defines reliability requirements for quantum-network service delivery by extending classical networking notions to entanglement-generation services between end nodes. It then introduces Arqon, a suite of control applications that performs admission control and scheduling in centrally controlled quantum networks. Analytical and numerical evaluations on static topologies show that accepted demands satisfy the stated reliability requirements. The authors also provide a full Python implementation and complexity analysis, with O(k^3) admission control in the number of incoming demands and O(N^3) schedule computation in the number of accepted demands.
platform_or_system: Centrally controlled quantum network control stack
main_result: They demonstrate a software control suite that reliably serves accepted entanglement demands and quantify its computational scaling for network operation.
application_angle: The work is directly useful for building operational quantum-network controllers that can offer dependable service guarantees to applications and users.

title: Immiscible to miscible quenching instabilities in two-dimensional binary Bose-Einstein condensates
