abstract: Immiscible to miscible quenching transitions (IMQT) in homogeneous Bose-Einstein condensate are investigated, considering rubidium isotopes $^{85}$Rb and $^{87}$Rb confined in a two-dimensional (2D) circular box, under two different initial configurations. These IMQT instabilities, triggered by sudden reductions in the two-body interspecies scattering length $a_{12}$, are explored under two distinct initialconditions, highlighting the critical role of nonlinear dynamics in their evolution. The numerical simulations indicate that the instability dynamics are primarily driven by the production of large vortices and the propagation of sound waves (phonons), with sound wave excitations prevailing in the long-term evolution. The compressible and incompressible parts of the kinetic energy spectra, in terms of the wave number $k$, are confronted with the classical Kolmogorov scaling, $k^{-5/3}$ for turbulence, which is observed in the onset of instabilities. Before reaching the ultraviolet dissipation region at small scales, the IMQT spectra exhibit a bottleneck effect, indicating a clear departure from classical scaling behavior. In the time asymptotic miscible regime, it is observed that the vorticity and sound-wave production remain practically stable. In this regime, for both cases investigated, a linear relation is also recognized between the miscibility parameter and the initial IMQT configuration.
one_liner: 2D Rb-85/Rb-87 condensates show quench-driven vortex and phonon dynamics during immiscible-to-miscible transitions.
why_it_matters: It clarifies nonequilibrium instability and turbulence signatures in binary condensates, informing controlled quantum-fluid dynamics experiments.
detailed_summary: The paper numerically studies sudden reductions of the interspecies scattering length in two-dimensional homogeneous binary Bose-Einstein condensates of Rb-85 and Rb-87 confined in a circular box. The resulting immiscible-to-miscible quench instability is dominated initially by large-vortex generation and sound-wave emission, with phonons governing the longer-time evolution. Compressible and incompressible kinetic-energy spectra show transient Kolmogorov-like k^-5/3 behavior at instability onset and then a bottleneck before ultraviolet dissipation. In the asymptotic miscible regime, vorticity and sound production stabilize and the miscibility parameter scales linearly with the initial configuration.
main_result: Quenches in interspecies interaction drive reproducible vortex-phonon instability dynamics with identifiable turbulence spectra and stable late-time miscible behavior.
application_angle: The results are relevant for engineering controlled quantum-fluid dynamics and nonequilibrium matter-wave platforms used in atom-optics and quantum simulation.

title: Frequency resolved optical gating using parametric amplification for characterizing ultrafast temporally multimode squeezed states
abstract: Temporally multimode squeezed states have been a topic of recent interest due to their applications in quantum communication, information processing, and sensing. Characterizing the mode shapes is crucial for effectively manipulating these states, but current mode shape and state characterization techniques necessitate constraining assumptions and complicated experimental setups. Here, we propose a characterization technique that simultaneously recovers the complex temporal mode shapes and quadrature variances of ultrafast multimode squeezed states based on frequency resolved optical gating (FROG) using an optical parametric amplifier (OPA). FROG is a promising tool for quantum state characterization due to its flexibility of implementation and high temporal resolution. Using an OPA as the nonlinear process in FROG has the benefit of amplifying weak quantum states to a detectable level while preserving quantum information. Numerical simulations demonstrate the recovery of the mode shapes and levels of squeezing and anti-squeezing of ultrafast multimode squeezed states. This scheme offers a practical experimental approach to measuring arbitrary temporal mode shapes and characterizing large-scale multimode ultrafast Gaussian quantum states.
one_liner: An OPA-based FROG scheme retrieves temporal modes and squeezing levels of ultrafast multimode squeezed states.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical route to fully characterize complex multimode Gaussian photonic states needed for scalable quantum communication and sensing.
detailed_summary: The authors propose frequency-resolved optical gating with an optical parametric amplifier as the nonlinear element to characterize ultrafast temporally multimode squeezed states. The OPA boosts weak quantum signals while preserving the information needed to reconstruct complex temporal mode shapes together with quadrature variances. Numerical simulations show recovery of both squeezing and anti-squeezing across arbitrary temporal modes without restrictive assumptions used in many existing methods. The approach targets large-scale ultrafast Gaussian states where flexible, high-time-resolution diagnostics are otherwise difficult.
main_result: The method simultaneously reconstructs complex temporal mode profiles and quadrature variances of multimode squeezed states using OPA-based FROG.
application_angle: Better state diagnostics can enable mode-matched quantum links, photonic processors, and multimode quantum sensors with higher usable state complexity.

title: Unlocking the O-Band: high-power, broadband soliton microcomb
abstract: The O-band (1260-1360 nm), located near the minimum of chromatic dispersion of standard single-mode fiber, is the transmission window of major interest and importance for short-reach data-center interconnects. However, full capacity offered by this spectral band is yet to be unlocked, due to limited availability of scalable multi-wavelength, high-power, low noise O-band light engines. While Kerr microcombs in CMOS-compatible silicon nitride resonators provide mutually coherent wavelength channels with precise spacing and chip-scale footprints, their practical deployment in the O-band has been hindered by limited pump laser power, insufficient per-line power and the lack of flat, wideband amplification technologies to uniformly boost multiple coherent carriers. Here we demonstrate a high-power O-band soliton microcomb architecture that overcomes this bottleneck by combining self-injection-locked (SIL) operation in a Silicon Nitride microring with a single-stage bismuth-doped phosphosilicate fiber amplifier designed for wideband, flat-top gain. The SIL microcomb operates with an 834 GHz free spectral range and spans over 1050-1650 nm. The amplifier simultaneously boosts 21 O-band lines across 100 nm to powers exceeding 0 dBm per carrier without gain flattening or external equalization, while preserving low-noise characteristics. We validate each amplified microcomb line as a carrier across the entire O-band using dual-polarization 32 GBaud 64-QAM coherent transmission. This approach establishes a practical route towards high-power, broadband O-band microcomb engines for next-generation data-center interconnects and scalable photonic systems.
one_liner: A self-injection-locked SiN microcomb plus Bi-doped amplifier delivers high-power coherent O-band carriers for data links.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical bottleneck for scalable O-band coherent light engines in short-reach optical interconnects and integrated photonics.
detailed_summary: This work demonstrates an O-band soliton microcomb architecture combining self-injection-locked operation in a silicon nitride microring with a single-stage bismuth-doped phosphosilicate fiber amplifier. The comb has an 834 GHz free spectral range and spans 1050-1650 nm, while the amplifier boosts 21 O-band lines over 100 nm to above 0 dBm per carrier without external gain flattening or equalization. Low-noise performance is preserved after amplification. The authors validate every amplified line using dual-polarization 32 GBaud 64-QAM coherent transmission across the full O-band.
main_result: The system produces 21 amplified, low-noise O-band carriers above 0 dBm per line and verifies them in coherent 64-QAM transmission.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to compact multiwavelength engines for next-generation data-center interconnects and other scalable photonic communication systems.

title: Homoclinic and heteroclinic solutions of the nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a complex Wadati potential
abstract: Stationary solutions asymptoting to nonlinear plane waves of the nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a PT-symmetric, complex linear potential are characterized. The potential includes both a spatially varying gain-loss profile and a repulsive real part, generated by a Wadati potential function,that support the existence of homoclinic and heteroclinic solutions that asymptote to the same or different, respectively, nonlinear plane waves in the far field. Asymptotic analysis and numerical simulations are used to examine solution existence, bifurcations, and structure. Such solutions play an important role in resonant nonlinear wave generation of dispersive media with localized gain and loss.
one_liner: PT-symmetric Wadati potentials support homoclinic and heteroclinic nonlinear Schrödinger wave states on finite backgrounds.
why_it_matters: It sharpens design rules for gain-loss-engineered nonlinear optical media that can host robust resonant wave structures.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes stationary nonlinear Schrödinger solutions on nonlinear plane-wave backgrounds in the presence of a complex PT-symmetric Wadati potential. Using asymptotic analysis and numerical simulations, it identifies existence conditions, bifurcations, and structures for both homoclinic and heteroclinic solutions supported by localized gain-loss landscapes with a repulsive real component. These solutions asymptote to identical or distinct nonlinear plane waves in the far field. The study links the supported wave families to resonant nonlinear wave generation in dispersive media with spatially structured non-Hermitian potentials.
main_result: It characterizes when and how complex Wadati potentials sustain homoclinic and heteroclinic finite-background solutions of the nonlinear Schrödinger equation.
application_angle: The results can inform engineered photonic structures with tailored gain and loss for nonlinear wave control and resonant signal generation.

title: Investigation of coherence of niobium-based resonators enabled by a fast-sealing microwave cavity
abstract: Resonators and qubits with a niobium (Nb) base metal layer achieve some of the highest coherence times in superconducting quantum devices. The performance of such devices is often limited by loss associated with two-level systems, which are found primarily at material surfaces and interfaces. The metal-air (MA) interface is a major contributor to device loss. In this work, we develop a fast-sealing microwave cavity that enables devices to be placed under vacuum within five minutes of oxide removal, thereby significantly reducing the MA interface loss compared to common device processing and packaging approaches. Using coplanar stripline resonators, we demonstrate that devices sealed in such a cavity exhibit internal quality factors exceeding one million at single-photon power. After re-exposure to air, the devices show downward resonance frequency shifts and quality factor degradations, quantitatively consistent with a model of Nb oxide regrowth. The fast-sealing microwave cavity provides a practical and consistent method to mitigate MA interface loss and sustain high coherence in Nb devices, and establishes a controlled platform for studying metal oxide regrowth kinetics and dielectric properties, the understanding of which is critical to achieving high coherence in superconducting quantum devices.
one_liner: A fast-sealing microwave cavity preserves niobium resonator coherence by minimizing post-clean metal-air interface oxidation.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical fabrication-packaging method to reduce surface-loss channels that limit superconducting quantum hardware performance.
detailed_summary: The authors build a fast-sealing microwave cavity that places niobium devices under vacuum within five minutes of oxide removal, sharply reducing exposure of the metal-air interface. Measurements on coplanar stripline resonators show internal quality factors above 10^6 at single-photon power for sealed devices. Re-exposure to air causes resonance-frequency shifts and quality-factor degradation consistent with a quantitative niobium-oxide regrowth model. The platform therefore both improves coherence and enables controlled studies of oxide kinetics and dielectric loss.
main_result: Rapid post-clean vacuum sealing yields niobium resonators with internal Q exceeding one million and directly links later degradation to oxide regrowth.
application_angle: The technique is immediately useful for improving fabrication and packaging flows for longer-coherence superconducting qubits and resonators.

title: High-resolution long-range 3D single-photon imaging with a compact SPAD array
abstract: High-resolution three-dimensional imaging under photon-starved conditions remains challenging. Here, we demonstrate a high-resolution long-range 3D single-photon imaging system based on a digital micromirror device (DMD) and a compact 64 multiply 64 single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) array. By combining high-resolution spatial modulation with parallel time-resolved detection, the system extends the effective spatial sampling beyond the native detector format while preserving depth information through time-of-flight measurement. In outdoor experiments at a stand-off distance of 670 m, we achieved 3D reconstruction of natural targets with an effective spatial resolution of 256 multiply 256. These results validate the proposed method as an effective approach for high-resolution long-range 3D single-photon imaging using compact SPAD arrays.
one_liner: A DMD plus 64x64 SPAD array achieves long-range 3D single-photon imaging with effective 256x256 spatial resolution.
why_it_matters: It shows a compact route to high-resolution photon-starved depth imaging for remote sensing, navigation, and surveillance.
detailed_summary: This system combines high-resolution spatial modulation from a digital micromirror device with parallel time-resolved detection on a compact 64x64 SPAD array. The modulation increases effective spatial sampling beyond the native detector format while preserving depth through time-of-flight measurements. In outdoor tests at a 670 m stand-off distance, the setup reconstructs natural scenes in 3D with effective 256x256 spatial resolution. The work demonstrates that compact SPAD hardware can support substantially finer long-range depth imaging under photon-starved conditions.
main_result: The authors demonstrate 670 m outdoor 3D single-photon imaging with effective 256x256 resolution using only a compact 64x64 SPAD array.
application_angle: This architecture is promising for practical long-range LiDAR and low-light 3D sensing where compact detectors and high depth fidelity are required.

title: Crosstalk-robust superconducting two-qubit geometric gates using tunable couplers
abstract: The design of coupler-based superconducting two-qubit gates simplifies circuit layout and alleviate frequency crowding, thereby enhancing the scalability and flexibility of quantum chips. However, in such architectures, a trade-off often exists between suppressing crosstalk and reducing gate duration, and how to achieve synergistic optimization of both remains an open challenge. To address this, this paper proposes a coupler-assisted superconducting two-qubit geometric gate scheme oriented towards crosstalk robustness. By introducing additional parametric degrees of freedom, the scheme steers the system evolution along desired trajectories, thereby flexibly avoiding crosstalk-sensitive operational regions. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme can effectively suppress crosstalk errors while enabling fast gate operations, and exhibits strong robustness against typical experimental imperfections such as qubit frequency drift. Moreover, even when accounting for unavoidable high-frequency oscillation terms and qubit decoherence in realistic physical systems, our crosstalk-robust two-qubit geometric gates still achieve high fidelity. This work provides a feasible pathway toward robust and efficient two-qubit gate implementation in superconducting quantum computation.
one_liner: A tunable-coupler gate protocol uses geometric control paths to suppress crosstalk while keeping superconducting two-qubit gates fast.
why_it_matters: High-fidelity, crosstalk-tolerant two-qubit gates directly address a central scaling bottleneck in dense superconducting quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a coupler-assisted superconducting two-qubit geometric gate that adds parametric control degrees of freedom to steer the evolution away from crosstalk-sensitive regions of Hilbert space. Numerical simulations indicate the approach simultaneously reduces crosstalk and preserves short gate duration, addressing the common tradeoff in tunable-coupler architectures. The authors further test robustness against qubit frequency drift, high-frequency oscillatory terms, and decoherence, finding that realistic high-fidelity operation remains achievable.
main_result: It demonstrates, in simulation, high-fidelity geometric two-qubit gates that remain robust to crosstalk and common superconducting-device imperfections.
application_angle: The scheme is directly relevant to scalable superconducting chips where dense integration demands fast entangling gates without sacrificing isolation.

title: Complexity-Aware Theory Testing from Bell Witnesses
abstract: Bell statistical-strength analyses and complexity-based model selection are usually treated separately. Here we relate them by showing that a witness obtained from a coarse-graining of full Bell trials yields, through data processing, a lower bound on the Kullback-Leibler (KL) distance to a competitor class in terms of the induced witness distribution. For binary Bell-game witnesses this reduces to a Bernoulli bound, and in the CHSH scenario the local image collapses to a single threshold, giving the closed-form expression D_KL(Bern(omega) || Bern(3/4)) under uniform inputs, with a corresponding extension to known nonuniform designs. A finite-sample Hoeffding argument gives a lower confidence bound under independent trials. We also include a non-CHSH example based on the three-party Mermin-GHZ game. Because the bound is measured in bits per trial, it can be compared directly with an MDL/BIC-type complexity penalty and thereby yields a conservative crossover criterion for when a more expressive competitor becomes worthwhile. For the reproducible four-photon data of Wang et al., the witness certifies a positive information gap against locality, while a full-table comparison across local, no-signaling, saturated, and two compact nonlocal families favors low-dimensional nonlocal descriptions once complexity is charged. A four-parameter unbiased-correlator control shows that the data support compact nonlocality over locality, while only weakly distinguishing the specific cosine structure of the two-parameter model; an AIC comparison instead favors broader nonlocal controls. We also report witness-based benchmarks from additional published CHSH experiments and discuss the interpretational scope of BIC for constrained or non-regular model classes.
one_liner: A Bell-witness framework converts experimental nonlocality data into complexity-aware statistical evidence against competing physical models.
why_it_matters: It gives photonic Bell tests a practical way to compare evidential strength and model complexity, improving how experimental claims are validated.
detailed_summary: The work links Bell statistical-strength analysis to complexity-based model selection by deriving lower bounds on KL distance from coarse-grained Bell witnesses. In binary Bell-game settings, including CHSH under uniform inputs, the bound reduces to a closed-form Bernoulli divergence, and finite-sample confidence bounds are obtained via Hoeffding arguments. Applied to reproducible four-photon data and other published CHSH experiments, the method finds a positive information gap against locality and supports compact nonlocal models once complexity penalties are included.
main_result: It provides a witness-based KL-distance bound, measured in bits per trial, that enables conservative complexity-aware discrimination between local and nonlocal model classes.
application_angle: This can strengthen certification workflows in quantum communication and photonic nonlocality experiments by making theory comparison more statistically disciplined.

title: Observing complementary Lucas sequences using non-Hermitian zero modes
abstract: The Lucas sequences are integers defined by a homogeneous recurrence relation. They include the well-known Fibonacci numbers, which appear abundantly in nature. The complementary Lucas numbers, defined by the same recurrence relation, are less well-known. In this work, we show that a special case of such complementary Lucas sequences can be observed on the same physical platform. It consists of a gain-and-loss-modulated non-Hermitian reservoir bridging two mirror-symmetric systems, which manifests the Lucas sequences in linearly localized edge states and a constant-intensity mode, respectively.
one_liner: Non-Hermitian photonic zero modes are used to realize complementary Lucas sequences in localized edge states and constant-intensity modes.
why_it_matters: It shows how engineered gain-loss photonic structures can encode mathematically structured modal behavior in experimentally accessible optical platforms.
detailed_summary: The authors study a gain-and-loss-modulated non-Hermitian reservoir connecting two mirror-symmetric systems and show that its zero-mode structure reproduces complementary Lucas sequences. In this setting, one sequence appears in linearly localized edge states while another is encoded in a constant-intensity mode. The result extends the use of non-Hermitian photonic architectures beyond transport and localization into direct observation of recurrence-defined integer structures.
main_result: It demonstrates that complementary Lucas sequences can be physically manifested on a single non-Hermitian optical platform through distinct zero-mode families.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for programmable photonic lattices and analog optical simulators that map discrete mathematical structures onto robust mode profiles.

title: Impact of Pump Phase-Noise on Josephson Traveling-Wave Parametric Amplifiers
abstract: Superconducting traveling-wave parametric amplifiers (TWPAs) are essential elements for enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and thus the read-out fidelity of superconducting qubits because of their high gain and near quantum-limited noise. However, the impact of the pump source, e.g., phase noise on these amplifiers, has not yet been studied. In this work, we show that among the two amplification processes in JTWPAs, the three-wave mixing (3WM) process is more sensitive to the pump phase noise than the four-wave mixing (4WM) process. We show that the even-order nonlinearity of 4th order and above in three-wave mixing is responsible for more than 10 dB increase of phase noise at high frequency offsets within the phase noise mask as the power of the pump increases. A polynomial model of the amplifier and cyclo-stationary property of phase noise also corroborate with the simulations. The Harmonic Balance (HB) periodic noise analysis tool and Leeson phase noise model in Keysight Advanced Design System (ADS) simulator were used in this study.
one_liner: This study quantifies how pump phase noise degrades Josephson TWPA performance, with three-wave mixing shown to be markedly more vulnerable than four-wave mixing.
why_it_matters: Readout amplifiers set superconducting-qubit measurement fidelity, so understanding pump-noise transfer is important for deployable quantum hardware stacks.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes the effect of pump phase noise on Josephson traveling-wave parametric amplifiers used for superconducting-qubit readout. Using harmonic-balance periodic noise simulations and a Leeson phase-noise model in ADS, it finds that 3WM architectures are more sensitive than 4WM ones. In particular, higher even-order nonlinearities in 3WM can drive more than 10 dB phase-noise increase at high offset frequencies as pump power rises, consistent with a polynomial amplifier model and cyclostationary phase-noise analysis.
main_result: It identifies and quantifies a strong excess sensitivity of 3WM JTWPAs to pump phase noise, including >10 dB noise growth from higher-order nonlinear effects.
application_angle: The results inform amplifier and pump-source design choices for low-noise superconducting-qubit readout chains and cryogenic microwave instrumentation.

title: A scalable platform for nanometer-scale quantum confinement
abstract: Overcoming the limitations of current nanofabrication techniques to achieve nanoscale feature sizes is essential for achieving new regimes of light-matter interactions at extreme frequencies and length scales. Here, we demonstrate a scalable nanofabrication platform capable of producing in-plane feature sizes down to 1.75 nm, pushing the boundaries of current top-down nanofabrication techniques. Using precise thickness control of atomic layer deposition (ALD) and employing widely spaced oxide nanofins, we transform conventional ALD into a surface structuring method that produces nanolaminates with sub-10 nm periodicities over large areas. The resulting nanostructures can be used as a one-dimensional gate array to control charge carriers in two-dimensional materials. As an initial demonstration, we integrate the platform with graphene and perform electron transport measurements. In the presence of the gate array enabled by the nanolaminate, we observe satellite Dirac peaks consistent with band-structure modulation, suggestive of quantum-confinement effects. Our platform paves the way for exploring previously inaccessible regimes of nanoscale light-matter interactions, holding significant promise for applications in short wavelength optics, electronics, and polaritonics.
one_liner: A scalable ALD-based nanofabrication method achieves 1.75 nm in-plane features and forms dense gate arrays that modulate graphene band structure.
why_it_matters: Sub-2 nm, large-area patterning could unlock new light-matter and carrier-confinement regimes relevant to quantum and nanophotonic devices.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a top-down-compatible nanofabrication platform that uses atomic layer deposition and oxide nanofins to create nanolaminates with sub-10 nm periodicity and feature sizes down to 1.75 nm. Integrated with graphene, the resulting one-dimensional gate array produces satellite Dirac peaks in transport measurements, consistent with band-structure modulation and suggestive of quantum confinement. The combination of precise thickness control and large-area scalability makes the approach notable for extreme nanoscale device engineering.
platform_or_system: ALD nanolaminate gate arrays on graphene
main_result: It demonstrates scalable fabrication of 1.75 nm features and uses the resulting gate superlattice to induce measurable graphene band-structure modulation.
application_angle: This platform is promising for ultrasmall quantum and photonic components where confinement, electrostatic patterning, and strong light-matter interaction must be engineered at nanometer scales.

title: Towards better nuclear charge radii
abstract: Nuclear charge radii constitute a physical observable of growing significance across multiple subdisciplines of physics and related fields. Their determination relies on a combination of complementary experimental techniques and advanced theoretical frameworks. Current recommended values are informed by the outcomes of several independent working groups, each employing distinct methodological approaches and evaluation strategies. The present effort is directed toward a more precise and reliable extraction of charge radii, as well as the development of a modern, transparent, and methodologically robust compilation of recommended values.
one_liner: This effort targets more precise and transparent recommended nuclear charge radii by combining complementary measurements with improved evaluation methods.
why_it_matters: Better charge-radius values strengthen atomic and nuclear metrology, with downstream impact on precision sensing and benchmark constants.
detailed_summary: The paper outlines a program to improve the extraction and compilation of nuclear charge radii by reconciling multiple experimental inputs with modern theoretical treatments. It emphasizes methodological transparency and robustness across independent evaluation strategies used by different working groups. Although it is a framework and compilation effort rather than a new sensor demonstration, it directly supports higher-precision interpretation of atom-based measurements.
main_result: It establishes a more rigorous path toward updated, precise, and transparent recommended nuclear charge radii values.
application_angle: More reliable radii improve calibration and interpretation in precision spectroscopy and related sensing workflows that depend on accurate nuclear-size inputs.

title: Loss-Tolerant Quantum Communication via Bosonic-GKP-Parity-Encoding
abstract: Quantum repeaters constitute a promising platform for enabling long distance quantum communication and may ultimately serve as the backbone of a secure quantum internet, a scalable quantum network, or a distributed quantum computer. An efficient approach to encoding qubits within an error-correcting code is provided by bosonic codes, in which even a single oscillator mode can function as a sufficiently large physical system. In this work, initially we focus on the bosonic Gottesman Kitaev Preskill (GKP) code as a natural candidate for loss correction based quantum repeaters, which can be implemented at room temperature. We demonstrate that transmission loss can be suppressed across three related protocols at the expense of the introduction of logical errors. The third protocol, where a relay-like teleamplifier is applied is optimal. This approach enables medium-distance quantum communication without requiring higher level encoding. We compute the resulting secure key rates while leveraging analog syndrome information. Furthermore, we propose a concatenated Bell state measurement (CBSM) scheme with a modified parity encoding based on GKP qubits, CV measurement and a clipping method that corrects transmission loss without introducing logical errors. This significantly enhances the possible transmission distance. We find that GKP based repeaters can achieve performance comparable to approaches relying on photonic qubits, while requiring orders of magnitude fewer qubits.
one_liner: Bosonic GKP and parity-encoded repeater protocols suppress transmission loss and extend secure quantum communication with far fewer qubits.
why_it_matters: It points to room-temperature, hardware-efficient repeaters that trade modest logical error overhead for much longer secure links.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes three loss-correction protocols for bosonic GKP qubits in repeater-style quantum communication, including a relay-like teleamplifier that is identified as optimal among the three. The authors compute secure key rates using analog syndrome information and show that medium-distance links are feasible without higher-level concatenated encoding. They then introduce a concatenated Bell-state measurement scheme with modified parity encoding, continuous-variable measurements, and clipping-based loss correction that avoids inducing logical errors. This extended scheme substantially increases achievable transmission distance while using orders of magnitude fewer qubits than photonic-qubit approaches.
main_result: The work shows that GKP-based repeater protocols can suppress channel loss and reach photonic-repeater-like performance with drastically reduced qubit overhead.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to practical quantum networks because it offers a route to medium- and longer-distance secure communication with compact bosonic hardware.

title: Tantalum-Encapsulated Niobium Superconducting Resonators: High Internal Quality Factor and Improved Temporal Stability via Surface Passivation
abstract: Superconducting coplanar waveguide resonators are essential components in quantum processors, where their internal quality factor (Qi) constrains qubit coherence and readout fidelity. In niobium devices, microwave losses at millikelvin temperatures are strongly influenced by two-level systems (TLS) associated with the complex NbOx surface oxide. To mitigate these losses, we investigate a surface-engineering approach in which Nb films are capped in situ with a thin tantalum layer to suppress Nb2O5 formation and replace the native NbOx interface with a Ta-based oxide. We fabricate Nb/Ta bilayer and reference Nb resonators on high-resistivity silicon using identical DC sputtering and wet etching conditions, and characterize their performance at millikelvin temperatures. Fresh Ta-encapsulated devices exhibit internal quality factors up to 2.4 x 10^6 in the near-single-photon regime, with power dependence consistent with reduced TLS-related loss at the metal-air interface. A control Nb device fabricated under the same process shows comparatively lower Q_TLS, consistent with the beneficial effect of the Ta capping layer. Furthermore, ageing tests performed on Nb/Ta resonators after six months reveal a moderate reduction in Q_TLS relative to their initial values, yet the performance remains superior to newly fabricated Nb-only devices. These results suggest that thin Ta encapsulation enhances interface quality and contributes to improved temporal stability while remaining compatible with Nb-based fabrication workflows.
one_liner: In situ tantalum capping of niobium resonators yields higher single-photon Qi and better long-term stability by mitigating surface-oxide loss.
why_it_matters: Surface passivation that fits standard Nb fabrication can improve coherence-critical microwave components without a major process redesign.
detailed_summary: The authors fabricate coplanar-waveguide resonators from niobium with a thin in situ tantalum encapsulation layer, designed to suppress lossy Nb2O5 formation and replace the native interface with a Ta-based oxide. Millikelvin measurements show fresh Nb/Ta devices reaching internal quality factors up to 2.4 x 10^6 in the near-single-photon regime, with power dependence consistent with reduced two-level-system loss at the metal-air interface. Control Nb resonators made in the same process exhibit lower TLS-limited quality factors, supporting the passivation mechanism. Ageing tests after six months show some degradation, but capped devices still outperform newly made Nb-only references.
main_result: Tantalum-encapsulated niobium resonators achieve up to 2.4 x 10^6 internal quality factor and retain superior performance over Nb-only devices after ageing.
application_angle: Higher-Q, more stable resonators directly benefit superconducting qubit coherence, readout fidelity, and manufacturable quantum-processor microwave packaging.

title: Continuous Quantum Aperture: Beamforming with a Single-Vapor-Cell Rydberg Receiver
abstract: Beamforming is conventionally understood as a collective property of many discrete antenna elements in both communication and radar fields, which links angular selectivity to array size, element spacing, and band-specific hardware. Here we uncover a fundamentally different beamforming mechanism achieved by a Rydberg atomic receiver: a Rydberg-atom vapor cell dressed by a local-oscillator field constitutes a continuous quantum aperture. In this regime, spatially-varying quantum coherence across the aperture provides continuous amplitude-phase control, allowing a directional beam pattern to emerge from one sensing volume rather than from an engineered array. We establish the theory of continuous quantum aperture and show that tailoring the local-oscillator field can directly program the aperture response. This enables reconfigurable single-peak, multipeak, and multiband beamforming within a single vapor cell. Experiments on a Rydberg atomic receiver prototype verify that practical beam patterns agree with theoretical predictions across aperture sizes, frequency bands, and local-oscillator configurations. Leveraging this new beamforming mechanism, we further demonstrate interference mitigation, multiuser access, and multiband multiuser access with the single-vapor-cell platform. Our results identify the continuous quantum aperture as a new operating principle of Rydberg atomic receivers and establish single-vapor-cell beamforming as an integrated and reconfigurable platform for spatially selective electromagnetic reception.
one_liner: A single Rydberg vapor cell acts as a continuous quantum aperture, enabling programmable beamforming without a discrete antenna array.
why_it_matters: It replaces complex array hardware with one reconfigurable atomic receiver that can steer, split, and multiplex spatial reception.
detailed_summary: This work introduces the concept of a continuous quantum aperture, where spatially varying quantum coherence across a Rydberg-atom vapor cell dressed by a local oscillator provides continuous amplitude and phase control. The theory predicts that shaping the local-oscillator field directly programs the beam pattern, enabling single-peak, multipeak, and multiband responses within one sensing volume. Prototype experiments validate the predicted patterns across different aperture sizes, frequency bands, and local-oscillator settings. The authors further demonstrate interference mitigation, multiuser access, and multiband multiuser access using the same single-cell receiver.
main_result: The paper experimentally demonstrates reconfigurable beamforming, including multipeak and multiband operation, from a single Rydberg vapor cell rather than a phased array.
application_angle: This could shrink and simplify RF front ends for communications and radar by delivering software-defined spatial selectivity in one atomic sensor package.

title: QuIKS: Near-Zero Latency Key Supply with Adaptive Buffering for Resource-Efficient Quantum Key Distribution Networks
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) networks provide information-theoretically secure keys for distant parties, emerging as a vital alternative to classical cryptography infrastructures threatened by quantum computing. In QKD networks, the immediacy of key supply service is crucial to the security and performance of applications, as their data must be encrypted before transmission. While key buffering can enable instant key supply services, existing schemes rely on heuristic solutions that incur prohibitive key resource consumption, thus significantly hindering practical deployment. To address this issue, we propose QuIKS, an instant key supply scheme based on adaptive buffering, offering the dominant advantage of near-zero key supply latency while consuming ultra-low key resources (i.e., ultra-low buffer size). Specifically, it is built upon a novel analytical model that determines the minimum buffer size required to guarantee near-zero-latency key supply performance. Guided by this model, QuIKS introduces a lightweight two-phase control algorithm that dynamically determines key relaying requests and adjusts the buffer size by probing real-time application patterns and network conditions. Experiments on a real QKD network testbed demonstrate that QuIKS achieves near-zero key supply latency while providing a more than 10-fold reduction in key buffer size compared to state-of-the-art schemes.
one_liner: QuIKS delivers near-zero-latency QKD key supply using adaptive buffering that cuts required buffer size by more than an order of magnitude.
why_it_matters: Reducing buffer overhead while preserving instant key availability addresses a core deployment bottleneck in operational QKD networks.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes QuIKS, a key-supply framework for QKD networks that targets near-zero service latency with minimal stored key material. It builds on an analytical model for the minimum buffer size needed to guarantee low-latency supply, then uses a lightweight two-phase control algorithm to adapt relaying requests and buffer size based on observed traffic and network conditions. Experiments on a real QKD testbed validate the model-driven strategy. Compared with prior schemes, QuIKS maintains near-zero latency while reducing key buffer size by more than 10x.
platform_or_system: QKD network buffering and control stack
main_result: QuIKS achieves near-zero key-supply latency on a real QKD testbed while requiring over 10-fold less buffering than state-of-the-art approaches.
application_angle: The scheme improves the practicality and economics of QKD deployment by lowering scarce-key storage demands without sacrificing service responsiveness.

title: Pontryagin's Principle for Leakage-Immune Adiabatic Quantum State Transfer
abstract: The standard stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) protocol enables high-fidelity quantum state transfer in an ideal three-level system via adiabatic following of a dark state evolution. However, in practical systems with more energy levels, control pulses with finite spectral selectivity often couple the three-level subspace to the remaining subspace, introducing leakage that fundamentally limits the transfer performance. Here, we adopt a multilevel chain model for STIRAP that explicitly incorporates this leakage subspace. Using Pontryagin's maximum principle, we formulate a leakage-penalized quantum optimal control problem with the control pulses constrained to experimentally feasible Gaussian pulse families. We derive explicit gradients of the objective functional with respect to the pulse parameters, enabling efficient low-dimensional optimization that suppresses leakage while preserving the counterintuitive STIRAP pulse ordering. Numerical simulations for a superconducting transmon platform demonstrate that the optimized control pulses can significantly enhance the target-state transfer fidelity and provide enhanced robustness to amplitude miscalibration and detuning drifts.
one_liner: Pontryagin-optimized Gaussian STIRAP pulses suppress leakage in multilevel transmons and improve robust adiabatic state transfer fidelity.
why_it_matters: It makes adiabatic transfer protocols more realistic for hardware with spectral crowding, where leakage often limits usable fidelity.
detailed_summary: The authors extend STIRAP to a multilevel-chain model that explicitly captures unwanted coupling from the intended three-level manifold into leakage states. They formulate a leakage-penalized optimal-control problem over experimentally feasible Gaussian pulse families and derive explicit gradients with respect to pulse parameters using Pontryagin's maximum principle. Numerical optimization preserves the counterintuitive STIRAP ordering while reducing leakage. Simulations for superconducting transmons show higher target-state transfer fidelity together with improved robustness against amplitude calibration errors and detuning drift.
main_result: Optimized Gaussian STIRAP pulses substantially reduce leakage and improve fidelity and robustness of adiabatic state transfer in a realistic transmon model.
application_angle: The method can be integrated into superconducting control stacks to realize more reliable state transfer and gate subroutines under hardware constraints.

title: Thermodynamical aspects of optically pumped dense atomic medium
abstract: Optically Pumped Magnetometers use light to drive an atomic vapor into a Non-Equilibrium Steady State for sensing. This kind of state is achieved when spin-exchange collisions, together with optical pumping, dominate the relaxation dynamics, redistributing the atomic populations and thereby shaping the steady-state configuration. Despite the rapid advancement of atomic magnetometer technology, a comprehensive thermodynamic analysis of the state preparation is largely unexplored. We apply a thermodynamic framework to alkali atoms in a vapor cell, modeling their interactions with the pump laser and their relaxation via spin-exchange and spin-destruction collisions. We analyze how the pump rate and light polarization determine the non-equilibrium steady state, quantifying irreversibility via entropy production, assessing useful energy via ergotropy, and defining the spin-polarization efficiency. Finally, we establish a connection between metrological performance and the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), demonstrating that a higher thermodynamic efficiency directly translates into an improved fundamental bound on magnetometer sensitivity. These results provide insights for optimizing state preparation in quantum sensors.
one_liner: A thermodynamic framework links optical pumping efficiency in dense atomic vapors to quantum-limited magnetometer sensitivity.
why_it_matters: It gives sensor designers a principled way to tune pumping and collisions for better sensitivity rather than relying only on empirical optimization.
detailed_summary: This paper analyzes dense alkali-vapor state preparation in optically pumped magnetometers as a non-equilibrium thermodynamic process driven by laser pumping and relaxed by spin-exchange and spin-destruction collisions. The authors quantify irreversibility through entropy production, characterize useful stored energy via ergotropy, and define a spin-polarization efficiency for the steady state. They then connect these thermodynamic quantities to metrological performance using the quantum Fisher information. The analysis shows that higher thermodynamic efficiency corresponds to a tighter fundamental bound on magnetic-field sensitivity.
platform_or_system: Optically pumped dense alkali vapor cell
main_result: The work establishes a direct relation between thermodynamic efficiency of optical pumping and the quantum Fisher information that bounds magnetometer sensitivity.
application_angle: This framework can guide operating-point optimization in practical atomic magnetometers for improved sensitivity and energy-efficient state preparation.

title: Quadratic Quantum Polarimetry with Entangled Photon Pairs
abstract: Conventional polarimetry, including schemes leveraging entangled light, characterizes optical samples through linear transformations of polarization states. We introduce a two-photon probing approach in which both photons of an entangled pair interact with the same depolarizing medium simultaneously. In this regime, the transformation of the two-photon polarization correlations becomes quadratic in the Mueller matrix, enabling access to second-order polarization information beyond conventional polarimetry. We develop a theoretical framework linking the Mueller matrix to the evolution of the two-photon polarization correlation tensor and show that depolarization induces quadratic degradation of entanglement and state purity. Experiments using polarization-entangled photon pairs transmitted through controlled scattering media confirm the predicted response and reveal enhanced sensitivity to polarization scrambling compared with single-photon probing. These results establish two-photon probing as a higher-order quantum polarimetric modality for characterizing polarization channels.
one_liner: Entangled photon pairs probing the same sample enable quadratic polarimetry that reveals higher-order depolarization effects.
why_it_matters: It introduces a directly measurable higher-order polarization probe that is more sensitive to scrambling than standard single-photon polarimetry.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a two-photon polarimetry framework where both photons of an entangled pair traverse the same depolarizing channel, making the output polarization-correlation tensor depend quadratically on the sample Mueller matrix. This exposes second-order polarization information inaccessible to conventional linear polarimetry. The authors show theoretically that depolarization causes quadratic loss of entanglement and purity, then verify the predicted response using polarization-entangled photon pairs sent through controlled scattering media. Experiments indicate stronger sensitivity to polarization scrambling than comparable single-photon probes.
platform_or_system: polarization-entangled photon pairs in scattering media
main_result: The work demonstrates and experimentally validates quadratic quantum polarimetry with enhanced sensitivity to depolarization and polarization scrambling.
application_angle: The approach could improve characterization of complex optical channels and depolarizing samples in imaging, biophotonics, and polarization-sensitive communications.

title: SatQNet: Satellite-assisted Quantum Network Entanglement Routing Using Directed Line Graph Neural Networks
abstract: Quantum networks are expected to become a key enabler for interconnecting quantum devices. In contrast to classical communication networks, however, information transfer in quantum networks is usually restricted to short distances due to physical constraints of entanglement distribution. Satellites can extend entanglement distribution over long distances, but routing in such networks is challenging because satellite motion and stochastic link generation create a highly dynamic quantum topology. Existing routing methods often rely on global topology information that quickly becomes outdated due to delays in the classical control plane, while decentralized methods typically act on incomplete local information. We propose SatQNet, a reinforcement learning approach for entanglement routing in satellite-assisted quantum networks that can be decentralized at runtime. Its key innovation is an edge-centric directed line graph neural network that performs local message passing on directed edge embeddings, enabling it to better capture link properties in high-degree and time-varying topologies. By exchanging messages with neighboring repeaters, SatQNet learns a local graph representation at runtime that supports agents in establishing high-fidelity end-to-end entanglements. Trained on random graphs, SatQNet outperforms heuristic and learning-based approaches across diverse settings, including a real-world European backbone topology, and generalizes to unseen topologies without retraining.
one_liner: A directed line-graph neural routing method improves decentralized entanglement delivery in dynamic satellite quantum networks.
why_it_matters: It targets a core deployment bottleneck for global quantum internet architectures: routing entanglement under rapidly changing satellite links.
detailed_summary: SatQNet uses reinforcement learning with an edge-centric directed line graph neural network so routing agents reason over directed link embeddings rather than only node states. That design is intended to better capture asymmetric, time-varying link behavior caused by satellite motion and stochastic entanglement generation. Agents exchange local messages with neighboring repeaters at runtime, enabling decentralized decisions without requiring globally fresh topology information. Across random graphs and a European backbone topology, the method reportedly outperforms heuristic and prior learning baselines and generalizes to unseen networks without retraining.
main_result: The method achieves higher-fidelity end-to-end entanglement routing than competing approaches in dynamic satellite-assisted network scenarios.
application_angle: This is relevant for scalable long-distance quantum communication, where practical routing must remain robust despite delayed control-plane information and moving satellite links.

title: Tailoring tensor network techniques to the quantics representation for highly inhomogeneous problems and few body problems
abstract: Tensor network techniques are becoming increasingly popular tools to solve partial differential equations within the so-called quantics representation. Their popularity stems from the fact that their spatial resolution depends only logarithmically on the number of grid points, making them very tempting approaches in situations where two or more characteristic length scales are vastly different. A first generation of technique used ``out-of-the-box'' algorithms of the tensor network toolkit (e.g. the celebrated Density Matrix Product State (DMRG) algorithm) to solve these problems. These techniques were designed for situations (e.g. quantum magnetism) where the different degrees of freedom (e.g. spins) play equivalent roles. In the quantics representation, however, the different degrees of freedom correspond to the physics at different scales and therefore play inequivalent role. Here we show that by tailoring the tensor network algorithms to this particular case, in the spirit of the multigrid approach, we obtain faster and more robust convergence of the algorithms. We showcase the approach on linear (Poisson equation) and eigenvalue (Schrödinger equation) problems in two, three and four dimensions. Our simulations involve up to $2^{80}$ grid points and would represent, we argue, a very strong challenge for conventional approaches.
one_liner: Customized quantics tensor-network solvers accelerate multiscale PDE and few-body quantum calculations on enormous grids.
why_it_matters: It improves simulation efficiency for highly inhomogeneous quantum and wave problems that are otherwise prohibitive with conventional discretizations.
detailed_summary: The authors adapt tensor-network algorithms specifically to the quantics representation, where tensor degrees of freedom correspond to different spatial scales rather than equivalent sites. By tailoring updates in a multigrid-like way instead of using generic DMRG-style routines, they obtain faster and more robust convergence on strongly inhomogeneous problems. Benchmarks include Poisson and Schrödinger equations in two to four dimensions, with simulations reaching effective grids up to 2^80 points. The method is particularly aimed at problems with widely separated length scales and few-body structure.
main_result: The paper shows that scale-aware quantics tensor-network algorithms solve large multiscale PDE and eigenvalue problems more efficiently and robustly than out-of-the-box tensor-network methods.
application_angle: Better multiscale solvers can support realistic modeling of quantum and photonic devices where extreme spatial resolution and disparate length scales are common.

title: Variational Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Hydrological PDE-Constrained Learning with Inherent Uncertainty Quantification
abstract: We propose a Hybrid Quantum-Classical Physics-Informed Neural Network (HQC-PINN) that integrates parameterized variational quantum circuits into the PINN framework for hydrological PDE-constrained learning. Our architecture encodes multi-source remote sensing features into quantum states via trainable angle encoding, processes them through a hardware-efficient variational ansatz with entangling layers, and constrains the output using the Saint-Venant shallow water equations and Manning's flow equation as differentiable physics loss terms. The inherent stochasticity of quantum measurement provides a natural mechanism for uncertainty quantification without requiring explicit Bayesian inference machinery. We further introduce a quantum transfer learning protocol that pre-trains on multi-hazard disaster data before fine-tuning on flood-specific events. Numerical simulations on multi-modal satellite and meteorological data from the Kalu River basin, Sri Lanka, show that the HQC-PINN achieves convergence in ~3x fewer training epochs and uses ~44% fewer trainable parameters compared to an equivalent classical PINN, while maintaining competitive classification accuracy. Theoretical analysis indicates that hydrological physics constraints narrow the effective optimization landscape, providing a natural mitigation against barren plateaus in variational quantum circuits. This work establishes the first application of quantum-enhanced physics-informed learning to hydrological prediction and demonstrates a viable path toward quantum advantage in environmental science.
one_liner: A hybrid variational quantum PINN is proposed for flood-model PDE learning with built-in uncertainty estimation.
why_it_matters: It links quantum machine learning with physics-constrained environmental prediction, aiming for lower training cost and native uncertainty outputs.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a hybrid quantum-classical PINN that encodes remote-sensing inputs into parameterized quantum states and processes them with a hardware-efficient variational circuit. Training is constrained by shallow-water Saint-Venant equations and Manning flow relations, while shot noise from quantum measurements is used as an intrinsic uncertainty signal. On Kalu River basin flood data, the authors report roughly 3x fewer training epochs and 44% fewer trainable parameters than a classical PINN with similar predictive accuracy. They also propose transfer learning from broader disaster datasets and argue the physics constraints help mitigate barren plateaus.
main_result: The proposed HQC-PINN attains competitive hydrological prediction with fewer parameters and faster convergence while yielding uncertainty estimates from quantum sampling.
application_angle: If borne out on hardware, the approach could make physics-constrained forecasting pipelines more efficient for remote sensing and disaster-response modeling.

title: Quantum Uncertainty and Entropy
abstract: We review the plethora of uncertainty relations that appear in quantum mechanics and their nuances. We present both foundational applications, e.g. in understanding and defining complementarity, and practical applications, e.g. in quantum metrology and cryptography. Both variance- and entropy-based uncertainties are covered here.
one_liner: A review surveys variance- and entropy-based uncertainty relations and their use in metrology and cryptography.
why_it_matters: It consolidates the theoretical tools that underpin precision sensing and security analyses across quantum technologies.
detailed_summary: This article reviews the landscape of quantum uncertainty relations, covering both variance-based and entropic formulations and clarifying their different operational meanings. It connects these relations to foundational notions such as complementarity as well as practical settings including metrology and cryptography. Rather than introducing a new experiment or device, the paper organizes the conceptual and mathematical structure behind uncertainty-based performance limits. The emphasis is on the nuances of different uncertainty measures and the contexts in which each is most useful.
main_result: The paper provides a consolidated treatment of modern quantum uncertainty and entropy relations relevant to sensing and secure information processing.
application_angle: It is useful as a reference for designing or analyzing metrological and cryptographic protocols that are fundamentally limited by uncertainty tradeoffs.

title: Generative Circuit Design for Quantum-Selected Configuration Interaction
abstract: Quantum-selected configuration interaction (QSCI) has emerged as a feasible approach for approximating electronic ground states on noisy quantum devices toward large-system demonstrations. In QSCI, Slater determinants are sampled from a quantum-prepared state, and the Hamiltonian is then diagonalized in the sampled subspace. To create a high-quality subspace under hardware constraints, the design of the state-preparation circuit is crucial. Here, we present a Generative Quantum Eigensolver (GQE)-based framework that optimizes ansatz structures using a Transformer policy trained on the QSCI subspace energy. We validate the framework on N2 in active spaces of up to 32 qubits. We found that the optimized circuits reach chemical precision with substantially lower gate counts than time-evolved circuits. Quantitatively, this corresponds to an average reduction of 98% in the required two-qubit gate count relative to the single-step first-order Trotterized approximation and 83% relative to the qDRIFT approximation. Furthermore, the resulting wavefunctions are competitive with heat-bath configuration interaction (HCI) in terms of compactness. In stretched-bond, strongly correlated regimes, they achieve chemical precision with subspaces that are 50% smaller than those required by HCI.
one_liner: A Transformer-guided generative eigensolver finds low-gate QSCI circuits that reach chemical accuracy on large active spaces.
why_it_matters: It directly addresses hardware efficiency in quantum chemistry by cutting two-qubit gate counts while preserving high-quality sampled subspaces.
detailed_summary: The paper uses a Generative Quantum Eigensolver framework in which a Transformer policy proposes ansatz circuit structures optimized against the QSCI subspace energy objective. Tested on N2 active spaces up to 32 qubits, the learned circuits produce compact determinant subspaces suitable for post-sampling diagonalization. Reported savings are substantial: average two-qubit gate counts drop by 98% relative to one-step first-order Trotter circuits and 83% relative to qDRIFT, while still reaching chemical precision. In stretched-bond strongly correlated regimes, the resulting subspaces are also about 50% smaller than those required by HCI for chemical accuracy.
platform_or_system: NISQ quantum chemistry circuits for QSCI
main_result: Transformer-optimized QSCI state-preparation circuits achieve chemical precision with dramatically fewer two-qubit gates and compact sampled subspaces.
application_angle: This makes near-term quantum chemistry experiments more viable on noisy hardware by reducing circuit depth without giving up chemically relevant accuracy.

title: Nonlocal Games Revisited: A Representation-Theoretic Path from Bell Locality to Quantum Pseudo-Telepathy
abstract: Nonlocal games provide a unified framework for studying the distinction between classical, quantum, and more general no-signaling correlations. In this work, we develop this perspective by connecting the Bell-locality framework to several complementary mathematical representations of nonlocal games and quantum strategies. We begin with local hidden-variable models, the CHSH inequality, and the role of Bell nonlocality as a device-independent witness of entanglement, and then introduce nonlocal games through the standard predicate/verifier formalism. We next examine a set of representative examples, including XOR games, the GHZ game, graph-based coloring games, the Mermin-Peres magic square game, and Hardy's paradox as a related logical manifestation of nonlocality. Building on this foundation, we compare four closely related representation frameworks: conditional-probability and correlation descriptions, Bell-functional formulations, entangled-value optimization, and the quantum-operator approach together with the Navascues-Pironio-Acin (NPA) hierarchy. These viewpoints are then instantiated for the CHSH, magic square, and GHZ games, showing how each representation emphasizes a different aspect of the same underlying task. Taken together, these examples show that nonlocal games can be studied simultaneously as geometric objects in correlation space, optimization problems over entangled resources, and operator-theoretic constructions. This multi-representation viewpoint clarifies the relation between Bell inequality violations, perfect quantum strategies, pseudo-telepathy, and semidefinite relaxations of quantum correlations.
one_liner: A representation-theoretic synthesis links Bell inequalities, nonlocal games, and operator relaxations for quantum pseudo-telepathy.
why_it_matters: It unifies the main mathematical lenses for nonlocal correlations, making device-independent communication and verification protocols easier to analyze and compare.
detailed_summary: The paper revisits nonlocal games by placing conditional probabilities, correlators, Bell functionals, entangled-value optimization, and operator-based formulations within one coherent framework. It works through CHSH, GHZ, magic-square, graph-coloring, and Hardy-type examples to show how the same task appears as a geometry problem in correlation space, an SDP-style optimization, or an operator constraint system. A key technical theme is clarifying how perfect quantum strategies and pseudo-telepathy emerge across these equivalent representations, with the NPA hierarchy providing systematic relaxations of the quantum set.
main_result: It provides a unified multi-representation account of nonlocal games that explicitly connects Bell-local models, quantum winning strategies, pseudo-telepathy, and NPA relaxations.
application_angle: The framework is relevant for device-independent photonic and quantum-network protocols where certifying nonclassical correlations is central.

title: Probing Electrostatic Disorder via g-Tensor Geometry
abstract: Low-frequency charge noise induced by fluctuating electrostatic disorder is a major limitation for semiconductor hole spin qubits. Here, we analyze the quasistatic response of a hole spin qubit to individual two-level fluctuators (TLFs). We show that, due to the anisotropy of the g-tensor, the qubit response depends on the geometry of the fluctuator-induced dipolar perturbation. We then propose a readout protocol that isolates selected g-tensor components through an accumulated Berry phase and estimate, within our readout model, an order-unity signal-to-noise ratio with a total protocol time in the tens of microseconds. Finally, using microscopic simulations, we compute the quantum Fisher information (QFI) to identify magnetic field directions and confinement regimes in which the qubit is most sensitive to disorder-induced variations of selected g-tensor components.
one_liner: A Berry-phase protocol uses g-tensor anisotropy to read out electrostatic disorder seen by semiconductor hole-spin qubits.
why_it_matters: It turns charge-noise sensitivity into a diagnostic tool, helping identify disorder sources and optimal operating points for higher-fidelity spin qubits.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze how individual two-level fluctuators perturb a hole-spin qubit through anisotropic changes in the g-tensor, making the response depend on fluctuator geometry. They propose a Berry-phase-based readout that selectively isolates chosen g-tensor components and estimate order-unity signal-to-noise with total acquisition times of only tens of microseconds. Microscopic simulations then use quantum Fisher information to identify magnetic-field orientations and confinement regimes that maximize sensitivity to disorder-induced parameter shifts.
main_result: The work shows that selected g-tensor components affected by electrostatic disorder can be resolved with a practical Berry-phase protocol and near-unit signal-to-noise.
application_angle: This offers a hardware-tuning and materials-diagnostics method for reducing low-frequency charge noise in scalable semiconductor qubit devices.

title: Sector length distributions of recursively definable graph states through analytic combinatorics
abstract: The sector length distribution or Shor-Laflamme distribution (SLD) of quantum states is governed by the $k$-body correlations amongst the different systems, and has been used to study entanglement and error correction. A succinct description of a quantum state's SLD can be obtained by representing it through the coefficients of an appropriate weight enumerator polynomial, yielding bounds on fidelity under depolarizing noise and on multipartite entanglement. However, such expressions quickly grow out of hand and are generally difficult to achieve analytically, reflecting the computational hardness of the SLD. We sidestep this problem and, instead of a single state's SLDs, encode a family of quantum state's SLD as a generating function. We then find closed-form expressions for a large class of graph states which we call `recursively definable' and which include many common graphs such as path graphs, cycle graphs, star graphs, grid graphs, and more. As direct corollary, we obtain analytical expressions for such graph states' concentratable entanglement, bounds on their depolarizing fidelity, and a multipartite entanglement criterion. Our work opens up the use of generating functions and more generally analytic combinatorics to solve problems in quantum information theory.
one_liner: Generating-function methods yield closed-form sector length distributions for broad families of graph states relevant to noise and entanglement analysis.
why_it_matters: Analytic formulas replace hard state-by-state computation, enabling faster benchmarking of multipartite resource states used in quantum hardware and codes.
detailed_summary: The paper encodes the Shor-Laflamme or sector length distribution of whole graph-state families into generating functions rather than attacking each state independently. For recursively definable graphs, including paths, cycles, stars, and grids, it derives closed-form expressions for the relevant weight-enumerator polynomials. These formulas directly produce concentratable entanglement, depolarizing-noise fidelity bounds, and multipartite entanglement criteria, giving an analytic route to characterize structured many-qubit states.
main_result: It derives closed-form generating-function expressions for sector length distributions of large classes of graph states and associated noise and entanglement metrics.
application_angle: The results provide efficient analytic tools for assessing graph-state resources used in measurement-based computing, networking, and error-correction architectures.

title: Rigorous quantum state tomography for distributed quantum computing
abstract: Distributed quantum computing offers a promising approach to scaling quantum devices by networking multiple quantum processors. We present a quantum state tomography protocol tailored for distributed quantum computers that avoids assuming remote entanglement as a primitive resource. The protocol extends projected least-squares (PLS) tomography based on projective 2-designs to systems composed of multiple quantum processors, using only local operations within each processor and classical communication between nodes. Assuming entanglement within each individual quantum processor is trusted, the protocol can be executed using mutually unbiased bases. We derive rigorous, non-asymptotic trace-norm error bounds for the PLS estimator, with explicit exponential dependence on the number of nodes. In addition, we establish certified error bounds for estimating entanglement negativity from the PLS estimator. Numerical simulations for systems of up to seven qubits distributed across several devices validate the theoretical error bounds.
one_liner: A distributed tomography protocol reconstructs networked quantum states using only local operations, classical communication, and trusted intra-node entanglement.
why_it_matters: It gives rigorous certification tools for modular quantum computers without assuming remote entanglement as a built-in primitive.
detailed_summary: This work extends projected least-squares tomography based on projective 2-designs to distributed processors connected only by classical communication. The protocol assumes trusted entanglement within each node and can be implemented with mutually unbiased bases, avoiding the need to posit remote entanglement as a primitive operation. The authors prove non-asymptotic trace-norm error bounds with explicit exponential node dependence and also derive certified bounds for entanglement negativity estimated from the reconstruction. Simulations up to seven qubits across multiple devices support the theory.
main_result: It establishes a practical tomography scheme for distributed quantum processors together with rigorous finite-sample error guarantees for state and negativity estimation.
application_angle: This is directly useful for validating modular processors and quantum-network links as multi-node systems scale beyond monolithic chips.

title: Resist-free shadow deposition using silicon trenches for Josephson junctions in superconducting qubits
abstract: Superconducting qubit fabrication innovations continue to be explored to achieve higher performance. Despite improvements to base layer fabrication and processing, resist-based Josephson junction (JJ) schemes have largely remained unchanged. The polymer mask during deposition causes chemical contamination and limits in situ and ex situ surface preparation, junction materials, and scalability. Here, we demonstrate a resist-free approach to junction fabrication based on etched silicon trenches that is CMOS compatible and easily integrated into existing innovations in qubit base layer fabrication and chemical processing. We fabricate Al-AlOx-Al JJs and qubits using this method, measuring median energy relaxation times up to 184 microseconds. We find minimal contamination at the substrate-metal interface and fluctuations of energy relaxation on a 35 hour timescale that are narrow and normally distributed. The method widens the process window for substrate preparation and new materials platforms.
one_liner: A CMOS-compatible resist-free trench process fabricates Al/AlOx/Al Josephson junctions and superconducting qubits with long coherence.
why_it_matters: It removes polymer-mask contamination from junction deposition, opening cleaner interfaces and broader materials options for better qubit fabrication.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a shadow-deposition scheme that uses etched silicon trenches instead of a resist mask during Josephson-junction fabrication. Using this process, the authors build Al-AlOx-Al junctions and superconducting qubits, report median energy-relaxation times up to 184 microseconds, and observe narrow, normally distributed T1 fluctuations over a 35-hour window. Interface analysis indicates minimal substrate-metal contamination, supporting the claim that the method improves cleanliness while staying compatible with standard CMOS-style processing.
platform_or_system: Superconducting transmons with trench-defined Al/AlOx/Al junctions
main_result: The authors demonstrate resist-free Josephson-junction fabrication for superconducting qubits and achieve median T1 values up to 184 microseconds.
application_angle: The process widens the fabrication window for cleaner interfaces, in situ preparation, and future junction-material integration in scalable superconducting hardware.

title: Optimising Quantum Error Correction Using Morphing Circuits
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) codes are traditionally defined and searched for without specifying the manner in which its syndrome extraction circuits are executed using elementary gates and measurements. We show how morphing circuits introduced in Refs. [1-3] provide a way of optimising syndrome extraction circuits and codes directly in terms of connectivity, choice of two-qubit gate (ISWAP versus CNOT) and number of physical qubits. We discuss morphing circuits in code optimisation among Abelian two-block group algebra (2BGA) codes, handling boundaries for 2D codes, codes with single-shot properties, and improving performance in stability experiments against measurement and reset errors. We show that alternating syndrome extraction circuits - executed with alternating time-reversed rounds - can be viewed as a two-round morphing circuit whose fault-tolerant properties are computationally much easier to examine than non-alternating syndrome extraction circuits. Our methods find new codes and syndrome extraction circuits of practical interest, including Abelian 2BGA morphing circuits with better code parameters and connectivity than existing circuits. [1] Matt McEwen, Dave Bacon, and Craig Gidney. Relaxing hardware requirements for surface code circuits using time-dynamics. Quantum, 7:1172, 2023. [2] Craig Gidney and Cody Jones. New circuits and an open source decoder for the color code, 2023. [3] Mackenzie H. Shaw and Barbara M. Terhal. Lowering connectivity requirements for bivariate bicycle codes using morphing circuits.
one_liner: Morphing circuits co-design syndrome extraction and code structure to lower connectivity costs and improve practical QEC implementations.
why_it_matters: It targets the real hardware bottleneck in QEC by optimizing codes together with executable circuits under native gate and connectivity constraints.
detailed_summary: The work argues that QEC codes should be optimized jointly with their syndrome-extraction circuits rather than in isolation. Using morphing circuits, it searches over implementations constrained by connectivity, native two-qubit gates such as ISWAP versus CNOT, and qubit count, covering Abelian 2BGA codes, boundaries in 2D layouts, and single-shot settings. It also shows that alternating time-reversed extraction rounds can be viewed as two-round morphing circuits with easier fault-tolerance analysis. This yields new circuits and code constructions with improved connectivity and practical stability-experiment performance under measurement and reset errors.
platform_or_system: Fault-tolerant QEC circuits on constrained qubit architectures
main_result: It shows that morphing-circuit optimization can produce new syndrome-extraction circuits and codes with better connectivity-resource tradeoffs than existing implementations.
application_angle: The approach is immediately relevant for compiling QEC to near-term hardware where gate set, layout, and reset/measurement errors dominate overhead.

title: High bandwidth traveling wave electro-optic modulator at 1μm on thin-film lithium tantalate
abstract: We present the first experimental demonstration of a high-bandwidth thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) electro-optic modulator operating at 1 μm, with a Vπ of 2.4 V, and less than 2 dB electro-optic roll-off up to 50 GHz and stable DC bias operation.
one_liner: A thin-film lithium tantalate traveling-wave modulator at 1 um achieves 50 GHz-class bandwidth with low drive voltage and stable bias.
why_it_matters: It extends high-speed integrated electro-optics into the 1 um band used by many quantum emitters and atomic systems, reducing a key packaging and control bottleneck.
detailed_summary: The work experimentally demonstrates a thin-film lithium tantalate traveling-wave electro-optic modulator operating at 1 um, a wavelength region important for quantum optics platforms but less developed than telecom. Reported performance includes Vpi = 2.4 V, less than 2 dB electro-optic roll-off up to 50 GHz, and stable DC bias operation. Together, these metrics indicate a device that combines efficient phase modulation, broad RF bandwidth, and practical operating stability in an integrated platform.
